(e) Any person submitting written comments to the Administrator with respect to an application filed under this section shall send a copy of the comments to the applicant. The person shall certify to the Administrator that he or she has complied with the requirements of this paragraph. The Administrator may notify other persons participating in the proceeding of the comments and provide an opportunity for those other persons to respond.

[[57 FR 44132](https://www.federalregister.gov/citation/57-FR-44132), Sept. 24, 1992, as amended at [80 FR 59075](https://www.federalregister.gov/citation/80-FR-59075), Oct. 1, 2015] 

#### § 397.217 Waiver processing.

(a) The Administrator may initiate an investigation of any statement in an application and utilize any relevant facts obtained by that investigation. The Administrator may solicit and accept submissions from third persons relevant to an application and will provide the applicant an opportunity to respond to all third person su

t and accept submissions from third persons relevant to an application and will provide the applicant an opportunity to respond to all third person submissions. In evaluating an application, the Administrator may convene a hearing or conference, if a hearing or conference will advance the evaluation of the application.

(b) The Administrator may dismiss the application without prejudice if:

(1) he or she determines that there is insufficient information upon which to base a determination;

(2) Upon his or her request, additional information is not submitted by the applicant; or

(3) The applicant fails to provide the notice required by this subpart.

(c) Except as provided in this subpart, the Administrator will only consider an application for a waiver of preemption determination if:

(1) The applicant expressly acknowledges in its application that the highway routing designation of the State, political subdivision thereof, or Indian tribe for which the determination is sought is preempted by the Act or the regulations thereunder; or

(2) The highway routing designation of the State, political subdivision thereof, or Indian tribe has been determined by a court of competent jurisdiction or in a determination issued pursuant to [§ 397.211](/current/title-49/section-397.211) to be preempted by the Act or the regulations issued thereunder.

(d) When the Administrator has received all substantive information necessary to process an application for a waiver of preemption determin

ereunder.

(d) When the Administrator has received all substantive information necessary to process an application for a waiver of preemption determination, notice of that fact will be served upon the applicant. Additional notice to all other persons who received notice of the proceeding may be served by publishing a notice in the Federal Register.

#### § 397.219 Waiver determination and order.

(a) Upon consideration of the application and other relevant information received or obtained during the proceeding, the Administrator issues an order setting forth his or her determination.

(b) The Administrator may issue a waiver of preemption order only if he or she finds that the requirement of the State, political subdivision thereof, or Indian tribe affords the public a level of safety at least equal to that afforded by the requirements of the Act and the regulations issued under the Act and does not unreasonably burden commerce. In determining whether the requirement of the State, political subdivision thereof, or Indian tribe unreasonably burdens commerce, the Administrator may consider the following factors:

(1) The extent to which increased costs and impairment of efficiency result from the highway routing designation of the State, political subdivision thereof, or Indian tribe;

(2) Whether the highway routing designation of the State, political subdivision thereof, or Indian tribe has a rational basis;

(3) Whether the highway routing designation of the State, political

f the State, political subdivision thereof, or Indian tribe has a rational basis;

(3) Whether the highway routing designation of the State, political subdivision thereof, or Indian tribe achieves its stated purpose; and

(4) Whether there is need for uniformity with regard to the subject concerned and if so, whether the highway routing designation of the State, political subdivision thereof, or Indian tribe competes or conflicts with those of other States, political subdivisions thereof, or Indian tribes.

(c) The order includes a written statement setting forth the relevant facts and the legal basis for the determination, and provides that any person aggrieved by the order may file a petition for reconsideration in accordance with [§ 397.223](/current/title-49/section-397.223).

(d) The Administrator serves a copy of the order upon the applicant, any other person who participated in the proceeding and upon any other person readily identifiable by the Administrator as one who may be affected by the order. A copy of each order is placed on file in the public docket. The Administrator may publish the order or notice of the order in the Federal Register.

(e) If no petition for reconsideration is filed within 20 days in accordance with [§ 397.223](/current/title-49/section-397.223), an order issued under this section constitutes the final agency decision regarding whether a particular requirement of a State, political subdivision thereof, or Indian tribe is preempted under the

he final agency decision regarding whether a particular requirement of a State, political subdivision thereof, or Indian tribe is preempted under the Act or any regulations issued thereunder, or whether preemption is waived.

#### § 397.221 Timeliness.

If the Administrator fails to take action on the application within 90 days of serving the notice required by [§ 397.217(d)](/current/title-49/section-397.217#p-397.217\(d\)), the applicant may treat the application as having been denied in all respects.

#### § 397.223 Petition for reconsideration.

(a) Any person aggrieved by an order issued under [§ 397.211](/current/title-49/section-397.211) or [§ 397.219](/current/title-49/section-397.219) may file a petition for reconsideration with the Administrator. The petition must be filed within 20 days of service of the determination or order issued under the above sections.

(b) The petition must contain a concise statement of the basis for seeking reconsideration, including any specific factual or legal errors, or material information not previously available.

(c) The petitioner shall mail a copy of the petition to each person who participated, either as an applicant or routing, in the waiver of preemption proceeding, accompanied by a statement that the person may submit comments concerning the petition to the Administrator within 20 days. The petition filed with the Administrator must contain a certification that the petitioner has complied with this paragraph and include the

20 days. The petition filed with the Administrator must contain a certification that the petitioner has complied with this paragraph and include the names and addresses of all persons to whom a copy of the petition was sent.

(d) The Administrator's decision under this section constitutes the final agency decision. If no petition for reconsideration is filed under this section, then the determination issued under [§ 397.211](/current/title-49/section-397.211) or [§ 397.219](/current/title-49/section-397.219) becomes the final agency decision at the end of the 20 day period.

#### § 397.225 Judicial review.

A party to a proceeding under [§ 397.205(a)](/current/title-49/section-397.205#p-397.205\(a\)), [§ 397.213(a)](/current/title-49/section-397.213#p-397.213\(a\)), or [§ 397.223(a)](/current/title-49/section-397.223#p-397.223\(a\)) may seek review by the appropriate district court of the United States of the decision of the Administrator under such proceeding only by filing a petition with such court within 60 days after the final agency decision.

# Hazardous Materials Regulations Introduction

## Learning Objectives
- Identify the federal agencies that regulate hazardous materials transportation
- Explain the structure of 49 CFR Parts 171-180
- Define key terms: hazmat employee, hazmat employer, hazmat transportation
- Describe the four categories of hazmat employees and their training requirements
- Understand penalties for non-compliance

## Section 1: Why Hazmat Regulations Exist

Every day in the United States, more than 800,000 shipments of hazardous materials move by highway, rail, air, and water. That is fuel for gas stations, chemicals for water treatment plants, medical gases for hospitals, and thousands of other products that keep the economy running. When these materials are handled correctly, they arrive safely. When they are not, people die.

The Hazardous Materials Regulations — known as the HMR — exist because history taught us what happens without them. In 1996, a ValuJet DC-9 crashed into the Florida Everglades after improperly packaged oxygen generators ignited in the cargo hold, killing all 110 people on board. In 2013, a freight train carrying crude oil derailed in Lac-Megantic, Quebec, destroying the downtown co

cargo hold, killing all 110 people on board. In 2013, a freight train carrying crude oil derailed in Lac-Megantic, Quebec, destroying the downtown core and killing 47 people. In 2023, a Norfolk Southern train derailed in East Palestine, Ohio, releasing vinyl chloride and forcing a controlled burn that blanketed the town in toxic smoke. Each of these disasters traces back to failures in how hazardous materials were classified, packaged, documented, or handled.

The HMR is not red tape for the sake of paperwork. It is a system designed to make sure that everyone in the transportation chain — from the shipper who fills a drum to the driver who hauls it to the receiver who unloads it — knows exactly what they are dealing with and how to handle it safely. The rules ensure that if something goes wrong during transport, first responders can quickly identify the material and take the right protective actions.

As a fleet operator or hazmat employee, you are a critical link in this safety chain. Whether you drive a tanker truck, load pallets at a warehouse, fill out shipping papers at a desk, or manage a fleet safety program, the HMR applies to you. Understanding these regulations is not optional — it is a legal requirement, and the penalties for getting it wrong are severe.

## Section 2: The Regulatory Framework

### Who Makes the Rules?

The **Pipeline and Hazardous Materials Safety Administration (PHMSA)** is the agency within the U.S. Department of Transportation (DOT) that writ

es?

The **Pipeline and Hazardous Materials Safety Administration (PHMSA)** is the agency within the U.S. Department of Transportation (DOT) that writes and administers the Hazardous Materials Regulations. PHMSA sets the standards for how hazmat must be classified, packaged, marked, labeled, placarded, and documented for transportation.

However, PHMSA does not act alone on the highway. The **Federal Motor Carrier Safety Administration (FMCSA)** enforces hazmat compliance for highway carriers. When a DOT inspector pulls your truck into a weigh station and checks your shipping papers, placards, and load securement, that inspector is enforcing FMCSA regulations — but the underlying hazmat standards come from PHMSA. State agencies also participate through the Motor Carrier Safety Assistance Program (MCSAP), which means state troopers and inspectors can conduct hazmat inspections and issue violations too.

For fleet operators, the practical takeaway is this: PHMSA writes the hazmat rulebook, and FMCSA (along with state partners) enforces it on the road.

### The Structure of 49 CFR Parts 171-180

The HMR lives in Title 49 of the Code of Federal Regulations, Parts 171 through 180. Here is what each part covers:

- **Part 171 — General Information and Definitions**: This is your starting point. It defines key terms like "hazmat employee," "hazmat employer," "person," and "transport vehicle." It also covers applicability — who the HMR applies to and when.

- **Part 172 — Hazardous M

ee," "hazmat employer," "person," and "transport vehicle." It also covers applicability — who the HMR applies to and when.

- **Part 172 — Hazardous Materials Table, Communications, and Training**: This is the largest and most frequently referenced part. It contains the Hazardous Materials Table (Section 172.101) — the master reference for identifying every regulated material. Part 172 also covers shipping papers, marking, labeling, placarding, emergency response information, and training requirements. If you only learn one part of the HMR deeply, make it Part 172.

- **Part 173 — Shippers — General Requirements for Shipments and Packagings**: This part tells shippers how to prepare hazmat for transportation. It covers classification criteria for each hazard class and specifies which packagings are authorized for which materials.

- **Part 174 — Carriage by Rail**: Requirements specific to railroad carriers.

- **Part 175 — Carriage by Aircraft**: Requirements specific to air carriers. This is why your airline asks if you have any lithium batteries or flammable liquids in your luggage.

- **Part 176 — Carriage by Vessel**: Requirements specific to ocean and inland waterway carriers.

- **Part 177 — Carriage by Public Highway**: This is the part fleet operators and drivers use most. It covers loading and unloading procedures, segregation and separation requirements (which hazmat classes cannot be loaded together), routing, attendance of vehicles, and parking rules.

- **Part 1

regation and separation requirements (which hazmat classes cannot be loaded together), routing, attendance of vehicles, and parking rules.

- **Part 178 — Specifications for Packagings**: Detailed manufacturing and testing specifications for drums, boxes, cylinders, portable tanks, and other hazmat containers.

- **Part 179 — Specifications for Tank Cars**: Manufacturing specs for railroad tank cars.

- **Part 180 — Continuing Qualification and Maintenance of Packagings**: Requirements for retesting, requalifying, and maintaining hazmat packagings and cargo tanks over time. This is where you find the rules for periodic testing of cargo tank motor vehicles.

### How It All Fits Together

Think of the HMR as a relay race. The shipper classifies the material, selects the right packaging (Parts 172, 173, 178), prepares the shipping papers, and applies the correct marks and labels (Part 172). The carrier accepts the shipment, verifies the paperwork, applies placards to the vehicle (Part 172), and follows mode-specific transportation rules (Part 177 for highway). If something goes wrong, the emergency response information on the shipping papers and the ERG guidebook help first responders protect the public.

Every person who touches the shipment along this chain has a specific role, and the HMR spells out what each person must do.

## Section 3: Who Is a Hazmat Employee?

The term "hazmat employee" is defined in 49 CFR 171.8, and it is broader than most people expect. You are a haz

Section 3: Who Is a Hazmat Employee?

The term "hazmat employee" is defined in 49 CFR 171.8, and it is broader than most people expect. You are a hazmat employee if you fall into any of these four categories:

### Category 1: Loaders, Unloaders, and Handlers
Anyone who loads, unloads, or handles hazardous materials during transportation. This includes the forklift operator moving pallets of paint cans in a warehouse, the dock worker strapping down drums of solvent on a flatbed, or the fuel terminal worker connecting hoses to a cargo tank. If you physically move hazmat as part of the transportation process, you are a hazmat employee.

### Category 2: Packaging Manufacturers and Testers
Anyone who manufactures, reconditions, tests, repairs, or modifies hazmat packagings. The shop that requalifies propane cylinders, the drum reconditioning facility, the cargo tank testing company — all of their workers are hazmat employees under the HMR.

### Category 3: Preparers and Classifiers
Anyone who is responsible for preparing hazardous materials for transportation. This includes classifying the material, determining the proper shipping name, completing shipping papers, applying marks and labels to packages, and ensuring the shipment meets all HMR requirements. The shipping clerk who fills out the bill of lading for a chemical shipment is a hazmat employee, even if they never physically touch the material.

### Category 4: Vehicle Operators
Anyone who operates a vehicle used to transpo

a hazmat employee, even if they never physically touch the material.

### Category 4: Vehicle Operators
Anyone who operates a vehicle used to transport hazardous materials. This is the most obvious category for fleet operations — if you drive a truck carrying hazmat, you are a hazmat employee. This applies whether you are hauling a full tanker of gasoline or a mixed load that includes a few drums of cleaning chemicals.

**Real-World Example**: Consider a small fleet that delivers cleaning supplies to commercial buildings. The warehouse worker who loads the truck with cases of bleach (Class 8, Corrosive) and aerosol cans of disinfectant (Class 2.1, Flammable Gas) is a hazmat employee. The office worker who prepares the shipping documents is a hazmat employee. The driver who delivers the products is a hazmat employee. The fleet safety manager who oversees training is a hazmat employee. If any of these people lack proper training, the company is in violation of the HMR.

### The Employer's Responsibility

A "hazmat employer" is any person who uses one or more hazmat employees, or who is self-employed and performs hazmat employee functions. The employer is legally responsible for ensuring every hazmat employee is trained, tested, and certified before performing hazmat functions unsupervised. The employer must also keep records of all training — we will cover those requirements next.

## Section 4: Training Requirements Overview

Training requirements for hazmat employees are fou

ll training — we will cover those requirements next.

## Section 4: Training Requirements Overview

Training requirements for hazmat employees are found in 49 CFR 172.704. These are not suggestions — they are legally binding requirements that apply to every hazmat employer.

### The Five Types of Required Training

**1. General Awareness/Familiarization Training**
Every hazmat employee must understand the basics of the HMR. This includes familiarity with the regulations, the ability to recognize and identify hazardous materials, and a general understanding of hazmat communication requirements (marking, labeling, placarding, shipping papers). This module you are reading right now is part of general awareness training.

**2. Function-Specific Training**
Each hazmat employee must receive detailed training on the specific hazmat functions they perform. A driver needs to know vehicle placarding, shipping paper placement, emergency procedures, and loading/unloading rules. A shipping clerk needs to know how to complete shipping papers, look up materials in the Hazmat Table, and apply correct marks and labels. The training must match the job.

**3. Safety Training**
Employees must be trained on emergency response procedures, personal protective measures, and accident prevention methods appropriate to the hazmat they handle. This includes what to do if a package leaks, how to use the Emergency Response Guidebook (ERG), and how to report hazmat incidents.

**4. Security Awareness Train

s what to do if a package leaks, how to use the Emergency Response Guidebook (ERG), and how to report hazmat incidents.

**4. Security Awareness Training**
Every hazmat employee must receive training on security risks associated with hazmat transportation, methods to recognize and respond to security threats, and how to report suspicious activities. This was added after September 11, 2001 and applies to all hazmat employees.

**5. In-Depth Security Training (when applicable)**
Employees of companies required to have a security plan under 49 CFR 172.800 must receive additional detailed security training. This applies to companies that ship or transport certain high-hazard materials such as explosives, poisonous-by-inhalation gases, and radioactive materials in highway-route-controlled quantities.

### Timing Requirements

- **Initial Training**: A new hazmat employee must complete all required training within **90 days** of their first day performing hazmat functions. During this 90-day window, the employee may perform hazmat duties, but only under the direct supervision of a properly trained and knowledgeable hazmat employee.

- **Recurrent Training**: All hazmat employees must receive recurrent training at least once every **3 years** from the date of their most recent training. However, if regulations change in a way that affects an employee's functions, the employer must provide updated training on those changes regardless of the 3-year cycle.

### Training Records

Employ

cts an employee's functions, the employer must provide updated training on those changes regardless of the 3-year cycle.

### Training Records

Employers must maintain training records for each hazmat employee. These records must include:

- The employee's name
- The most recent training completion date
- A description, copy, or location reference of the training materials used
- The name and address of the person providing the training
- A certification that the employee has been trained and tested

Training records must be retained for as long as the employee performs hazmat functions, plus **90 days** after the employee leaves that position. Records must be made available to DOT inspectors upon request.

**Practical Tip**: Keep training records organized and accessible. During a roadside inspection or facility audit, an inspector may ask to see proof that a specific driver or dock worker has current hazmat training. If you cannot produce the records, you will be cited.

## Section 5: Penalties for Non-Compliance

The consequences of violating the Hazardous Materials Regulations are among the most severe in all of transportation law. The government treats hazmat violations seriously because the potential for public harm is so high.

### Civil Penalties

PHMSA can impose civil penalties of up to **$89,678 per violation per day**. Each day a violation continues counts as a separate offense. A single shipment with multiple deficiencies — say, wrong shipping name, missing emerg

*. Each day a violation continues counts as a separate offense. A single shipment with multiple deficiencies — say, wrong shipping name, missing emergency phone number, and incorrect placard — can result in multiple violations stacked on top of each other. A fleet that routinely fails to train employees or maintain records can face penalties that climb into the hundreds of thousands of dollars quickly.

### Criminal Penalties

Willful violations of the HMR can result in criminal prosecution. Penalties include fines up to **$500,000** and imprisonment for up to **10 years**. If a willful violation results in death or serious bodily injury, the penalties escalate further. "Willful" means you knew the rules and chose not to follow them — or you were so reckless that you should have known.

### Inspection Reality

PHMSA, FMCSA, and state enforcement officers all conduct hazmat inspections. These happen at:

- Roadside weigh stations and inspection sites
- Shipper and carrier facilities (announced and unannounced audits)
- Hazmat incident investigations
- Complaint-driven investigations

### Scenario: A Costly Mistake at the Weigh Station

A driver pulls into a weigh station hauling a mixed load that includes 10 drums of a Class 3 flammable liquid. The inspector asks for the shipping papers. The driver pulls out the bill of lading, but the inspector notices several problems: the emergency response telephone number listed is a general company number that goes to voicemail after 5 P

ut the inspector notices several problems: the emergency response telephone number listed is a general company number that goes to voicemail after 5 PM (it is 7 PM), and the shipper's certification line is unsigned. The inspector walks around the vehicle and sees that the FLAMMABLE 3 placards are faded and unreadable from 50 feet.

Result: The driver is placed out of service. The vehicle cannot move until the violations are corrected. The carrier receives three separate violation notices, each carrying potential penalties. The shipper is also notified because the shipping paper deficiencies originated at their facility. A simple load that should have been routine turns into thousands of dollars in fines, a delayed delivery, and a compliance file that will follow the carrier through future audits.

This scenario plays out at weigh stations and inspection sites across the country every day. The rules exist for a reason, and enforcement agencies have the authority and the willingness to shut down operations that do not comply.

---

## Key Takeaways
- The HMR (49 CFR 171-180) governs all aspects of hazmat transportation
- PHMSA writes the rules; FMCSA enforces them for highway carriers
- Anyone who touches hazmat in the transportation chain is a "hazmat employee"
- Training must be completed within 90 days and renewed every 3 years
- Penalties are severe — both civil fines and criminal prosecution are possible

## Assessment Questions

### Q1
**Type**: multiple_choice
**Question**: Which federal agency is responsible for writing and administering the Hazardous Materials Regulations (HMR)?
**A**: Federal Motor Carrier Safety Administration (FMCSA)
**B**: Pipeline and Hazardous Materials Safety Administration (PHMSA)
**C**: Environmental Protection Agency (EPA)
**D**: Occupational Safety and Health Administration (OSHA)
**Correct Answer**: B
**Explanation**: PHMSA, a division of the U.S. Department of Transportation, writes and administers the HMR (49 CFR Parts 171-180). FMCSA enforces hazmat rules on the highway but does not write them. EPA and OSHA have their own hazmat-related regulations but are not responsible for the HMR.
**CFR Reference**: 49 CFR 171.1

### Q2
**Type**: true_false
**Question**: A shipping clerk who prepares hazmat shipping papers but never physically handles hazardous materials is NOT considered a hazmat employee under the HMR.
**Correct Answer**: False
**Explanation**: Under 49 CFR 171.8, anyone who prepares hazardous materials for transportation — including classifying materials, completing shipping papers, and applying marks and labels — is a hazmat employee, even if they never physically touch the material.
**CFR Reference**: 49 CFR 171.8

**Question**: How often must hazmat employees receive recurrent training?
**A**: Every year
**B**: Every 2

CFR 171.8

### Q3
**Type**: multiple_choice
**Question**: How often must hazmat employees receive recurrent training?
**A**: Every year
**B**: Every 2 years
**C**: Every 3 years
**D**: Every 5 years
**Correct Answer**: C
**Explanation**: Under 49 CFR 172.704(c)(2), hazmat employees must receive recurrent training at least once every 3 years from the date of the most recent training. However, if regulatory changes affect an employee's duties, updated training must be provided sooner.
**CFR Reference**: 49 CFR 172.704(c)(2)

### Q4
**Type**: multiple_choice
**Question**: Which part of 49 CFR contains the Hazardous Materials Table and requirements for shipping papers, marking, labeling, placarding, and training?
**A**: Part 171
**B**: Part 172
**C**: Part 173
**D**: Part 177
**Correct Answer**: B
**Explanation**: Part 172 is the largest and most frequently referenced part of the HMR. It contains the Hazardous Materials Table (172.101) and all hazmat communication requirements: shipping papers (Subpart C), marking (Subpart D), labeling (Subpart E), placarding (Subpart F), and training (Subpart H).
**CFR Reference**: 49 CFR Part 172

### Q5
**Type**: scenario
**Question**: A new driver joins your fleet on March 1. She will be hauling loads that include hazardous materials. By what date must she complete all required hazmat training, and what must happen in the meantime?
**A**: She must complete training before her first trip and cannot perform any hazmat functions until then
**B**

and what must happen in the meantime?
**A**: She must complete training before her first trip and cannot perform any hazmat functions until then
**B**: She must complete training within 90 days (by May 30) and may perform hazmat duties under direct supervision of a trained employee during that period
**C**: She must complete training within 6 months (by September 1) and may work unsupervised during that period
**D**: She must complete training within 30 days (by March 31) and may work unsupervised during that period
**Correct Answer**: B
**Explanation**: Under 49 CFR 172.704(c)(1), a new hazmat employee must complete required training within 90 days of employment or change in job function. During this 90-day window, the employee may perform hazmat duties only under the direct supervision of a properly trained hazmat employee.
**CFR Reference**: 49 CFR 172.704(c)(1)

### Q6
**Type**: multiple_choice
**Question**: What is the maximum civil penalty per violation per day for a hazmat violation under the HMR?
**A**: $10,000
**B**: $25,000
**C**: $89,678
**D**: $500,000
**Correct Answer**: C
**Explanation**: Civil penalties for hazmat violations can reach up to $89,678 per violation per day. The $500,000 figure is the maximum fine for criminal prosecution of willful violations, which can also carry up to 10 years imprisonment.
**CFR Reference**: 49 CFR 171.1(g)

# Hazardous Materials Table

## Learning Objectives
- Explain the purpose of the Hazardous Materials Table and where to find it
- Identify each column in the Hazmat Table and describe what information it provides
- Look up a material in the Hazmat Table and extract the required shipping information
- List and describe the nine DOT hazard classes
- Explain the three packing groups and what they indicate about danger severity
- Recognize common mistakes when using the Hazmat Table

## Section 1: Purpose of the Hazmat Table

The Hazardous Materials Table, found at 49 CFR 172.101, is the single most important reference in the entire HMR. It is the master list of every material regulated as hazardous for transportation purposes. When a shipper needs to determine how to classify, package, mark, label, and document a hazardous material, the Hazmat Table is where they start. When a carrier needs to verify that a shipment was properly prepared, the Hazmat Table is where they look. When an inspector finds a discrepancy, the Hazmat Table is the standard they measure against.

The table contains over 3,000 entries organized in alphabetical order by proper shipping name. Each entry provides all the regulatory information

t.

The table contains over 3,000 entries organized in alphabetical order by proper shipping name. Each entry provides all the regulatory information needed to ship that specific material: its hazard class, identification number, packing group, authorized packagings, quantity limits, and special conditions. Think of it as a detailed instruction manual for every hazardous material that moves in commerce.

For fleet operators, familiarity with the Hazmat Table is essential. Even if you are not the one preparing shipments, you need to understand the table well enough to verify that the shipping papers, marks, labels, and placards on your vehicles match what the table requires. A mismatch between what the table says and what is on the truck is a violation — and potentially a safety hazard.

The Hazmat Table also connects directly to the Emergency Response Guidebook (ERG). The Yellow Pages of the ERG are organized by UN/NA identification number, and the Blue Pages are organized by material name — both of which come straight from the Hazmat Table. Being able to cross-reference between the Hazmat Table and the ERG is a core competency for anyone in hazmat transportation.

## Section 2: Column-by-Column Walkthrough

The Hazmat Table has 10 columns. Understanding what each column tells you is fundamental to using the table correctly.

### Column 1: Symbols

The first column may contain one or more letter symbols that provide special instructions or limitations for that entry. The most

# Column 1: Symbols

The first column may contain one or more letter symbols that provide special instructions or limitations for that entry. The most common symbols you will encounter are:

- **"+" (Plus sign)**: This material must be shipped under the exact proper shipping name and hazard class shown in the table, even if it could technically fit a different entry. The shipper has no flexibility.
- **"A"**: This material is subject to the HMR only when transported by aircraft. If you are hauling it by highway, the HMR may not apply (though other regulations might).
- **"D"**: This proper shipping name is appropriate for domestic transportation only. For international shipments, a different entry with a "UN" identification number must be used instead.
- **"G"**: This is a generic or "not otherwise specified" (n.o.s.) entry. The shipper must add a technical name in parentheses after the proper shipping name on shipping papers. For example, "Flammable liquid, n.o.s. (contains ethanol)."
- **"I"**: This entry is appropriate for international transportation only. A different entry must be used for domestic highway shipments.
- **"W"**: This material is subject to the HMR only when transported by water (vessel). Highway carriers generally will not encounter these entries.

**Practical Tip**: Most entries a highway fleet will encounter have no symbol or a "D" symbol. If you see a "G" symbol, check the shipping papers carefully — the technical name must be included.

### Column 2:

er have no symbol or a "D" symbol. If you see a "G" symbol, check the shipping papers carefully — the technical name must be included.

### Column 2: Proper Shipping Name

This is the official, regulated name that must be used on shipping papers, marks, and labels. It is not a brand name, trade name, or common nickname. For example, the proper shipping name for gasoline is "Gasoline" — not "unleaded regular" or "87 octane." The proper shipping name for bleach is "Hypochlorite solution" — not "Clorox" or "bleach."

Proper shipping names are shown in **regular type** (roman) in the table. Italicized text in Column 2 is not part of the proper shipping name — it is a description or cross-reference to help you find the right entry. For example, you might see "Alcohol — see Ethanol" in italics, directing you to the correct entry.

Some proper shipping names include qualifiers like "flammable," "corrosive," "solution," or "solid" as part of the official name. These qualifiers are part of the required name and must appear on shipping papers.

### Column 3: Hazard Class or Division

This column tells you the hazard class and division number assigned to the material. The nine DOT hazard classes are covered in detail in Section 4 of this module. The hazard class drives the labeling, placarding, and compatibility requirements for the material.

Some materials have a primary hazard class and one or more subsidiary hazard classes. For example, a material might be primarily a Class 3 Flamma

al.

Some materials have a primary hazard class and one or more subsidiary hazard classes. For example, a material might be primarily a Class 3 Flammable Liquid but also have a subsidiary hazard of Class 6.1 Toxic. The subsidiary hazards are listed in Column 6 (Special Provisions) or may require additional labels.

### Column 4: Identification Number

Every hazardous material is assigned a four-digit identification number preceded by either "UN" (United Nations — used internationally) or "NA" (North America — used only in the U.S. and Canada). Examples:

- Gasoline: **UN1203**
- Diesel Fuel: **NA1993**
- Acetone: **UN1090**
- Propane: **UN1075**

This number appears on shipping papers, package marks, placards (for bulk shipments), and in the ERG. It is the universal identifier that allows anyone — a driver, an inspector, a firefighter — to quickly determine what material they are dealing with.

### Column 5: Packing Group

The packing group indicates the degree of danger the material presents during transportation. There are three levels:

- **Packing Group I**: Great danger. These materials are the most hazardous within their class. They require the most robust packaging and the most restrictive handling.
- **Packing Group II**: Medium danger. These materials pose a moderate level of hazard.
- **Packing Group III**: Minor danger. These materials are the least hazardous within their class, though they are still regulated and require proper handling.

Not all hazard classes us

r. These materials are the least hazardous within their class, though they are still regulated and require proper handling.

Not all hazard classes use packing groups. Classes 1 (Explosives), 2 (Gases), 5.2 (Organic Peroxides), 6.2 (Infectious Substances), and 7 (Radioactive) do not use packing groups — they have their own internal classification systems.

### Column 6: Special Provisions

This column lists numbers that correspond to special provisions in 49 CFR 172.102. These provisions modify the standard requirements for a particular material. For example, a special provision might exempt certain concentrations of a material from the HMR, require a specific packaging type, or mandate additional labeling. Always check the special provisions — they can significantly change what is required for a shipment.

### Columns 7 (Label Codes)

This column specifies which hazard labels must be applied to packages of the material. Label codes correspond to specific diamond-shaped labels (e.g., "3" requires a FLAMMABLE LIQUID label, "8" requires a CORROSIVE label). If multiple codes are listed, multiple labels are required.

### Columns 8A, 8B, 8C: Packaging Authorizations

These three sub-columns reference the specific sections of 49 CFR Part 173 that authorize packagings for the material:

- **8A — Exceptions**: References exceptions from packaging requirements (less restrictive rules for certain quantities or configurations).
- **8B — Non-bulk**: References the authorized non-bulk pa

m packaging requirements (less restrictive rules for certain quantities or configurations).
- **8B — Non-bulk**: References the authorized non-bulk packagings (drums, boxes, cans — generally under 119 gallons for liquids or 882 pounds for solids).
- **8C — Bulk**: References the authorized bulk packagings (cargo tanks, portable tanks, intermediate bulk containers — over the non-bulk thresholds).

### Columns 9A, 9B: Quantity Limitations

- **9A — Passenger Aircraft/Rail**: The maximum quantity of the material allowed on a passenger-carrying aircraft or railcar. "Forbidden" means the material cannot travel on passenger conveyances at all.
- **9B — Cargo Aircraft Only**: The maximum quantity allowed on cargo-only aircraft.

These columns are primarily relevant for air transportation but can come into play when a fleet handles intermodal shipments.

### Columns 10A, 10B: Vessel Stowage

- **10A — Location**: Specifies stowage categories for ocean vessels (e.g., "A" for on deck or below deck, "B" for on deck only).
- **10B — Other**: Lists additional stowage codes with specific requirements.

These columns apply to water transportation and are generally not a concern for highway-only fleet operations.

## Section 3: How to Look Up a Material

Using the Hazmat Table effectively requires a systematic approach. Here is a step-by-step example using gasoline, one of the most commonly transported hazardous materials.

### Step 1: Find the Entry

Open 49 CFR 172.101 (or use an electroni

xample using gasoline, one of the most commonly transported hazardous materials.

### Step 1: Find the Entry

Open 49 CFR 172.101 (or use an electronic version) and look up "Gasoline" alphabetically. You will find:

| Symbol | Proper Shipping Name | Hazard Class | ID Number | Packing Group |
|--------|---------------------|-------------|-----------|---------------|
| (none) | Gasoline | 3 | UN1203 | II |

### Step 2: Read the Hazard Class

Gasoline is **Class 3 — Flammable Liquid**. This means it has a flash point below 141 degrees Fahrenheit (60 degrees Celsius). This classification determines the labels on packages and the placards on vehicles.

### Step 3: Note the ID Number

**UN1203**. This number will appear on the shipping papers, on package marks, and on the orange panel of bulk placards. If a first responder arrives at a gasoline spill on the highway, they will look for UN1203 on the placard, then turn to Guide 128 in the ERG for response instructions.

### Step 4: Check the Packing Group

**Packing Group II — Medium danger**. This tells you gasoline requires mid-level packaging integrity. It is not the most volatile flammable liquid (that would be PG I), but it is more dangerous than PG III materials like diesel fuel.

### Step 5: Review Special Provisions

Check Column 6 for any special provision numbers, then look them up in 49 CFR 172.102 to see if any modify the standard requirements.

### Step 6: Identify Packaging Authorizations

Check Columns 8A, 8B, and 8C f

em up in 49 CFR 172.102 to see if any modify the standard requirements.

### Step 6: Identify Packaging Authorizations

Check Columns 8A, 8B, and 8C for the specific Part 173 sections that authorize packaging. For gasoline, non-bulk packaging authorizations reference 49 CFR 173.202, and bulk packaging authorizations reference 49 CFR 173.242.

### Another Example: Acetone

Look up "Acetone" and you find:

| Symbol | Proper Shipping Name | Hazard Class | ID Number | Packing Group |
|--------|---------------------|-------------|-----------|---------------|
| (none) | Acetone | 3 | UN1090 | II |

Acetone is also a Class 3 Flammable Liquid with Packing Group II, just like gasoline. But their specific packaging authorizations and special provisions may differ, so you must always check the full table entry for the specific material you are shipping — never assume two materials with the same class and packing group have identical requirements.

## Section 4: The Nine Hazard Classes

The DOT classifies hazardous materials into nine hazard classes. Every hazmat employee should be able to name these classes and recognize examples of materials in each one.

### Class 1: Explosives
Materials that can detonate or explode. Divided into six divisions based on blast hazard, projection hazard, and fire hazard.
- **Division 1.1**: Mass explosion hazard (dynamite, TNT)
- **Division 1.2**: Projection hazard (military munitions)
- **Division 1.3**: Fire hazard with minor blast (propellant, rocket

azard (dynamite, TNT)
- **Division 1.2**: Projection hazard (military munitions)
- **Division 1.3**: Fire hazard with minor blast (propellant, rocket motors)
- **Division 1.4**: Minor explosion hazard (small arms ammunition, fireworks)
- **Division 1.5**: Very insensitive explosives (blasting agents)
- **Division 1.6**: Extremely insensitive detonating articles

**Fleet Relevance**: Ammunition shipments, commercial fireworks, and blasting agents for construction and mining operations.

### Class 2: Gases
Materials that are gaseous at 68 degrees Fahrenheit (20 degrees Celsius) and 14.7 psi (standard atmospheric pressure). Three divisions:
- **Division 2.1 — Flammable Gas**: Burns in air (propane, butane, acetylene, hydrogen)
- **Division 2.2 — Non-Flammable, Non-Toxic Gas**: Compressed or liquefied gases that are not flammable or toxic (nitrogen, carbon dioxide, helium, compressed air)
- **Division 2.3 — Toxic Gas**: Poisonous by inhalation (chlorine, ammonia, hydrogen sulfide)

**Fleet Relevance**: Propane deliveries, welding gas cylinders, medical oxygen, refrigerant cylinders.

### Class 3: Flammable Liquids
Liquids with a flash point of 141 degrees Fahrenheit (60 degrees Celsius) or below. This is one of the most commonly transported hazard classes.
- Examples: Gasoline (UN1203), diesel fuel (NA1993), acetone (UN1090), ethanol (UN1170), paint (UN1263), adhesives (UN1133)

**Fleet Relevance**: Fuel hauling, chemical distribution, paint and coatings delivery, cleaning produc

l (UN1170), paint (UN1263), adhesives (UN1133)

**Fleet Relevance**: Fuel hauling, chemical distribution, paint and coatings delivery, cleaning product distribution.

### Class 4: Flammable Solids
Three divisions of solid materials with fire hazards:
- **Division 4.1 — Flammable Solid**: Burns readily from friction or self-sustained combustion (matches, magnesium)
- **Division 4.2 — Spontaneously Combustible**: Ignites on contact with air (white phosphorus, certain metal powders)
- **Division 4.3 — Dangerous When Wet**: Reacts with water to produce flammable or toxic gas (sodium, calcium carbide, lithium)

**Fleet Relevance**: Metal powder shipments, match shipments, certain chemical raw materials.

### Class 5: Oxidizers and Organic Peroxides
- **Division 5.1 — Oxidizer**: Supplies oxygen to support combustion of other materials (ammonium nitrate, hydrogen peroxide, calcium hypochlorite)
- **Division 5.2 — Organic Peroxide**: Unstable compounds that can decompose explosively or burn rapidly (benzoyl peroxide, methyl ethyl ketone peroxide)

**Fleet Relevance**: Pool chemical deliveries (calcium hypochlorite), fertilizer shipments (ammonium nitrate), industrial chemical distribution.

### Class 6: Toxic and Infectious Substances
- **Division 6.1 — Toxic (Poison)**: Materials that can cause death or serious injury through ingestion, inhalation, or skin absorption (pesticides, cyanide, arsenic compounds)
- **Division 6.2 — Infectious Substance**: Materials that contain pathogens

n, inhalation, or skin absorption (pesticides, cyanide, arsenic compounds)
- **Division 6.2 — Infectious Substance**: Materials that contain pathogens (medical waste, diagnostic specimens, biological cultures)

**Fleet Relevance**: Agricultural pesticide deliveries, medical waste transportation, pharmaceutical distribution.

### Class 7: Radioactive Materials
Materials that emit ionizing radiation above specified thresholds. Classified by transport index and surface radiation levels.
- Examples: Medical isotopes, industrial radiography sources, uranium ore, smoke detectors (exempt quantities)

**Fleet Relevance**: Medical isotope deliveries, construction density gauge transport.

### Class 8: Corrosive Materials
Materials that cause visible destruction or irreversible damage to skin tissue, or that corrode steel or aluminum at a specified rate.
- Examples: Sulfuric acid (UN1830), hydrochloric acid (UN1789), sodium hydroxide solution (UN1824), battery acid, bleach (hypochlorite solution)

**Fleet Relevance**: Chemical distribution, battery deliveries, cleaning product distribution, water treatment chemical deliveries.

### Class 9: Miscellaneous Hazardous Materials
Materials that present a hazard during transportation but do not fit into Classes 1-8. This is a catch-all class.
- Examples: Lithium batteries (UN3481, UN3091), dry ice/solid carbon dioxide (UN1845), environmentally hazardous substances, magnetized materials, elevated temperature materials

**Fleet Relevance**: Ele

ce/solid carbon dioxide (UN1845), environmentally hazardous substances, magnetized materials, elevated temperature materials

**Fleet Relevance**: Electronics shipments with lithium batteries, dry ice shipments for food or medical products, asphalt tankers (elevated temperature).

## Section 5: Packing Groups Explained

Packing groups tell you how dangerous a material is relative to others in the same hazard class. They drive packaging requirements — a PG I material must go in a stronger, more rigorously tested container than a PG III material.

### Packing Group I — Great Danger
These are the most hazardous materials within their class. For flammable liquids (Class 3), PG I materials have an initial boiling point of 95 degrees Fahrenheit (35 degrees Celsius) or below — meaning they vaporize very easily and create an explosive atmosphere fast.
- **Example**: Diethyl ether (UN1155, Class 3, PG I) — extremely volatile, flash point of -49 degrees Fahrenheit. A single spilled drum can create a vapor cloud that reaches an ignition source dozens of feet away.

### Packing Group II — Medium Danger
Mid-range hazard. For flammable liquids, PG II materials have a flash point below 73 degrees Fahrenheit (23 degrees Celsius) and an initial boiling point above 95 degrees Fahrenheit.
- **Example**: Gasoline (UN1203, Class 3, PG II) — flammable and volatile, but not as extreme as PG I materials.

### Packing Group III — Minor Danger
The lowest hazard level within a class, though these mater

e and volatile, but not as extreme as PG I materials.

### Packing Group III — Minor Danger
The lowest hazard level within a class, though these materials are still regulated for a reason. For flammable liquids, PG III materials have a flash point between 73 degrees Fahrenheit and 141 degrees Fahrenheit (23-60 degrees Celsius).
- **Example**: Diesel fuel (NA1993, Class 3, PG III) — combustible, but significantly harder to ignite than gasoline. It requires sustained heat to reach its flash point.

### Why Packing Groups Matter for Fleet Operations

Packing groups affect more than just the container the material ships in. They influence:

- **Packaging strength and testing requirements**: PG I containers must pass more rigorous drop tests and pressure tests.
- **Quantity limitations**: Some modes of transport restrict PG I materials to smaller quantities.
- **Placarding thresholds**: Certain placarding rules apply differently based on packing group (Table 2 vs. Table 1 materials under 49 CFR 172.504).
- **Segregation rules**: PG I materials may have additional separation requirements during transport.

A driver accepting a shipment should note the packing group on the shipping papers. A PG I flammable liquid demands more caution than a PG III flammable liquid — not because the regulations say to drive more carefully, but because the material itself is more dangerous if something goes wrong.

## Section 6: Common Mistakes

Misusing the Hazmat Table leads to violations, failed in

he material itself is more dangerous if something goes wrong.

## Section 6: Common Mistakes

Misusing the Hazmat Table leads to violations, failed inspections, and unsafe shipments. Here are the errors that fleet operators, shippers, and drivers make most often.

### Mistake 1: Using the Wrong Proper Shipping Name
Trade names, abbreviations, and common names are not acceptable on shipping papers. "Gas" is not a proper shipping name. "MEK" is not a proper shipping name. The material must be listed by its exact proper shipping name as it appears in Column 2 of the Hazmat Table. If a customer calls it "thinning solvent," you still need to look it up and use the correct proper shipping name.

### Mistake 2: Missing or Wrong Hazard Class
Every hazardous material has a specific hazard class. Using the wrong class means wrong labels, wrong placards, and wrong emergency response instructions. A material classified as Class 8 (Corrosive) that is actually Class 6.1 (Toxic) will get the wrong treatment in an emergency.

### Mistake 3: Ignoring Special Provisions
Special provisions can exempt certain forms or concentrations of a material from the HMR entirely, or they can add requirements beyond the standard rules. Skipping Column 6 means missing critical information. For example, Special Provision 148 exempts certain concentrations of hydrogen peroxide from the HMR. If you do not check, you might be packaging and documenting a non-regulated material as hazmat — wasting time and money —

rogen peroxide from the HMR. If you do not check, you might be packaging and documenting a non-regulated material as hazmat — wasting time and money — or worse, treating a regulated material as non-hazmat.

### Mistake 4: Ignoring the "G" Symbol
When a "G" appears in Column 1, the proper shipping name is generic (like "Corrosive liquid, n.o.s."). The shipper must add the technical name of the hazardous constituent in parentheses. Failing to include the technical name is a violation and deprives emergency responders of information they need.

### Mistake 5: Confusing NA and UN Numbers
"NA" numbers are valid only in the United States and Canada. For international shipments, a "UN" number must be used. Shipping a material internationally with an NA number is a violation that can cause rejection at borders or ports.

### Mistake 6: Assuming Two Materials with the Same Class Are Identical
Gasoline and acetone are both Class 3, PG II flammable liquids. But their packaging authorizations, special provisions, and emergency response procedures differ. Always look up the specific material — do not copy the information from a different entry with the same class and packing group.

---

## Key Takeaways
- The Hazmat Table at 49 CFR 172.101 is the master reference for all regulated hazardous materials
- Each entry provides the proper shipping name, hazard class, ID number, packing group, packaging rules, and quantity limits
- Always use the exact proper shipping name from the table — no t

g name, hazard class, ID number, packing group, packaging rules, and quantity limits
- Always use the exact proper shipping name from the table — no trade names or abbreviations
- The nine hazard classes cover explosives, gases, flammable liquids, flammable solids, oxidizers, toxics, radioactive, corrosives, and miscellaneous
- Packing Groups I, II, and III indicate great, medium, and minor danger within a class
- Always check special provisions — they can change what is required for a specific material

**A**: Fuel, Class 3, UN1203
**B**: Gasoline, Class 3, UN1203
**C**: Gasoline, Class 2.1, UN1075
**D**: Petroleum Product, Class 3, NA1993
**Correct Answer**: B
**Explanation**: The Hazmat Table lists gasoline under the proper shipping name "Gasoline," hazard class 3 (Flammable Liquid), with identification number UN1203. "Fuel" and "Petroleum Product" are not proper shipping names. UN1075 is propane (a gas, not a liquid). NA1993 is the ID number for diesel fuel, not gasoline.

# Shipping Papers

## Learning Objectives
- Explain the purpose of hazmat shipping papers and why they are critical for safety
- List all required information elements on a hazmat shipping paper
- Describe the correct format and sequence for hazmat descriptions
- Explain where shipping papers must be kept in the cab at all times
- Identify the requirements for emergency response telephone numbers
- Understand the shipper's certification and when it is required
- Describe shipping paper retention requirements for shippers and carriers
- Recognize common shipping paper violations

## Section 1: What Are Shipping Papers?

Shipping papers are the written documents that must accompany every shipment of hazardous materials during transportation. They serve two critical purposes: regulatory compliance and emergency response.

For regulatory compliance, shipping papers provide a verifiable record that the hazardous material was properly classified, described, and packaged before being offered for transport. They connect the shipper, the carrier, and the consignee in a documented chain of accountability. If an inspector pulls your truck over and asks what you are hauling, the shipping papers are the official answer.

For emergenc

ain of accountability. If an inspector pulls your truck over and asks what you are hauling, the shipping papers are the official answer.

For emergency response, shipping papers may be the first — and sometimes the only — source of information available to firefighters, hazmat teams, and law enforcement when they arrive at an accident scene involving hazmat. If a driver is incapacitated after a rollover and cannot communicate, responders will look for the shipping papers to identify what materials are involved, how dangerous they are, and whom to call for expert guidance. The information on those papers can determine whether responders approach the scene in standard gear or full chemical suits, whether they evacuate a quarter-mile or a half-mile, and whether they use water or a specialized agent to address a spill or fire.

This is why the location of shipping papers in the cab is strictly regulated. When the driver is at the controls of the vehicle, the shipping papers must be **within the driver's reach while the seat belt is fastened** — typically in a door pocket, a pouch mounted on the door, or clipped to the visor. When the driver is away from the vehicle, the shipping papers must be placed **on the driver's seat** or in a holder mounted on the inside of the driver's door, where they are visible and immediately accessible to anyone who opens the door.

**Why this matters in real life**: A state trooper arrives at a jackknifed tractor-trailer on the highway at 2 AM. Ther

to anyone who opens the door.

**Why this matters in real life**: A state trooper arrives at a jackknifed tractor-trailer on the highway at 2 AM. There is liquid leaking from the trailer. The driver was taken to the hospital by ambulance. The trooper opens the driver's door and needs to find the shipping papers instantly — not rummage through a messy cab, not dig through a briefcase, not search behind the seats. If the papers are on the driver's seat as required, the trooper can identify the material, call CHEMTREC, and direct the hazmat response team within minutes. If the papers cannot be found, the entire scene becomes exponentially more dangerous and response time increases dramatically.

## Section 2: Required Information

Every hazmat shipping paper must include the following information for each hazardous material in the shipment. Missing any of these elements is a violation.

### The Basic Description (The "Big Four")

The basic description of a hazardous material must include these four elements in a specific sequence:

1. **UN/NA Identification Number**: The four-digit ID number preceded by "UN" or "NA" (e.g., UN1203 for gasoline).

2. **Proper Shipping Name**: The exact name from the Hazardous Materials Table, Column 2 (e.g., "Gasoline"). No trade names, abbreviations, or slang.

3. **Hazard Class or Division Number**: The numeric hazard class from the Hazmat Table, Column 3 (e.g., "3" for flammable liquid).

4. **Packing Group**: Shown in Roman numerals — I, II, o

: The numeric hazard class from the Hazmat Table, Column 3 (e.g., "3" for flammable liquid).

4. **Packing Group**: Shown in Roman numerals — I, II, or III (e.g., "II" for gasoline). Not all materials have packing groups.

The required sequence for these four elements is: **ID number, proper shipping name, hazard class, packing group**. For example:

> UN1203, Gasoline, 3, PG II

### Additional Required Information

Beyond the basic description, the following must also appear on the shipping paper:

5. **Total Quantity**: The total amount of hazardous material, by weight or volume, including the unit of measurement (e.g., "2,000 gallons" or "500 lbs"). The number and type of packages must also be stated (e.g., "10 drums" or "1 cargo tank").

6. **Number and Type of Packages**: The count and description of packages (e.g., "4 cylinders," "20 pails," "1 portable tank").

7. **Emergency Response Telephone Number**: A telephone number where someone knowledgeable about the specific material can be reached 24 hours a day, 7 days a week. This is covered in detail in Section 4.

8. **Shipper's Certification**: A signed statement certifying that the shipment has been properly classified, described, packaged, marked, labeled, and is in proper condition for transportation. This is covered in Section 5.

### Optional but Common Additional Information

- **Technical name**: Required when the proper shipping name is generic (has a "G" symbol in the Hazmat Table). Must appear in parentheses

formation

- **Technical name**: Required when the proper shipping name is generic (has a "G" symbol in the Hazmat Table). Must appear in parentheses immediately after the proper shipping name.
- **Subsidiary hazard class**: If the material has more than one hazard, subsidiary hazards must be listed in parentheses after the primary hazard class.
- **Reportable Quantity (RQ)**: If the material is listed as a hazardous substance under CERCLA and the quantity in a single package meets or exceeds the reportable quantity, "RQ" must appear before or after the basic description.
- **"HOT"**: Required for elevated temperature materials transported at or above 212 degrees Fahrenheit (100 degrees Celsius) for liquids.
- **"Marine Pollutant"**: Required if the material meets the marine pollutant criteria.

### Putting It All Together: Example Shipping Paper Entry

Here is what a complete hazmat entry looks like on a shipping paper:

> **HM** | **Description** | **Quantity**
> X | UN1203, Gasoline, 3, PG II | 5,000 gallons, 1 cargo tank

With emergency response phone number at the top or bottom of the document:
> Emergency Contact: CHEMTREC 1-800-424-9300, Contract #12345

And a signed shipper's certification at the bottom.

## Section 3: Format and Order Rules

The HMR imposes strict formatting requirements to ensure that hazmat entries are immediately identifiable on a shipping paper — especially when the paper also lists non-hazardous materials.

### Identifying Hazmat Entries

When a

e immediately identifiable on a shipping paper — especially when the paper also lists non-hazardous materials.

### Identifying Hazmat Entries

When a shipping paper lists both hazardous and non-hazardous materials, the hazmat entries must be made clearly distinguishable by one of these three methods:

1. **Listed first**: The hazmat entries appear before any non-hazmat entries on the paper. This is the simplest approach.

2. **Highlighted**: The hazmat entries are highlighted in a contrasting color or with a visible border. The highlighting must be something that would be obvious on a photocopy — do not use a highlighter color that disappears when the paper is copied.

3. **Marked with an "X"**: An "X" is placed in a column designated as "HM" (Hazardous Material) next to each hazmat entry. This column must be clearly labeled so the reader knows what the "X" means.

Many carriers and shippers use a combination of these methods for extra clarity. The goal is simple: anyone looking at the shipping paper should be able to immediately see which entries are hazmat without reading every line.

### Basic Description Sequence

The four elements of the basic description must appear in this order:

1. Identification number
2. Proper shipping name
3. Hazard class or division
4. Packing group

Additional information (total quantity, technical name, subsidiary hazard) may be placed before the basic description, after it, or in separate columns, but the four elements of the basic descripti

al name, subsidiary hazard) may be placed before the basic description, after it, or in separate columns, but the four elements of the basic description must maintain their prescribed sequence.

**Correct**: UN1203, Gasoline, 3, PG II
**Incorrect**: Gasoline, 3, UN1203, PG II (proper shipping name and class cannot precede the ID number)

### Consistency

All hazmat entries on a single shipping paper must use the same format. If you use an HM column with an "X" for one hazmat entry, use it for all of them. If you list hazmat entries first, all hazmat entries must come before all non-hazmat entries — you cannot scatter them throughout the document.

## Section 4: Emergency Response Telephone Number

Under 49 CFR 172.604, every hazmat shipping paper must include a telephone number where someone knowledgeable about the hazardous material being shipped can provide immediate emergency response information 24 hours a day, 7 days a week.

### Requirements for the Phone Number

The phone number must meet all of these criteria:

- **Monitored at all times**: The number must be answered 24/7/365. A number that goes to voicemail after business hours does not comply. An automated system that requires a caller to navigate a menu or leave a message does not comply.

- **Knowledgeable person**: The person answering must be able to provide detailed information about the hazardous material, or must have immediate access to someone who can. "I don't know, our hazmat manager comes in at 8 AM" is

led information about the hazardous material, or must have immediate access to someone who can. "I don't know, our hazmat manager comes in at 8 AM" is not acceptable when a firefighter calls at 3 AM.

- **Specific to the material**: The person must be able to provide information about the specific material in the shipment — not just generic hazmat information.

### CHEMTREC

Many shippers and carriers use **CHEMTREC** (Chemical Transportation Emergency Center) to meet this requirement. CHEMTREC is operated by the American Chemistry Council and provides 24/7 emergency response information for hazardous material incidents.

- **CHEMTREC phone number**: 1-800-424-9300
- CHEMTREC requires a contract or registration. The shipper's CHEMTREC contract number should appear on the shipping paper next to the phone number.

Using CHEMTREC is not required — a shipper can use its own 24/7 number if it meets all the regulatory criteria. But for many small and mid-size shippers, CHEMTREC is the most practical way to comply because maintaining a staffed 24/7 phone line is expensive and operationally challenging.

### Where the Phone Number Appears

The emergency response telephone number can appear:

- Next to each hazmat entry on the shipping paper
- At the top or bottom of the shipping paper in a clearly identified location (acceptable if it applies to all hazmat entries on the paper)
- On a separate document that is attached to and accompanies the shipping paper

The number must be clearly

pplies to all hazmat entries on the paper)
- On a separate document that is attached to and accompanies the shipping paper

The number must be clearly associated with the hazmat shipment so that a first responder or inspector does not have to guess which phone number to call.

### Common Phone Number Violations

- **General company number**: A main office line that is not staffed after hours or on weekends.
- **Cell phone of the shipper's contact**: Acceptable only if that person is truly available 24/7 and knowledgeable about the material. In practice, individual cell phones often go unanswered.
- **No number at all**: A surprisingly common violation, especially on shipments prepared by infrequent hazmat shippers.
- **Disconnected or wrong number**: The number must actually work. Inspectors and auditors do test them.

## Section 5: Shipper's Certification

The shipper's certification is a signed statement on the shipping paper that certifies the hazardous material has been properly prepared for transportation. Under 49 CFR 172.204, the certification must read substantially as follows:

> "This is to certify that the above-named materials are properly classified, described, packaged, marked, and labeled, and are in proper condition for transportation according to the applicable regulations of the Department of Transportation."

Alternatively, the shortened form is acceptable:

> "I hereby declare that the contents of this consignment are fully and accurately described above b

Alternatively, the shortened form is acceptable:

> "I hereby declare that the contents of this consignment are fully and accurately described above by the proper shipping name, and are classified, packaged, marked and labeled/placarded, and are in all respects in proper condition for transport according to applicable international and national governmental regulations."

### Who Signs

The shipper (or their authorized agent) must sign the certification. The signature can be manual (handwritten) or digital, and must include the signer's name and date.

### When It Is Required

The shipper's certification is required on virtually all hazmat shipping papers. There are limited exceptions — for example, a shipper who is also the carrier does not need to certify to themselves, and certain movements of empty hazmat packagings may be exempt.

### Why It Matters

By signing the certification, the shipper is accepting legal responsibility for the accuracy of everything on the shipping paper and the compliance of the physical shipment. If the material is misclassified, the packaging is wrong, or the labels are missing, the person who signed that certification can be held personally liable — not just the company.

**Practical Tip for Drivers**: Before you pull out of a shipper's facility, check that the shipper's certification is signed. An unsigned certification is a violation that will be attributed to both the shipper and the carrier. It takes two seconds to check and can save thousa

n unsigned certification is a violation that will be attributed to both the shipper and the carrier. It takes two seconds to check and can save thousands in penalties.

## Section 6: Hazardous Waste Manifest

When hazardous materials are also regulated as hazardous waste under EPA rules, a Uniform Hazardous Waste Manifest (EPA Form 8700-22) is used in place of — or in addition to — standard shipping papers.

### Key Differences from Standard Shipping Papers

- **Manifest Tracking Number**: Every hazardous waste manifest has a unique 12-digit tracking number issued by the receiving state. This number follows the waste from the point of generation through transportation to its final treatment, storage, or disposal facility.

- **EPA ID Numbers**: The generator (shipper), each transporter, and the designated receiving facility must have EPA identification numbers. These numbers appear on the manifest.

- **Cradle-to-Grave Tracking**: The manifest creates a chain of custody. The generator prepares the manifest, the transporter signs for receipt, and the receiving facility signs upon delivery and sends a copy back to the generator. If the generator does not receive a signed copy from the receiving facility within 35 days, they must file an Exception Report with the EPA.

- **Multiple Copies**: The manifest is a multi-copy form. Each party in the chain retains copies for their records.

### What Drivers Need to Know

If you are hauling hazardous waste, you must have the hazardous w

party in the chain retains copies for their records.

### What Drivers Need to Know

If you are hauling hazardous waste, you must have the hazardous waste manifest in the cab, signed by both the generator and you (the transporter). You must deliver the waste only to the facility designated on the manifest. If you cannot deliver to the designated facility, you must contact the generator for revised instructions — you cannot simply take it to a different facility.

Hazardous waste manifests must be retained by the carrier for at least **3 years** from the date the waste was accepted for transport. This is longer than the standard shipping paper retention period.

## Section 7: Retention Requirements

The HMR requires both shippers and carriers to retain copies of hazmat shipping papers after the shipment is complete.

### Shipper Retention

Under 49 CFR 172.201(e), the shipper must retain a copy of the hazmat shipping paper (or an electronic image of it) for a minimum of **2 years** after the material is accepted by the initial carrier. The retained copy must include the date of acceptance by the carrier.

### Carrier Retention

Under 49 CFR 177.817(f), the carrier must retain a copy of the hazmat shipping paper for a minimum of **1 year** after the material is accepted. The retained copy must include the date of acceptance.

### Hazardous Waste Manifests

As noted above, hazardous waste manifests must be retained for **3 years** from the date the waste was accepted for transpo

Hazardous Waste Manifests

As noted above, hazardous waste manifests must be retained for **3 years** from the date the waste was accepted for transport.

### Why Retention Matters

Retained shipping papers serve as evidence of compliance during audits and investigations. If an incident occurs months after a shipment was made — a contamination event, a latent health effect, a delayed complaint — the shipping papers are the primary record of what was shipped, how it was described, and who was responsible. Without retained records, a carrier or shipper has no defense against allegations of non-compliance.

**Practical Tip**: Set up a systematic filing process — by date, by customer, or by material — so that you can retrieve shipping papers quickly when an inspector or auditor requests them. A box of unsorted papers in a back room does not meet the spirit of the regulation, even if technically the papers exist.

## Section 8: Common Violations

Shipping paper violations are among the most frequently cited hazmat infractions during roadside inspections and facility audits. Here are the mistakes that get companies in trouble.

### Violation 1: Missing Emergency Response Phone Number
This is the single most common shipping paper violation. The phone number is missing entirely, or the number listed goes to voicemail, an automated menu, or a person who has no knowledge of the material. Inspectors routinely call the number to verify it works — and they do it during off-hours to test 2

or a person who has no knowledge of the material. Inspectors routinely call the number to verify it works — and they do it during off-hours to test 24/7 availability.

### Violation 2: Wrong Description Sequence
The basic description must follow the prescribed order: ID number, proper shipping name, hazard class, packing group. Rearranging these elements — even if all the correct information is present — is a violation. This trips up shippers who design their own bill of lading forms with columns in the wrong order.

### Violation 3: Unsigned Shipper's Certification
The shipper's certification must be signed. A printed certification without a signature is incomplete. This is an easy violation to catch and an easy one to prevent — just check before the truck leaves.

### Violation 4: Hazmat Entries Not Distinguished
When a shipping paper lists both hazmat and non-hazmat items, the hazmat entries must be highlighted, listed first, or marked with an "X" in an HM column. If hazmat entries are buried in the middle of a long list of non-hazmat items with no visual distinction, that is a violation.

### Violation 5: Missing or Wrong Information
Common errors include: wrong UN/NA number, wrong hazard class, missing packing group, incorrect proper shipping name (using a trade name), missing quantity, or missing technical name when required for a generic (n.o.s.) entry.

### Violation 6: Papers Not Accessible in the Cab
The shipping papers must be within the driver's reach while belte

red for a generic (n.o.s.) entry.

### Violation 6: Papers Not Accessible in the Cab
The shipping papers must be within the driver's reach while belted or on the driver's seat when the driver is away from the vehicle. Papers stuffed in the glove compartment, behind the seat, or in a backpack on the sleeper berth do not comply.

### Violation 7: Outdated or Retained Papers for Previous Loads
Keeping old shipping papers from previous hazmat loads in the cab creates confusion. If an inspector or first responder finds shipping papers for materials that are no longer on the vehicle, they may respond to the wrong hazard. Remove shipping papers from the cab promptly when a hazmat load is delivered. Filed copies belong in the office, not in the truck.

---

## Key Takeaways
- Shipping papers must accompany every hazmat shipment and be immediately accessible in the cab
- The basic description sequence is: ID number, proper shipping name, hazard class, packing group
- The emergency response phone number must connect to a knowledgeable person 24/7
- The shipper's certification must be signed before the vehicle leaves the facility
- Shippers retain shipping papers for 2 years; carriers retain them for 1 year
- Hazmat entries must be visually distinguished from non-hazmat entries on the shipping paper
- Shipping papers on the driver's seat when the driver is away — within arm's reach when the driver is belted in

### Q1
**Type**: multiple_choice
**Question**: When a driver leaves the cab of a vehicle transporting hazardous materials, where must the shipping papers be placed?
**A**: In the glove compartment
**B**: On the driver's seat or in a holder mounted on the inside of the driver's door
**C**: In the sleeper berth
**D**: In a sealed envelope on the dashboard
**Correct Answer**: B
**Explanation**: Under 49 CFR 177.817(e), when the driver is not at the vehicle controls, the shipping papers must be placed on the driver's seat or in a holder mounted to the inside of the driver's door, where they are visible and immediately accessible to emergency responders or inspectors.

fication number, proper shipping name, hazard class or division, and packing group. Placing these elements out of order is a violation, even if all the required information is present on the document.

The emergency response phone number is listed as CHEMTREC 1-800-424-9300, and the shipper's certification is printed at the bottom but has no signature. Should you accept this shipment?
**A**: Yes — all the required information is present

First, the basic description is out of sequence — it should read "UN1203, Gasoline, 3, PG II" (ID number first, then proper shipping name), not "Gasoline, UN1203, 3, PG II." Second, the shipper's certification must be signed under 49 CFR 172.204. An unsigned certification is incomplete. The driver should request corrections before accepting the shipment. CHEMTREC is a valid emergency response number, so that is not the issue.
