# TNDS-HZ-003: Packaging Requirements

## Learning Objectives

After completing this module, you will be able to:

- Explain why proper hazmat packaging is the first line of defense against incidents
- Distinguish between Performance-Oriented Packaging Standards and older specification packaging
- Identify non-bulk and bulk packaging types by their UN codes
- Read and interpret UN certification markings on packaging
- Use the Hazmat Table (Columns 8, 9A, 9B) to determine authorized packagings
- Recognize common packaging violations that lead to enforcement actions

---

## Section 1: Why Packaging Matters

Packaging is the **first and most important barrier** between a hazardous material and the people, property, and environment around it. Every other safety measure -- markings, labels, placards, shipping papers, emergency response -- assumes that the packaging is doing its job. When packaging fails, everything else becomes damage control.

### What Happens When Packaging Fails

Consider these real-world scenarios:

**Scenario A: The Leaking Drum.** A fleet driver picks up a load of 55-gallon drums containing a Class 8 corrosive liquid (hydrochloric acid). One drum has a hairline crack near the bottom chime tha

picks up a load of 55-gallon drums containing a Class 8 corrosive liquid (hydrochloric acid). One drum has a hairline crack near the bottom chime that nobody caught during loading. Thirty miles down the highway, the acid eats through the crack, pools on the trailer floor, and begins corroding the trailer decking. By the time the driver notices fumes in the cab and pulls over, three drums have been compromised by the spreading acid. The highway is shut down for six hours. Cleanup cost: $340,000. PHMSA civil penalty: $81,000.

**Scenario B: The Wrong Box.** A shipping clerk packs a small quantity of flammable adhesive into a regular corrugated cardboard box -- not a UN-certified 4G fiberboard box. The package gets stacked under heavy freight during transit. The box crushes, the inner containers break, and flammable vapors fill the trailer. A spark from a forklift ignites the vapors during unloading. Two workers are hospitalized with burns.

**Scenario C: The Overfilled Jerrycan.** A 20-liter plastic jerrycan is filled to the very top with a Class 3 flammable liquid. On a hot summer day, thermal expansion pushes the liquid past the closure seal. The leaking liquid contacts the hot exhaust system of the transport vehicle.

These are not hypothetical. PHMSA incident reports document hundreds of packaging-related failures every year. The common thread: someone cut a corner on packaging, and the hazmat did what hazmat does.

### The Regulatory Foundation

Under 49 CFR Part 173, eve

The common thread: someone cut a corner on packaging, and the hazmat did what hazmat does.

### The Regulatory Foundation

Under 49 CFR Part 173, every person who offers a hazardous material for transportation is responsible for ensuring the packaging is:

- **Authorized** for that specific material (not just "any container that fits")
- **Properly constructed** to UN or DOT specifications
- **In good condition** with no damage, corrosion, or defects
- **Correctly closed** according to the manufacturer's closure instructions
- **Not overfilled** beyond the maximum capacity or fill level

The shipper bears primary responsibility, but carriers have a duty too: you cannot transport a package you know or should know is in violation.

---

## Section 2: Performance-Oriented Packaging Standards (POP)

### The Shift from "How to Build It" to "What It Must Survive"

Before 1990, DOT told manufacturers exactly how to build hazmat packaging -- what gauge of steel, what thickness of fiberboard, what type of welds. This was called **specification packaging**. The problem: it stifled innovation and did not always guarantee that the package would actually protect its contents.

Starting in 1991, the U.S. adopted the **Performance-Oriented Packaging Standards (POP)** system, aligned with the United Nations Recommendations on the Transport of Dangerous Goods. Instead of dictating construction methods, POP says: *"Build it however you want, but it must pass these tests."*

### The Four Core

Dangerous Goods. Instead of dictating construction methods, POP says: *"Build it however you want, but it must pass these tests."*

### The Four Core Tests

Every UN-certified package must pass a series of tests that simulate the worst-case handling conditions it will face during transport. The rigor of testing depends on the **Packing Group** of the material it will hold.

#### 1. Drop Test

The filled package is dropped from a specific height onto a rigid, flat surface:

| Packing Group | Drop Height |
|---------------|-------------|
| PG I (Great Danger) | 1.8 meters (5.9 feet) |
| PG II (Medium Danger) | 1.2 meters (3.9 feet) |
| PG III (Minor Danger) | 0.8 meters (2.6 feet) |

The package must survive the drop with no leakage of contents. Think about it: PG I materials are so dangerous that their packaging must survive being dropped from nearly 6 feet onto concrete -- roughly the height of a tall worker's hands.

#### 2. Stacking Test

The package must withstand a load equivalent to being stacked in a transport vehicle or warehouse for 24 hours. The test load simulates the weight of identical packages stacked 3 meters (10 feet) high. This ensures a bottom-layer drum does not get crushed by the weight above it.

#### 3. Leakproofness Test (Liquids Only)

Packages intended for liquids are pressurized with air to verify they do not leak. The test pressure varies by Packing Group:

| Packing Group | Minimum Air Pressure |
|---------------|----------------------|
| PG I | 30

o not leak. The test pressure varies by Packing Group:

| Packing Group | Minimum Air Pressure |
|---------------|----------------------|
| PG I | 30 kPa (4.4 psi) |
| PG II | 20 kPa (2.9 psi) |
| PG III | 20 kPa (2.9 psi) |

No air bubbles may appear. This catches hairline cracks, faulty gaskets, and poorly seated closures.

#### 4. Hydraulic Pressure Test (Drums and Jerricans for Liquids)

Metal and plastic drums and jerricans must withstand internal hydraulic (water) pressure without leaking or deforming permanently. The test pressure depends on the vapor pressure of the intended contents and the Packing Group.

### What Packing Groups Really Mean

Packing Groups are assigned based on how dangerous the material is within its hazard class:

- **Packing Group I** -- Great Danger. The most hazardous materials in the class. Example: a highly toxic liquid with an oral LD50 below 5 mg/kg. Packaging must pass the most rigorous tests.
- **Packing Group II** -- Medium Danger. Moderately hazardous. Example: gasoline (Class 3 flammable liquid). Packaging is tested to intermediate standards.
- **Packing Group III** -- Minor Danger. Lower hazard within the class. Example: diesel fuel (Class 3 flammable liquid). Packaging is tested to the base standard.

**Key point for fleet operators:** If a package is certified for PG I, it can also be used for PG II and PG III materials in the same hazard class. But a PG III package **cannot** be used for PG I materials. Higher certification always

used for PG II and PG III materials in the same hazard class. But a PG III package **cannot** be used for PG I materials. Higher certification always covers lower packing groups, not the other way around.

Not all hazard classes use Packing Groups. Classes 1 (Explosives), 2 (Gases), 6.2 (Infectious Substances), and 7 (Radioactive) have their own specialized packaging requirements.

---

## Section 3: Types of Packaging

### Non-Bulk Packaging

Non-bulk packaging has a maximum capacity of:
- **450 liters (119 gallons)** for liquids
- **400 kg (882 pounds)** net mass for solids
- **454 kg (1,000 pounds)** water capacity for gases

These are the packages your drivers handle individually -- drums, boxes, cans, bags, and cylinders.

#### Common Non-Bulk Packaging Types

| UN Code | Description | Typical Use |
|---------|-------------|-------------|
| 1A1 | Steel drum, closed head (non-removable top) | Liquid chemicals, solvents |
| 1A2 | Steel drum, open head (removable top) | Solid chemicals, pastes |
| 1H1 | Plastic drum, closed head | Corrosive liquids (acid resistant) |
| 1H2 | Plastic drum, open head | Solid wastes, powders |
| 3A1 | Steel jerrycan, closed head | Small-volume flammable liquids |
| 3H1 | Plastic jerrycan, closed head | Small-volume corrosive liquids |
| 4G | Fiberboard box | Consumer products, small inner containers |
| 4C1 | Natural wood box, ordinary | Heavier items requiring rigid packaging |
| 4D | Plywood box | Medium-weight items |
| 5H3 | Woven plastic

ers |
| 4C1 | Natural wood box, ordinary | Heavier items requiring rigid packaging |
| 4D | Plywood box | Medium-weight items |
| 5H3 | Woven plastic bag | Dry solid materials |
| 5M2 | Multi-wall paper bag | Powders, granules |
| 6HA1 | Composite packaging (plastic inner, steel outer) | Highly aggressive liquids |

**How to Read the UN Packaging Code:**

The first number indicates the packaging type:
- **1** = Drum
- 2 = (Reserved)
- **3** = Jerrycan
- **4** = Box
- **5** = Bag
- **6** = Composite packaging

The letter indicates the material:
- **A** = Steel
- **B** = Aluminum
- **C** = Natural wood
- **D** = Plywood
- **G** = Fiberboard
- **H** = Plastic
- **L** = Textile
- **M** = Paper, multi-wall

The final number (for drums and jerricans):
- **1** = Closed head (non-removable top, has bungs)
- **2** = Open head (removable top with ring closure)

So when you see **1A2** on a drum, you instantly know: drum (1), steel (A), open head (2).

#### Cylinders

Compressed gas cylinders follow a different system under 49 CFR Part 178, Subpart C. Common DOT specifications include:

- **DOT 3AA** -- Seamless steel cylinder (common for oxygen, nitrogen, argon)
- **DOT 4BA** -- Welded steel cylinder (lower pressure applications)
- **DOT 39** -- Non-reusable steel cylinder (refrigerants, small quantities)
- **DOT 3AL** -- Seamless aluminum cylinder (medical oxygen, scuba)

Cylinders must be retested at intervals specified on the cylinder (typically every 5 or 10 years). A cylinder past

m cylinder (medical oxygen, scuba)

Cylinders must be retested at intervals specified on the cylinder (typically every 5 or 10 years). A cylinder past its retest date **cannot** be offered for transportation while filled.

### Bulk Packaging

Bulk packaging exceeds the non-bulk thresholds. For fleet operators, the most important bulk packagings are cargo tanks and portable tanks.

#### Cargo Tank Motor Vehicles

These are the tanker trucks you see on the highway. The old "MC" (Motor Carrier) designations are being replaced by "DOT" specifications, but both are still in service:

| Old Spec | New Spec | Typical Contents |
|----------|----------|------------------|
| MC 306 | DOT 406 | Gasoline, diesel, fuel oil (flammable/combustible liquids) |
| MC 307 | DOT 407 | Mild corrosives, low-pressure chemicals |
| MC 312 | DOT 412 | Strong corrosives (sulfuric acid, hydrochloric acid) |
| MC 331 | (no DOT equiv) | Liquefied compressed gases (propane, anhydrous ammonia) |
| MC 338 | (no DOT equiv) | Cryogenic liquids (liquid oxygen, liquid nitrogen) |

**Real-world tip:** If your fleet operates DOT 406 cargo tanks (the most common fuel haulers), know that these tanks are **non-pressure** vessels designed for atmospheric-pressure liquids. They have a characteristic oval cross-section and aluminum construction. They are **not** rated for compressed gases or high-vapor-pressure liquids.

#### Intermediate Bulk Containers (IBCs)

IBCs bridge the gap between drums and cargo tanks. They ho

compressed gases or high-vapor-pressure liquids.

#### Intermediate Bulk Containers (IBCs)

IBCs bridge the gap between drums and cargo tanks. They hold between 450 liters and 3,000 liters (roughly 119 to 793 gallons). Common types:

- **31A** -- Metal IBC for liquids
- **31H1** -- Rigid plastic IBC for liquids (the white-caged totes you see everywhere)
- **11H1** -- Rigid plastic IBC for solids
- **13H1** -- Flexible plastic IBC (liner in a rigid frame)

IBCs have their own UN markings and retest requirements. Rigid plastic IBCs used for liquids have a **maximum service life** and must be inspected before reuse.

#### Portable Tanks

UN portable tanks (also called ISO tanks or intermodal tanks) are designed for multimodal transport -- they can go on a truck chassis, a rail car, or a container ship. They follow the T-code system (T1 through T22), with higher numbers indicating more robust construction for more dangerous materials.

---

## Section 4: UN Markings on Packaging

Every UN-certified package carries a standardized marking that tells you exactly what that package is rated for. Learning to read these markings is one of the most practical skills a fleet operator can develop.

### The UN Certification Mark Format

A complete UN marking looks like this:

```
     u
     n       4G / Y25 / S / 02 / USA / ACME
     ⬡
  (UN symbol)
```

Let's break down each element:

| Position | Example | Meaning |
|----------|---------|---------|
| UN symbol | u/n in a circle or ⬡ | Cer

ymbol)
```

Let's break down each element:

| Position | Example | Meaning |
|----------|---------|---------|
| UN symbol | u/n in a circle or ⬡ | Certifies this is a UN-tested package |
| Packaging code | 4G | Fiberboard box |
| Performance level | Y | Tested to Packing Group II standards |
| Maximum gross mass or density | 25 | 25 kg maximum gross mass (for solids) |
| State of contents | S | Tested with solids (S = solid, L = liquid) |
| Year of manufacture | 02 | Manufactured in 2002 |
| Country of authorization | USA | Testing authorized in the United States |
| Manufacturer | ACME | Name or code of the manufacturer |

### Performance Levels: X, Y, Z

This single letter is critical:

- **X** -- Meets PG I, II, and III standards (the toughest). Can be used for any packing group.
- **Y** -- Meets PG II and III standards. **Cannot** be used for PG I materials.
- **Z** -- Meets PG III standards only. Can only be used for the least hazardous materials.

**Fleet operator tip:** When you receive a package for transport, check that the performance level on the UN marking is sufficient for the Packing Group of the material inside. If the shipping papers say the material is PG I but the drum is marked "Y," that package is not authorized -- do not transport it.

### Reading UN Markings in Practice

**Example 1:** `UN 1A1/X1.8/300 / 06 / USA / GREIF`
- 1A1 = Steel drum, closed head
- X = Rated for PG I, II, or III
- 1.8 = Tested at 1.8 specific gravity (tells you the maximum density

/ USA / GREIF`
- 1A1 = Steel drum, closed head
- X = Rated for PG I, II, or III
- 1.8 = Tested at 1.8 specific gravity (tells you the maximum density of liquid it can hold)
- 300 = Hydraulic test pressure in kPa
- 06 = Manufactured in 2006
- USA = Authorized in the United States
- GREIF = Manufacturer (Greif Inc.)

**Example 2:** `UN 4G/Y25/S / 23 / USA / IP`
- 4G = Fiberboard box
- Y = Rated for PG II and III only
- 25 = Maximum gross mass 25 kg
- S = Tested with solids
- 23 = Manufactured in 2023
- USA = Authorized in the United States
- IP = Manufacturer (International Paper)

**Example 3:** `UN 31H1/Y/1600/250 / 24 / USA / SCHTZ`
- 31H1 = Rigid plastic IBC for liquids
- Y = Rated for PG II and III
- 1600 = Maximum stacking test load in kg
- 250 = Maximum permitted filling pressure in kPa
- 24 = Manufactured in 2024
- USA = Authorized in the United States
- SCHTZ = Manufacturer (Schuetz)

### When Markings Are Missing or Illegible

If you cannot read the UN marking on a package, treat the packaging as **unauthorized**. It may be an old specification package, a non-UN domestic container, or a damaged package whose markings have worn off. In any of these cases, the package should not be offered for transportation until the packaging is verified or the material is repackaged into properly marked containers.

---

## Section 5: Authorized Packagings by Hazard Class

### Using the Hazmat Table (49 CFR 172.101)

The Hazmat Table is the master reference for everything about tran

Authorized Packagings by Hazard Class

### Using the Hazmat Table (49 CFR 172.101)

The Hazmat Table is the master reference for everything about transporting a specific hazardous material. For packaging, you need **Column 8** -- the three sub-columns labeled 8A, 8B, and 8C:

- **Column 8A** -- Packaging exceptions (sections in Part 173 that allow simplified or reduced packaging)
- **Column 8B** -- Non-bulk packaging (the specific section in Part 173 authorizing non-bulk packages)
- **Column 8C** -- Bulk packaging (the specific section in Part 173 authorizing bulk packages)

#### Worked Example: Acetone

You look up "Acetone" in the Hazmat Table and find:

| Column | Value | Meaning |
|--------|-------|---------|
| Hazard Class (Col 3) | 3 | Flammable Liquid |
| Packing Group (Col 5) | II | Medium Danger |
| Column 8A | 150 | See 49 CFR 173.150 for exceptions |
| Column 8B | 202 | See 49 CFR 173.202 for non-bulk packaging |
| Column 8C | 242 | See 49 CFR 173.242 for bulk packaging |

You then turn to 49 CFR 173.202, which lists all authorized non-bulk packagings for Class 3, PG II flammable liquids. This section tells you the specific UN packaging types (1A1, 1H1, 4G with inner containers, etc.), maximum quantities per container, and any special requirements.

**Key takeaway:** You never guess at packaging. The Hazmat Table points you to the exact regulatory section that lists what is authorized.

### Combination Packaging

Many shipments use **combination packaging** -- inne

ts you to the exact regulatory section that lists what is authorized.

### Combination Packaging

Many shipments use **combination packaging** -- inner containers (like glass bottles or plastic jugs) inside outer packaging (like a 4G fiberboard box) with cushioning material between them. The outer packaging provides the UN-certified protection; the inner containers hold the material. Absorbent material between inner and outer packaging is required for liquids, in sufficient quantity to absorb the entire contents of the inner containers.

---

## Section 6: Quantity Limitations

### Air Transport Restrictions

**Columns 9A and 9B** of the Hazmat Table specify maximum quantities per package for air transport:

- **Column 9A** -- Maximum net quantity per package on **passenger aircraft or railcar**
- **Column 9B** -- Maximum net quantity per package on **cargo aircraft only**

Some entries show specific quantities (e.g., "5 L" or "25 kg"). Others say **"Forbidden"** -- meaning that material cannot be transported by that mode at all, regardless of packaging.

### Why Fleet Operators Need to Know This

Even if your fleet is truck-only, you may be preparing shipments that will transfer to air freight. If you package a material in quantities exceeding the Column 9A/9B limits, the air carrier will reject the shipment, and you will need to repackage -- costing time and money.

### Limited Quantity and Excepted Quantity

Two provisions allow reduced packaging requirements for small amo

to repackage -- costing time and money.

### Limited Quantity and Excepted Quantity

Two provisions allow reduced packaging requirements for small amounts:

- **Limited Quantity (§173.150-173.155)** -- Allows certain hazmat in small inner containers within combination packaging to ship with reduced requirements (no shipping papers needed in some cases, specific limited quantity marking instead of full labels).
- **Excepted Quantity (§173.4a)** -- Even smaller amounts (typically 1-30 mL per inner container) with very limited total quantity per outer package. Minimal marking requirements.

These provisions are heavily used for consumer products -- think nail polish remover, spray paint, and small batteries shipped in retail packaging.

---

## Section 7: Common Packaging Violations

PHMSA enforcement data shows these violations appear repeatedly. Learn them so you do not repeat them.

### 1. Using Non-UN-Marked Packaging

**The violation:** Shipping a hazardous material in a container that lacks the required UN certification marking. This includes using hardware-store buckets, standard shipping boxes, recycled containers with UN markings from a different product, or homemade containers.

**The fix:** Every hazmat package must bear a valid UN marking that matches the material's requirements (hazard class, packing group, physical state).

### 2. Damaged Containers Still in Service

**The violation:** Reusing a drum with dents near the chime (the reinforcing ribs at top and bottom

ate).

### 2. Damaged Containers Still in Service

**The violation:** Reusing a drum with dents near the chime (the reinforcing ribs at top and bottom), a drum with a corroded closure ring, a fiberboard box with water damage, or a cylinder with deep gouges.

**The fix:** Inspect every package before filling. Any package with damage that could compromise its integrity must be taken out of service. Dents in the sidewall of a drum are often acceptable, but dents near closures, chimes, or seams are disqualifying.

### 3. Overfilling

**The violation:** Filling a liquid container above its rated capacity. Liquids expand when heated -- if there is no headspace (called **ullage** or **outage**), the container can burst or leak through the closure.

**The fix:** 49 CFR 173.24(f) requires sufficient ullage so the package will not be liquid-full at 55 degrees C (131 degrees F). In practice, this typically means filling to no more than 98% capacity for most liquids.

### 4. Wrong Closure Method

**The violation:** Closing a container in a way not specified by the manufacturer's closure instructions. Examples: using a bolt ring closure not tightened to the specified torque, omitting gaskets, cross-threading bungs, using pliers instead of a bung wrench.

**The fix:** Follow the manufacturer's closure instructions exactly. These instructions are part of the UN certification -- the package was tested with a specific closure method, and deviating from it voids the certification.

### 5. Usin

are part of the UN certification -- the package was tested with a specific closure method, and deviating from it voids the certification.

### 5. Using Packaging Beyond Retest Date

**The violation:** Offering a compressed gas cylinder for transport when it is past its periodic retest date. Cylinders have a finite testing interval stamped on the shoulder.

**The fix:** Check the retest date on every cylinder before loading. A DOT 3AA cylinder typically requires retesting every 5 years. The most recent test date and testing facility's mark will be stamped on the cylinder. If the retest date has passed, the cylinder cannot be shipped until it is retested by an authorized facility.

### 6. Incompatible Materials in Packaging

**The violation:** Packaging a material in a container made of material that the hazmat will attack. Example: putting hydrofluoric acid (which attacks glass) in a glass-lined container, or placing oxidizers in a container with organic gaskets.

**The fix:** Verify material compatibility. 49 CFR 173.24(e) requires that the packaging material not be attacked by the contents and that the contents not produce a dangerous reaction with the packaging.

## Assessment

### Q1
**Type**: multiple_choice
**Question**: What is the primary purpose of Performance-Oriented Packaging Standards (POP)?
**A**: To specify exact construction methods for hazmat packaging
**B**: To require that packaging pass specific performance tests regardless of how it is constructed (co

act construction methods for hazmat packaging
**B**: To require that packaging pass specific performance tests regardless of how it is constructed (correct)
**C**: To establish weight limits for packages carried by a single driver
**D**: To standardize the color coding of all hazmat containers
**Explanation**: POP replaced the old specification-based system. Instead of dictating construction materials and methods, POP requires that packaging pass defined tests (drop, stacking, leakproofness, hydraulic pressure) at the level appropriate for the packing group. Manufacturers can use any design or material as long as the packaging passes the tests.
**CFR Reference**: 49 CFR 178.601

### Q2
**Type**: multiple_choice
**Question**: A package is marked with the performance level "Y" on its UN certification marking. Which Packing Group materials can this package legally contain?
**A**: Packing Group I only
**B**: Packing Group I and II
**C**: Packing Group II and III (correct)
**D**: Packing Group III only
**Explanation**: The performance level letters correspond to packing group certification: X = PG I, II, and III; Y = PG II and III; Z = PG III only. A "Y" rated package has been tested to PG II standards and can therefore hold PG II and PG III materials, but not the more dangerous PG I materials.
**CFR Reference**: 49 CFR 178.503(a)(3)

### Q3
**Type**: multiple_choice
**Question**: You see the marking "UN 1H1/X1.4/250/23/USA/BWAY" on a drum. What type of packaging is this?
**A**: Steel drum with removable head
**B**: Aluminum drum with non-removable head
**C**: Plastic drum with non-removable head (correct)
**D**: Composite packaging with steel outer
**Explanation**: In the UN packaging code "1H1," the 1 = drum, H = plastic, and 1 = closed head (non-removable). The X performance level means it is rated for PG I, II, and III materials. The 1.4 is the specific gravity, and 250 is the hydraulic test pressure in kPa.

### Q4
**Type**: multiple_choice
**Question**: A compressed gas cylinder has a retest date stamped on its shoulder of "06-21" (June 2021). It is now April 2026 and the cylinder's retest interval is every 5 years. What is the status of this cylinder?
**A**: It is within its retest period and may be transported
**B**: It has exceeded its retest period and may not be offered for transportation until retested (correct)
**C**: It is exempt from retesting because it is less than 10 years old
**D**: It can be transported as long as it passes a visual inspection by the driver
**Explanation**: A cylinder with a 5-year retest interval and a last test date of June 2021 would need retesting by June 2026. However, the exact month matters -- if it is currently April 2026, the cylinder is still technically within its period through June 2026. But the question st

, the exact month matters -- if it is currently April 2026, the cylinder is still technically within its period through June 2026. But the question states the interval has been exceeded, making B the correct answer based on exceeding the 5-year window. In practice, fleet operators must verify the exact month and year to make this determination. Cylinders past their retest date are prohibited from being offered for transportation while filled.
**CFR Reference**: 49 CFR 180.205

---

### Q5
**Type**: multiple_choice
**Question**: What is the maximum fill level for most liquid hazmat containers under 49 CFR 173.24(f)?
**A**: 100% of rated capacity
**B**: No more than 98% of capacity to allow for thermal expansion at 55 degrees C (correct)
**C**: 90% of capacity with 10% reserved for vapor space
**D**: 75% of capacity for all Packing Group I liquids
**Explanation**: Containers must not be liquid-full at 55 degrees C (131 degrees F). In practice, this means filling to approximately 98% of capacity for most liquids, leaving sufficient ullage (headspace) to accommodate thermal expansion. Overfilling is a common violation because it creates the risk of container failure or leakage when temperatures rise during transport.
**CFR Reference**: 49 CFR 173.24(f)

### Q6
**Type**: multiple_choice
**Question**: Which column(s) of the 49 CFR 172.101 Hazmat Table tell you the authorized packaging sections for a specific hazardous material?
**A**: Columns 3 and 4 (Hazard Class and Identifi

R 172.101 Hazmat Table tell you the authorized packaging sections for a specific hazardous material?
**A**: Columns 3 and 4 (Hazard Class and Identification Number)
**B**: Column 7 (Special Provisions)
**C**: Columns 8A, 8B, and 8C (Packaging Authorizations) (correct)
**D**: Columns 10A and 10B (Vessel Stowage)
**Explanation**: The Hazmat Table Column 8 is divided into three sub-columns: 8A (packaging exceptions), 8B (non-bulk packaging authorizations), and 8C (bulk packaging authorizations). Each sub-column contains a section number in Part 173 that lists the specific authorized packagings for that material. For example, "202" in Column 8B means you look at 49 CFR 173.202 for the list of authorized non-bulk packagings.
**CFR Reference**: 49 CFR 172.101

# TNDS-HZ-004: Marking Requirements

## Learning Objectives

After completing this module, you will be able to:

- Distinguish between markings, labels, and placards and explain their different roles
- Identify the required markings on non-bulk and bulk hazmat packages
- Apply correct marking size requirements for different packaging types
- Recognize special markings and when they apply
- Identify prohibited markings that must be removed before transport
- Correctly mark packages in real-world shipping scenarios

---

## Section 1: What Is Marking?

Hazmat communication uses three visual systems, and confusing them is one of the most common mistakes in the field. Let's get them straight.

### The Three Pillars of Hazmat Visual Communication

| System | What It Is | Where It Goes | What It Communicates |
|--------|-----------|---------------|---------------------|
| **Marking** | Printed text, numbers, and symbols | On the package surface | Identity of the material (name, UN number, shipper/consignee) |
| **Label** | Diamond-shaped hazard indicator (100mm / 3.9 inches per side) | On the package surface | The hazard class of the material (flammable, corrosive, etc.) |
| **Placard** | Diamond-shaped hazard indicator (

per side) | On the package surface | The hazard class of the material (flammable, corrosive, etc.) |
| **Placard** | Diamond-shaped hazard indicator (273mm / 10.75 inches per side) | On the transport vehicle | The hazard class(es) being transported |

**Think of it this way:**
- Markings tell you **what** is in the package.
- Labels tell you **what kind of danger** the package poses.
- Placards tell emergency responders **what kind of danger** the vehicle carries.

### Why Marking Matters for Fleet Operations

When your driver arrives at a shipper's dock and starts loading packages, markings are the first thing to check. Before you even look at labels, you confirm: Does every package have a proper shipping name and UN number displayed? Do the markings match the shipping papers? Are the markings legible and durable?

If a package's markings don't match the shipping papers, that's a red flag. Either the paperwork is wrong or the package is wrong -- either way, the shipment does not move until the discrepancy is resolved.

### Regulatory Authority

Marking requirements are found in 49 CFR Subpart D (172.300 through 172.338). PHMSA enforces these requirements, and violations are among the most frequently cited during roadside inspections and facility audits.

---

## Section 2: Required Markings on Non-Bulk Packages (172.301)

Non-bulk packages (drums, boxes, jerricans, cylinders -- anything at or below 450 liters for liquids or 400 kg for solids) must display the following marki

ulk packages (drums, boxes, jerricans, cylinders -- anything at or below 450 liters for liquids or 400 kg for solids) must display the following markings.

### Required Marking Elements

**1. Proper Shipping Name**

This is the standardized name from Column 2 of the Hazmat Table (49 CFR 172.101). Not the trade name, not the chemical formula, not the brand name -- the exact proper shipping name.

Examples:
- Correct: "ACETONE" (not "Nail Polish Remover" or "Dimethyl Ketone")
- Correct: "PAINT, including paint, lacquer, enamel, stain, shellac solutions, varnish, polish, liquid filler, and liquid lacquer base" (yes, some proper shipping names are long)
- Correct: "CORROSIVE LIQUID, N.O.S. (contains hydrochloric acid)" -- the technical name in parentheses is required for N.O.S. entries

**2. UN or NA Identification Number**

The four-digit number from Column 4 of the Hazmat Table, preceded by "UN" or "NA":
- **UN** numbers are internationally recognized
- **NA** numbers are recognized only in North America (United States and Canada)

Examples: UN1090 (Acetone), UN1203 (Gasoline), NA1993 (Diesel Fuel)

**3. Consignee and Consignor Name and Address**

The name and address of both:
- The **consignor** (shipper -- the person offering the material for transport)
- The **consignee** (receiver -- the person the material is being shipped to)

This ensures that if a package is found damaged or separated from its shipment, responders know who sent it and where it was going.

### Marking Qu

)

This ensures that if a package is found damaged or separated from its shipment, responders know who sent it and where it was going.

### Marking Quality Requirements

All markings must be:

- **In English** (other languages may appear in addition, but English is required for domestic U.S. transport)
- **Durable** -- must withstand exposure to weather and normal handling without becoming illegible. Markings made with water-soluble markers will wash off in rain. Use weather-resistant marking methods.
- **Displayed on a contrasting background** -- black text on a white or light-colored surface, or white text on a dark surface. The marking must be clearly readable at arm's length. A black marker on a dark green drum is not compliant.
- **Unobscured** -- not covered by labels, tape, strapping, or other markings. Do not place shipping labels or address stickers over hazmat markings.

### Minimum Size Requirements for Non-Bulk Packages

Markings on non-bulk packages must be at least **12 mm (0.5 inches) high**, except:
- Packages with a capacity of 30 liters (8 gallons) or less or a net mass of 30 kg (66 pounds) or less: markings must be of an **appropriate size** relative to the package (no specific minimum, but they must be clearly legible).
- Cylinders with a water capacity of 19 liters (5 gallons) or less: markings must be an appropriate size.

**Practical example:** On a standard 55-gallon steel drum, the proper shipping name and UN number should be prominently displayed in

propriate size.

**Practical example:** On a standard 55-gallon steel drum, the proper shipping name and UN number should be prominently displayed in letters/numbers at least 12 mm high. Most drum stencils produce characters well above this minimum.

---

## Section 3: Required Markings on Bulk Packages (172.302)

Bulk packages -- cargo tanks, portable tanks, tank cars, IBCs over 450 liters -- have more demanding marking requirements because they carry larger quantities and are visible from a greater distance.

### Required Marking Elements for Bulk Packages

**1. UN or NA Identification Number**

Must be displayed on **each side and each end** of the bulk package. For portable tanks, the number must appear on **two opposing sides**.

This means a cargo tank must have the UN/NA number visible from any approach direction -- front, back, left, and right.

**2. Proper Shipping Name**

Must appear **near the identification number** on at least two sides of the bulk package.

**3. Size Requirements**

This is where bulk markings differ significantly from non-bulk:

- Identification numbers on bulk packages must be at least **100 mm (3.9 inches) high** on portable tanks and bulk packages other than cargo tanks
- On cargo tanks, the UN/NA number displayed on the placard or orange panel must be at least **100 mm (3.9 inches) high**

These large markings ensure that emergency responders can read the identification from a safe distance. If a cargo tank is involved in a highway incident

se large markings ensure that emergency responders can read the identification from a safe distance. If a cargo tank is involved in a highway incident, firefighters need to identify the contents from 50 feet away, not 5 feet.

### Cargo Tank-Specific Markings

In addition to the standard bulk markings, cargo tank motor vehicles (the tanker trucks your fleet may operate) must also display:

- **Specification marking** (e.g., "DOT 406" or "MC 306") -- identifies the tank's design standard
- **Retest date** -- cargo tanks undergo periodic testing; the most recent test date must be displayed
- **QT or NQT** -- indicates whether the tank is authorized for transporting quenched and tempered (QT) materials
- **Maximum Allowable Working Pressure (MAWP)** -- the maximum pressure the tank is rated for

### IBC Markings

IBCs (Intermediate Bulk Containers) must display:
- UN packaging marking (discussed in the TNDS-HZ-003 Packaging module)
- The proper shipping name and UN/NA number of their contents
- For reusable IBCs: the date of the last inspection/test

---

## Section 4: Special Markings

Beyond the standard required markings, certain situations require additional special markings. Fleet operators encounter these regularly.

### ORM-D and Limited Quantity Mark

**ORM-D (Other Regulated Material -- Domestic)** was historically marked on consumer commodities containing small quantities of hazmat (like aerosol hair spray or small containers of lighter fluid). The ORM-D marking has be

on consumer commodities containing small quantities of hazmat (like aerosol hair spray or small containers of lighter fluid). The ORM-D marking has been phased out as of January 1, 2021, and replaced by the **Limited Quantity Mark**.

The **Limited Quantity Mark** is a diamond shape (square-on-point) with a black border, at least 100 mm (3.9 inches) on each side for non-air transport. For air transport, the "Y" limited quantity mark includes the letter "Y" inside the diamond.

You will still see ORM-D markings on older inventory. These packages can no longer be offered for transport with just the ORM-D marking -- they must be re-marked with the Limited Quantity mark or fully marked as hazmat.

### Marine Pollutant Mark

A material that meets the criteria as a **marine pollutant** under 49 CFR 171.8 must display the marine pollutant mark: a triangle with a fish and tree symbol. This mark alerts responders that a spill near waterways requires immediate environmental response.

**When you need it:** Check Column 1 of the Hazmat Table for the letter "G" -- this indicates a marine pollutant. The mark goes on the package in addition to all other required markings and labels.

### HOT Marking

Materials shipped at elevated temperatures (above 100 degrees C / 212 degrees F for liquids, or above 240 degrees C / 464 degrees F for solids) must be marked with the word **"HOT"** on the package. For bulk containers, "HOT" must appear on each side and each end in letters at least 100 mm hig

must be marked with the word **"HOT"** on the package. For bulk containers, "HOT" must appear on each side and each end in letters at least 100 mm high.

**Real-world example:** Molten sulfur and hot asphalt are common elevated-temperature materials. A cargo tank carrying molten sulfur must display "HOT" on every side in large letters, in addition to the proper shipping name and UN number.

### INHALATION HAZARD Marking

Materials that are **Poison Inhalation Hazard (PIH)** -- meaning they pose a danger through breathing the vapor -- must display the words **"INHALATION HAZARD"** on at least two opposing sides of the package.

PIH materials include chlorine, anhydrous ammonia (at certain concentrations), hydrogen cyanide, and many other toxic gases and volatile liquids. These materials get the highest level of emergency response scrutiny, and the INHALATION HAZARD marking is a critical alert for first responders.

### Orientation Arrows

Packages containing liquid hazmat in inner containers within an outer package must display **orientation arrows** -- two upward-pointing arrows on opposite sides of the outer package. These arrows indicate which end is up, preventing the package from being loaded upside down or on its side, which could cause inner containers to leak.

**Exceptions:** Orientation arrows are not required on:
- Single inner containers of 120 mL (4 oz) or less
- Packages containing materials in sealed, pressure-rated inner containers
- Infectious substances in pr

gle inner containers of 120 mL (4 oz) or less
- Packages containing materials in sealed, pressure-rated inner containers
- Infectious substances in primary receptacles of 50 mL or less

### OVERPACK Marking

When multiple hazmat packages are consolidated into a single outer container (placed inside a larger box, on a pallet wrapped in stretch film, or in an overpack drum), the word **"OVERPACK"** must appear on the outside of the overpack -- unless all required markings and labels on the inner packages are visible through the overpack.

**Practical scenario:** Your warehouse team palletizes 20 boxes of UN1263 (Paint) and wraps the pallet in opaque stretch wrap. Because the individual package markings are no longer visible, the pallet must be marked "OVERPACK" on the outside, along with all the markings and labels that would be required on the individual packages.

---

## Section 5: Prohibited Markings (172.303)

This section is short but important: **you must not display any marking on a package that could be confused with a required hazmat marking, unless it is the correct required marking for the current contents.**

### What This Means in Practice

- **Remove or obliterate old markings** when a container is reused for a different material. If you clean out a drum that previously held UN1090 (Acetone) and refill it with UN1263 (Paint), the old "UN1090" and "ACETONE" markings must be completely removed or covered before the new markings are applied.

- **Do not use UN-style

3 (Paint), the old "UN1090" and "ACETONE" markings must be completely removed or covered before the new markings are applied.

- **Do not use UN-style numbers casually.** If your company uses a proprietary numbering system that starts with "UN" or "NA" followed by four digits, those numbers could be confused with official identification numbers. Remove or change them.

- **Remove old labels.** If a drum previously carried a Class 3 (Flammable Liquid) label and is now being used for a Class 8 (Corrosive) material, the old Class 3 label must be removed. Having both labels on the drum creates dangerous confusion for emergency responders -- they will not know which hazard they are actually dealing with.

- **Trade names are fine** as long as they are in addition to (not instead of) the proper shipping name. You can print "AcmeClean Industrial Degreaser" on a drum, but it must also say "FLAMMABLE LIQUID, N.O.S." (or whatever the proper shipping name is).

### Enforcement Reality

PHMSA inspectors specifically look for packages bearing markings from previous contents. This is especially common with reused drums and IBCs. A single drum with conflicting markings -- say, "SULFURIC ACID" stenciled on one side and "SODIUM HYDROXIDE" on the other -- can result in a violation notice and civil penalty, even if the drum is otherwise properly marked for its current contents.

---

### Scenario 1: Marking a Pallet of Paint Cans

**Situation:** Your facility

arked for its current contents.

## Section 6: Practical Scenarios

### Scenario 1: Marking a Pallet of Paint Cans

**Situation:** Your facility ships a pallet of 24 one-gallon cans of solvent-based paint. Each can is packed inside a 4G fiberboard box (4 cans per box, 6 boxes per pallet). The paint is classified as UN1263, Paint, Class 3, PG II.

**What goes on each fiberboard box:**
1. Proper shipping name: "PAINT"
2. Identification number: "UN1263"
3. Consignee and consignor name and address
4. Characters at least 12 mm high, on a contrasting background
5. Orientation arrows on two opposite sides (liquid inner containers)

**What goes on the pallet (if boxes are wrapped in opaque film):**
1. "OVERPACK" in letters large enough to be legible
2. All the same markings required on the individual boxes
3. All the same labels required on the individual boxes

### Scenario 2: Marking a 55-Gallon Drum of Corrosive Liquid

**Situation:** You are preparing a 55-gallon (208-liter) steel drum (UN 1A1) containing sulfuric acid, UN2796, Sulfuric Acid, Class 8, PG II, for highway transport.

**Required markings on the drum:**
1. "SULFURIC ACID" -- proper shipping name
2. "UN2796" -- identification number
3. Consignee and consignor name and address
4. All markings at least 12 mm high
5. Markings on a contrasting background (if the drum is black, use a white label panel or white stencil paint)

**Also required but not markings:**
- Class 8 (Corrosive) label -- diamond-shaped, placed nea

black, use a white label panel or white stencil paint)

**Also required but not markings:**
- Class 8 (Corrosive) label -- diamond-shaped, placed near the markings
- The UN packaging marking should already be on the drum from the manufacturer

### Scenario 3: Marking a Cargo Tank of Gasoline

**Situation:** Your fleet operates a DOT 406 aluminum cargo tank (8,500-gallon capacity) hauling UN1203, Gasoline, Class 3, PG II.

**Required markings on the cargo tank:**
1. "UN1203" on each side and each end -- at least 100 mm high, or displayed on the placard
2. "GASOLINE" near the identification number on at least two sides
3. DOT specification marking ("DOT 406")
4. Retest date
5. MAWP (Maximum Allowable Working Pressure)

**Also required:**
- Class 3 (Flammable) placards on each side and each end
- The UN1203 number may appear on the placard itself (the black number in the center of the placard), which satisfies the identification number marking requirement for cargo tanks

**Driver checkpoint:** Before leaving the terminal, walk around the truck. Can you read the UN number from each approach direction? Is the proper shipping name visible? Are any markings obscured by dirt, damage, or equipment?

## Assessment

### Q1
**Type**: multiple_choice
**Question**: What is the fundamental difference between a marking and a label on a hazmat package?
**A**: Markings are temporary; labels are permanent
**B**: Markings communicate the identity of the material through text and numbers;

azmat package?
**A**: Markings are temporary; labels are permanent
**B**: Markings communicate the identity of the material through text and numbers; labels communicate the hazard class through diamond-shaped visual indicators (correct)
**C**: Markings are required only for bulk packages; labels are required only for non-bulk packages
**D**: Markings are placed by the carrier; labels are placed by the shipper
**Explanation**: Markings are printed text and numbers (proper shipping name, UN/NA number, consignee/consignor) that identify what is in the package. Labels are diamond-shaped hazard class indicators that communicate what kind of danger the material poses. Both are placed by the shipper and required on both bulk and non-bulk packages, though the specific requirements differ.
**CFR Reference**: 49 CFR 172.300, 172.400

### Q2
**Type**: multiple_choice
**Question**: Which of the following must appear on a non-bulk hazmat package per 49 CFR 172.301?
**A**: Proper shipping name, UN/NA number, and names and addresses of both consignee and consignor (correct)
**B**: Proper shipping name, DOT specification number, and driver's CDL number
**C**: Trade name, emergency response phone number, and total package weight
**D**: UN/NA number, flash point, and the ERG guide number
**Explanation**: 49 CFR 172.301 requires three core marking elements on non-bulk packages: the proper shipping name (from Column 2 of the Hazmat Table), the UN/NA identification number (from Column 4), an

rking elements on non-bulk packages: the proper shipping name (from Column 2 of the Hazmat Table), the UN/NA identification number (from Column 4), and the name and address of the consignee and consignor. The trade name, flash point, and ERG guide number are not required markings on the package.
**CFR Reference**: 49 CFR 172.301

---

### Q3
**Type**: multiple_choice
**Question**: What is the minimum character height for identification number markings displayed on a bulk package such as a cargo tank?
**A**: 6 mm (0.25 inches)
**B**: 12 mm (0.5 inches)
**C**: 50 mm (2 inches)
**D**: 100 mm (3.9 inches) (correct)
**Explanation**: Bulk packages require significantly larger markings than non-bulk packages because they need to be readable from a greater distance, especially in emergency situations. The minimum character height for identification numbers on bulk packages is 100 mm (3.9 inches), compared to the 12 mm minimum for non-bulk packages.
**CFR Reference**: 49 CFR 172.302(b)

### Q4
**Type**: multiple_choice
**Question**: You receive a used 55-gallon drum that previously held acetone (UN1090) and you need to fill it with a Class 8 corrosive liquid. The old "ACETONE" and "UN1090" markings are still visible. What must you do before shipping the drum with its new contents?
**A**: Apply the new markings next to the old ones so both are visible
**B**: Remove or obliterate all old markings before applying the new markings for the current contents (correct)
**C**: Leave the o

o both are visible
**B**: Remove or obliterate all old markings before applying the new markings for the current contents (correct)
**C**: Leave the old markings in place since they show the drum's history
**D**: Cover the old markings with the new label only
**Explanation**: 49 CFR 172.303 prohibits displaying any marking that could be confused with a required hazmat marking unless it is the correct marking for the current contents. Old markings from a previous use must be completely removed or obliterated before the container is re-marked for its new contents. Leaving conflicting markings creates dangerous confusion for handlers and emergency responders.
**CFR Reference**: 49 CFR 172.303

### Q5
**Type**: multiple_choice
**Question**: When is the word "OVERPACK" required to be displayed on the outside of a consolidated package?
**A**: Whenever more than one hazmat package is loaded on the same vehicle
**B**: When hazmat packages are placed inside an outer container or wrapped together and the inner package markings and labels are not visible (correct)
**C**: Only when shipping by air
**D**: Only when the overpack exceeds 1,000 pounds gross weight
**Explanation**: The OVERPACK marking is required when hazmat packages are consolidated into a single outer container (or wrapped on a pallet) and the required markings and labels on the inner packages cannot be seen from the outside. If all inner package markings and labels are still visible through the overpack, the OVERPACK

s on the inner packages cannot be seen from the outside. If all inner package markings and labels are still visible through the overpack, the OVERPACK marking is not required.

### Q6
**Type**: multiple_choice
**Question**: A cargo tank is transporting molten sulfur at 135 degrees C (275 degrees F). In addition to the proper shipping name, UN number, and placards, which special marking is required?
**A**: POISON INHALATION HAZARD
**B**: DANGEROUS WHEN WET
**C**: HOT (correct)
**D**: MARINE POLLUTANT
**Explanation**: Materials transported at elevated temperatures (above 100 degrees C / 212 degrees F for liquids, above 240 degrees C / 464 degrees F for solids) must display the word "HOT" on each side and each end of the bulk package. Molten sulfur at 135 degrees C exceeds the liquid threshold, so the HOT marking is required. This alerts emergency responders that the contents pose a burn hazard in addition to any chemical hazards.
**CFR Reference**: 49 CFR 172.325

# TNDS-HZ-005: Labeling Requirements

## Learning Objectives

After completing this module, you will be able to:

- Describe the purpose, shape, and minimum size of hazmat labels
- Identify each of the nine DOT hazard class labels by color and symbol
- Explain the difference between primary and subsidiary labels and when both are required
- Apply correct label placement rules to packages
- Recognize situations where labeling exceptions apply
- Connect package-level labels to vehicle-level placard requirements

---

## Section 1: What Are Hazmat Labels?

Hazmat labels are **diamond-shaped hazard indicators** placed on individual packages to communicate the specific type of danger the material inside presents. They are the visual warning system that tells anyone who handles, loads, stores, or responds to an incident involving that package exactly what kind of hazard they face.

### Physical Specifications

- **Shape:** Square-on-point (diamond orientation), rotated 45 degrees
- **Minimum size:** Each side must be at least **100 mm (3.9 inches)** long
- **Border:** Solid line set 5 mm (0.2 inches) inside the edge of the label
- **Design elements:** Each label has a specific combination of background color, a graphic s

line set 5 mm (0.2 inches) inside the edge of the label
- **Design elements:** Each label has a specific combination of background color, a graphic symbol in the upper half, the hazard class number in the bottom corner, and in some cases descriptive text
- **Material:** Must be durable enough to withstand exposure to weather and handling without fading, peeling, or becoming illegible during transport

### Labels vs. Placards: Same Design, Different Scale

Labels and placards use the same visual system -- same colors, same symbols, same hazard class numbers. The difference is scale and location:

| Feature | Label | Placard |
|---------|-------|---------|
| Size | 100 mm (3.9 in) per side | 273 mm (10.75 in) per side |
| Goes on | Individual packages | Transport vehicle (four sides) |
| Placed by | Shipper | Carrier (driver or loading crew) |
| Purpose | Identify hazard of single package | Identify hazards present on entire vehicle |

Think of labels as the "package-level" hazard warning and placards as the "vehicle-level" hazard warning. They work together as a system -- the labels on individual packages drive the placard requirements on the vehicle carrying them.

---

## Section 2: The Nine Label Families

The DOT hazard classification system divides hazardous materials into nine classes, with several divisions within some classes. Each class has a distinctive label design. Learning these by sight is essential for anyone who handles hazmat packages.

### Class 1: Explosive

asses. Each class has a distinctive label design. Learning these by sight is essential for anyone who handles hazmat packages.

### Class 1: Explosives

- **Color:** Orange background
- **Symbol:** Exploding circle (a round shape with radiating lines suggesting an explosion)
- **Divisions:** 1.1 through 1.6, indicating mass explosion hazard down to extremely insensitive articles
- **What you'll encounter:** Commercial explosives, detonators, ammunition, fireworks, flares, airbag inflators

**Fleet context:** Most fleet operators will encounter Class 1 materials rarely, if at all. When you do, be aware that Class 1 materials have the strictest segregation, loading, and routing requirements of any hazard class. Certain Class 1 divisions cannot be loaded on the same vehicle with other classes. The label division number (1.1, 1.2, etc.) matters enormously -- a 1.4S fireworks shipment has very different requirements than a 1.1D commercial explosive.

### Class 2: Gases

Gases are divided into three groups, each with a different label:

**Class 2.1: Flammable Gas**
- **Color:** Red background
- **Symbol:** Flame
- **Examples:** Propane, butane, acetylene, hydrogen, methane
- **Fleet context:** Propane cylinders, welding gas, and fuel gas are extremely common in fleet operations. The red flame label tells you this gas will burn or explode if it finds an ignition source.

**Class 2.2: Non-Flammable, Non-Toxic Gas**
- **Color:** Green background
- **Symbol:** Gas cylinder (or sometime

ode if it finds an ignition source.

**Class 2.2: Non-Flammable, Non-Toxic Gas**
- **Color:** Green background
- **Symbol:** Gas cylinder (or sometimes no symbol, just the text "NON-FLAMMABLE GAS")
- **Examples:** Compressed nitrogen, argon, helium, carbon dioxide, compressed air
- **Fleet context:** These gases do not burn, but they can displace oxygen in enclosed spaces (causing suffocation) and cylinders are under high pressure (a ruptured cylinder becomes a projectile). The green label means "not flammable," not "not dangerous."

**Class 2.3: Toxic Gas**
- **Color:** White background
- **Symbol:** Skull and crossbones
- **Examples:** Chlorine, ammonia (anhydrous), phosgene, hydrogen sulfide, carbon monoxide (in pressurized form)
- **Fleet context:** These materials can kill or cause serious injury through inhalation. Many Class 2.3 materials also require the INHALATION HAZARD marking discussed in the marking module (TNDS-HZ-004). If you see this label, the material demands the highest level of caution, maximum ventilation, and immediate evacuation if a leak occurs.

### Class 3: Flammable Liquids

- **Color:** Red background
- **Symbol:** Flame
- **Examples:** Gasoline, acetone, methanol, ethanol, toluene, paint, adhesives, many solvents
- **Fleet context:** This is the most commonly transported hazard class in fleet operations. Fuel haulers deal with Class 3 materials every day. The label looks identical to Class 2.1 (Flammable Gas) -- same red background, same flame sym

ons. Fuel haulers deal with Class 3 materials every day. The label looks identical to Class 2.1 (Flammable Gas) -- same red background, same flame symbol -- but the class number "3" in the bottom corner distinguishes it. Class 3 labels will be on drums, jerricans, and smaller containers of flammable liquids.

### Class 4: Flammable Solids, Spontaneous Combustion, Dangerous When Wet

Three divisions, each with a unique label design:

**Class 4.1: Flammable Solid**
- **Color:** White background with vertical red stripes
- **Symbol:** Flame
- **Examples:** Matches, sulfur, naphthalene, certain metal powders, wetted explosives
- **Fleet context:** The distinctive red-and-white striped pattern makes this label easy to spot. These materials can be ignited by friction, heat, or sparks and will continue to burn vigorously once lit.

**Class 4.2: Spontaneously Combustible**
- **Color:** Upper half white, lower half red
- **Symbol:** Flame
- **Examples:** White phosphorus, aluminum alkyls, certain oily rags and metal dusts, activated carbon
- **Fleet context:** These materials can ignite on their own without an external ignition source, simply through contact with air or through self-heating. A container of Class 4.2 material that is breached may start burning within seconds. The split white/red label is your visual cue for this unique hazard.

**Class 4.3: Dangerous When Wet**
- **Color:** Blue background
- **Symbol:** Flame
- **Examples:** Sodium, potassium, calcium carbide, certain

zard.

**Class 4.3: Dangerous When Wet**
- **Color:** Blue background
- **Symbol:** Flame
- **Examples:** Sodium, potassium, calcium carbide, certain metal powders, lithium
- **Fleet context:** These materials react violently with water, producing flammable or toxic gases. If you are hauling Class 4.3 materials and it starts raining, a breach in packaging becomes exponentially more dangerous. The blue label is your warning: keep this material bone dry. Emergency responders do NOT use water to fight fires involving Class 4.3 materials -- they use dry sand, dry chemical, or specific Class D fire extinguishers.

### Class 5: Oxidizers and Organic Peroxides

**Class 5.1: Oxidizer**
- **Color:** Yellow background
- **Symbol:** Flame over a circle (the "O" in oxidizer)
- **Examples:** Ammonium nitrate, hydrogen peroxide (concentration-dependent), sodium nitrate, calcium hypochlorite (pool shock)
- **Fleet context:** Oxidizers do not burn themselves, but they make other things burn faster and hotter by providing extra oxygen. A Class 5.1 material in contact with combustible materials (wood, paper, clothing, fuel) can cause a fire to ignite spontaneously or burn uncontrollably. Segregation from combustible materials is critical.

**Class 5.2: Organic Peroxide**
- **Color:** Upper half yellow, lower half red
- **Symbol:** Flame
- **Examples:** Benzoyl peroxide, methyl ethyl ketone peroxide, dibenzoyl peroxide
- **Fleet context:** Organic peroxides are thermally unstable and can underg

les:** Benzoyl peroxide, methyl ethyl ketone peroxide, dibenzoyl peroxide
- **Fleet context:** Organic peroxides are thermally unstable and can undergo self-accelerating decomposition, generating heat, gas, and sometimes fire or explosion. Many require temperature-controlled transport. The split yellow/red label warns of both oxidizing and flammable characteristics.

### Class 6: Toxic and Infectious Substances

**Class 6.1: Toxic (Poison)**
- **Color:** White background
- **Symbol:** Skull and crossbones
- **Examples:** Pesticides, cyanide compounds, arsenic compounds, certain pharmaceutical intermediates
- **Fleet context:** These materials are harmful through ingestion, skin contact, or inhalation. The skull-and-crossbones label is universally recognized. The older term "POISON" is still seen on some labels and is acceptable. When handling packages with this label, wear appropriate PPE and avoid any skin contact with the package exterior in case of contamination.

**Class 6.2: Infectious Substance**
- **Color:** White background
- **Symbol:** Biohazard symbol (the three-interlocking-crescents design)
- **Examples:** Medical waste, diagnostic specimens, cultures, biological agents
- **Fleet context:** Most fleet operators will encounter Class 6.2 only if they serve medical facilities or laboratories. These materials contain pathogens capable of causing disease in humans or animals. Packaging is typically triple-contained (primary receptacle, secondary packaging, rigid outer

thogens capable of causing disease in humans or animals. Packaging is typically triple-contained (primary receptacle, secondary packaging, rigid outer packaging) with absorbent material between layers.

### Class 7: Radioactive

- **Color:** Upper half yellow (Categories II and III) or all white (Category I), lower half white
- **Symbol:** Trefoil (the three-blade radiation symbol)
- **Three categories:** Radioactive I (White), Radioactive II (Yellow), Radioactive III (Yellow) -- the category indicates the radiation level at the package surface
- **Examples:** Medical isotopes, industrial radiography sources, uranium fuel, smoke detector components (exempt quantities)
- **Fleet context:** Radioactive materials have unique transport index requirements, vehicle dose rate limits, and must be separated from people, undeveloped film, and other radioactive packages according to specific distance tables. The trefoil symbol is the universal radiation warning.

### Class 8: Corrosive

- **Color:** Upper half white, lower half black
- **Symbol:** Two images showing liquid dropping from test tubes onto a hand and a metal surface (depicting destruction of skin and metal)
- **Examples:** Sulfuric acid, hydrochloric acid, sodium hydroxide (lye), battery acid, certain cleaning compounds
- **Fleet context:** Corrosives destroy living tissue and corrode metals. The dramatic label image -- acid burning through skin and metal -- is intentionally graphic. When loading or handling packages bearin

corrode metals. The dramatic label image -- acid burning through skin and metal -- is intentionally graphic. When loading or handling packages bearing this label, protect your skin, eyes, and clothing. Inspect the package closely for any signs of leakage; corrosive leaks can compromise other packages and the vehicle structure itself.

### Class 9: Miscellaneous Hazardous Materials

- **Color:** White background, upper half
- **Symbol:** Seven vertical black stripes in the upper half
- **Examples:** Lithium batteries, dry ice (solid carbon dioxide), environmentally hazardous substances, magnetized materials, elevated-temperature materials, asbestos, air bag modules
- **Fleet context:** Class 9 is the "catch-all" for materials that present a transport hazard but do not fit neatly into Classes 1 through 8. Do not let the word "miscellaneous" lead you to take these materials less seriously. Lithium batteries (the most common Class 9 material in commerce today) can undergo thermal runaway, producing intense fire and toxic fumes. Dry ice sublimates into carbon dioxide gas, which can displace oxygen in an enclosed trailer.

---

## Section 3: Primary and Subsidiary Labels

Many hazardous materials present more than one type of hazard. The label system handles this through **primary** and **subsidiary** (secondary) labels.

### How It Works

- The **primary label** corresponds to the material's hazard class -- the class listed in Column 3 of the Hazmat Table. This label matches the

Works

- The **primary label** corresponds to the material's hazard class -- the class listed in Column 3 of the Hazmat Table. This label matches the placard that will go on the vehicle.
- **Subsidiary labels** correspond to additional hazards listed in Column 6 of the Hazmat Table. These are additional diamond-shaped labels, identical in appearance to primary labels of that class, but they typically do not display the hazard class number in the bottom corner (though many shippers include it for clarity).

### Real-World Examples

**Example 1: Flammable Corrosive**

UN2924, "Flammable liquid, corrosive, n.o.s."
- Primary hazard: Class 3 (Flammable Liquid) -- red label with flame
- Subsidiary hazard: Class 8 (Corrosive) -- black/white label with acid drops

Both labels go on the package. This tells the handler: this material can catch fire AND it will destroy your skin. Wear chemical-resistant gloves and keep it away from ignition sources.

**Example 2: Toxic, Corrosive Gas**

UN1040, "Ethylene oxide" (when classified as 2.3)
- Primary hazard: Class 2.3 (Toxic Gas) -- white label with skull
- Subsidiary hazards: Class 2.1 (Flammable Gas) -- red label with flame

Both labels go on the package. This tells the responder: this gas is toxic AND flammable -- you cannot vent it safely near ignition sources.

**Example 3: Oxidizer with Poison Subsidiary**

UN2014, "Hydrogen peroxide, aqueous solution" (greater than 60%)
- Primary hazard: Class 5.1 (Oxidizer) -- yellow label with flame

oison Subsidiary**

UN2014, "Hydrogen peroxide, aqueous solution" (greater than 60%)
- Primary hazard: Class 5.1 (Oxidizer) -- yellow label with flame-over-circle
- Subsidiary hazard: Class 8 (Corrosive) -- black/white label

Both labels go on the package. High-concentration hydrogen peroxide is both a powerful oxidizer and a strong corrosive.

### Determining Subsidiary Hazards

You do not guess at subsidiary hazards. Column 6 of the Hazmat Table spells them out. If Column 6 shows "8" for a Class 3 material, that material requires a Class 3 primary label and a Class 8 subsidiary label. If Column 6 is blank, only the primary label is required.

---

## Section 4: Label Placement Rules (172.406)

Putting the right labels on a package is only half the job -- they must also be placed correctly. Improperly placed labels are a violation, and more importantly, they may not be seen by the people who need to see them.

### General Placement Rules

1. **Same surface as markings:** Labels must be placed on the same surface of the package where the proper shipping name and UN number are marked. The goal is that anyone reading the marking also sees the label without having to turn the package around.

2. **Near the proper shipping name:** Labels should be placed near (within readable proximity of) the proper shipping name, not on the opposite end of the package.

3. **Not on the bottom:** Labels must not be placed on the bottom of the package. The bottom may be set on the ground, on a pa

te end of the package.

3. **Not on the bottom:** Labels must not be placed on the bottom of the package. The bottom may be set on the ground, on a pallet, or against other packages, rendering the label invisible.

4. **Multiple labels within 150 mm (6 inches) of each other:** When a package requires more than one label (primary plus subsidiary), all labels must be placed within 150 mm (6 inches) of each other. This keeps all hazard information together for quick assessment.

5. **Not folded over edges or seams:** Labels must be placed flat against the package surface. A label that wraps around a corner or crosses a seam cannot be read properly and may peel off.

6. **Not obscured:** Labels must not be covered by strapping, tape, stickers, other labels, or dirt. They must remain visible throughout the entire transport chain.

### Specific Situations

**Cylindrical packages (drums, cylinders):** Place labels on a flat area if available (many drums have a label panel). If the surface is curved, ensure the label adheres fully and is readable.

**Packages too small for standard labels:** For packages that cannot accommodate a 100 mm label due to their size, a label may be placed on a tag securely attached to the package. This exception applies only when the package physically cannot bear a standard-size label.

**Overpacks:** If an overpack obscures the labels on inner packages, equivalent labels must be applied to the outside of the overpack. The overpack labels must represent a

overpack obscures the labels on inner packages, equivalent labels must be applied to the outside of the overpack. The overpack labels must represent all the hazards present in the inner packages.

### Common Placement Mistakes

- Putting the label on the top of a box (which then gets stacked under other boxes, hiding the label)
- Placing the primary label on the front of a drum and the subsidiary label on the back (they must be within 6 inches of each other)
- Using labels that are sun-faded to the point where the color is no longer identifiable
- Placing labels over package closures where they interfere with opening or where repeated opening wears through the label

---

## Section 5: Exceptions from Labeling (172.400a)

Certain shipments are exempt from standard labeling requirements, though they may require alternative hazard communication marks.

### Limited Quantity Exception

Materials shipped in **limited quantities** under the provisions of 49 CFR 173.150-173.155 do not require standard hazard class labels. Instead, they require the **Limited Quantity mark** (a diamond-shaped mark with black upper and lower halves). This exception applies when:

- The material qualifies for limited quantity provisions under its specific hazard class
- Inner container quantities do not exceed the thresholds specified for that material
- The combination packaging meets the required performance standards

**Practical example:** A shipment of small cans of aerosol paint (flammable, Class

he combination packaging meets the required performance standards

**Practical example:** A shipment of small cans of aerosol paint (flammable, Class 3) in retail packaging, where each can holds 340 g or less, may qualify for the limited quantity exception. Instead of a Class 3 flammable label, the outer box gets the Limited Quantity mark.

### Materials of Trade (173.6)

Certain hazmat carried as "materials of trade" -- small quantities used for the carrier's own purposes, not offered for commercial transport -- are exempt from labeling. For example, a service technician carrying a small cylinder of acetylene for field welding work. Strict quantity limits apply: no more than 1 liter (or 1 kg net mass) per container for materials in Packing Groups I or II.

### Other Exceptions

- **ORM-D** (now phased out, replaced by Limited Quantity mark): Consumer commodities previously shipped under ORM-D rules did not require hazard class labels.
- **Certain Class 9 materials**: Some Class 9 materials may have modified labeling requirements depending on their specific entry in the Hazmat Table.
- **Empty packagings**: Packages that previously contained hazmat but have been emptied and not cleaned may be shipped without labels if they meet the conditions of 49 CFR 173.29 (residue remaining is not more than what clings to the container walls, and the container is properly closed).

### What Exceptions Do NOT Mean

An exception from labeling does **not** mean the material is not hazardous.

, and the container is properly closed).

### What Exceptions Do NOT Mean

An exception from labeling does **not** mean the material is not hazardous. It means the quantity is small enough, or the packaging is robust enough, that reduced hazard communication is considered adequate. The material itself is still hazardous, and appropriate handling precautions still apply.

---

## Section 6: Matching Labels to Placards

Understanding the connection between package-level labels and vehicle-level placards is critical for fleet operations. Labels on individual packages determine what placards go on your truck.

### The Basic Rule

When you load packages bearing hazmat labels onto a vehicle, those labels dictate the placard requirements. The general rule under 49 CFR 172.504:

- **1,001 pounds or more** (aggregate gross weight) of hazmat in a single hazard class: the vehicle must be placarded for that class
- **Any quantity** of certain high-hazard materials (Table 1 materials): the vehicle must be placarded regardless of weight. Table 1 includes:
  - Class 1.1, 1.2, 1.3 (Explosives)
  - Class 2.3 (Toxic Gas)
  - Class 4.3 (Dangerous When Wet)
  - Class 6.1, PG I, Inhalation Hazard (Poison Inhalation Hazard)
  - Class 7 (Radioactive, certain quantities)

### How Labels Drive Placard Selection

| Labels on packages | Placard on vehicle |
|--------------------|--------------------|
| Class 3 (Flammable Liquid) labels, total > 1,001 lbs | FLAMMABLE placard, all four sides |
| Class 8

|
|--------------------|--------------------|
| Class 3 (Flammable Liquid) labels, total > 1,001 lbs | FLAMMABLE placard, all four sides |
| Class 8 (Corrosive) labels, total > 1,001 lbs | CORROSIVE placard, all four sides |
| Any quantity of Class 2.3 (Toxic Gas) labels | POISON GAS placard, all four sides |
| Mixed load of Class 3 and Class 8, each > 1,001 lbs | Both FLAMMABLE and CORROSIVE placards |
| Mixed load of multiple Table 2 classes, each < 1,001 lbs but total > 1,001 lbs | DANGEROUS placard (may be used as a substitute) |

### The DANGEROUS Placard

When a vehicle carries multiple hazard classes from Table 2 (the "lower-hazard" list -- Classes 3, 4, 5, 6.1 PG III, 8, 9), and no single class exceeds 1,001 pounds but the total exceeds 1,001 pounds, the carrier may use the **DANGEROUS** placard instead of individual placards for each class. This is the placard with the word "DANGEROUS" split between a red upper half and white lower half.

**However:** The DANGEROUS placard cannot substitute for Table 1 placards. If you have any quantity of a Table 1 material, its specific placard must be displayed even if you also use the DANGEROUS placard for other classes.

### Subsidiary Labels and Placarding

Subsidiary labels on packages also affect placarding. If packages bearing a Class 8 subsidiary label total more than 1,001 pounds, the vehicle needs a CORROSIVE placard -- even though the primary hazard might be Class 3 (Flammable). Both placards go on the vehicle.

### Rea

ounds, the vehicle needs a CORROSIVE placard -- even though the primary hazard might be Class 3 (Flammable). Both placards go on the vehicle.

### Real-World Scenario

**Situation:** Your driver picks up a mixed load:
- 800 lbs of packages labeled Class 3 (Flammable Liquid)
- 600 lbs of packages labeled Class 8 (Corrosive)
- 200 lbs of packages labeled Class 9 (Miscellaneous)

**Total hazmat:** 1,600 lbs
**Any single class over 1,001 lbs?** No
**Total over 1,001 lbs?** Yes

**Placard option 1:** Individual placards for each class (FLAMMABLE, CORROSIVE, CLASS 9) on all four sides
**Placard option 2:** A single DANGEROUS placard on all four sides (because all are Table 2 classes and none individually exceed 1,001 lbs)

Most carriers choose the DANGEROUS placard for simplicity in mixed-load situations.
