# TNDS-HZ-006: Placarding Requirements

## Learning Objectives

After completing this module, you will be able to:

- Explain the purpose of placards and who relies on them
- Distinguish between Table 1 and Table 2 placarding requirements
- Apply the 1,001-pound threshold rule correctly
- Determine when the DANGEROUS placard may or may not be used
- Identify subsidiary hazard placarding requirements
- Describe proper placard placement, maintenance, and removal
- Recognize the role of UN/NA identification numbers on placards

---

## 1. What Is Placarding?

Placards are diamond-shaped signs displayed on the outside of transport vehicles and freight containers. They are at least 250 mm (10.8 inches) on each side, printed in bold colors that correspond to hazard classes. Their single purpose is to communicate what kind of hazardous material is inside the vehicle -- quickly and at a distance.

**Who reads placards?** Firefighters, police officers, EMS crews, HAZMAT teams, and the general public. When a truck is involved in a crash or fire, the placard is often the very first piece of information a first responder has to work with. If the placard is wrong, missing, or unreadable, responders may use the wrong approach

first piece of information a first responder has to work with. If the placard is wrong, missing, or unreadable, responders may use the wrong approach -- and people can get hurt or killed.

**How placards relate to labels:** Labels are smaller (100 mm / 3.9 inches on a side) and go on individual packages. Placards use the same color scheme and symbol design as labels, but they are scaled up for vehicle-level visibility. You can think of it this way: labels tell the warehouse worker what is in a box; placards tell the firefighter what is on the truck.

**Where placards go:** Federal regulations under 49 CFR 172.516 require placards on all four sides of the vehicle -- front, rear, and both sides. This ensures that no matter what angle a responder approaches from, they can identify the hazard. The placard must be clearly visible, upright in the "square-on-point" diamond orientation, and not obscured by ladders, doors, mud, or equipment.

> **Real-World Scenario:** A tanker carrying gasoline rolls over on an interstate off-ramp at 2:00 AM. The first fire engine arrives within four minutes. The crew cannot see the shipping papers because the cab is crushed. But from 200 feet away, the crew chief reads the red FLAMMABLE placard on the rear of the tanker. That single piece of information tells the crew to keep ignition sources away, set up foam lines instead of water, and establish a larger evacuation perimeter. The placard just drove the entire response strategy before anyone got cl

up foam lines instead of water, and establish a larger evacuation perimeter. The placard just drove the entire response strategy before anyone got close to the vehicle.

---

## 2. Table 1 vs. Table 2 Placards (49 CFR 172.504)

This is the most important concept in placarding. The regulations divide hazardous materials into two tables, and the rules for when you need placards are completely different depending on which table your material falls under.

### Table 1: Placard at ANY Quantity

Table 1 materials are so dangerous that a placard is required no matter how little you carry -- even one package. There is no weight threshold. If it is on the truck, you placard.

Table 1 materials include:

| Hazard Class/Division | Placard Name | Why It Is Table 1 |
|---|---|---|
| Division 1.1 | EXPLOSIVES 1.1 | Mass explosion hazard |
| Division 1.2 | EXPLOSIVES 1.2 | Projection hazard |
| Division 1.3 | EXPLOSIVES 1.3 | Fire, minor blast, or projection hazard |
| Division 2.3 | POISON GAS | Toxic by inhalation -- immediate life threat |
| Division 4.3 | DANGEROUS WHEN WET | Produces flammable or toxic gas on contact with water |
| Division 6.1 (PG I, inhalation hazard) | POISON (INHALATION HAZARD) | Fatal vapors at low concentration |
| Class 7 (Yellow III label only) | RADIOACTIVE | Highest external radiation level for transport |

**Memory aid:** Table 1 materials are the ones where even a small release could cause mass casualties. Explosives blow up. Poison gas kills by breathing.

emory aid:** Table 1 materials are the ones where even a small release could cause mass casualties. Explosives blow up. Poison gas kills by breathing. Dangerous when wet reacts violently with something that is everywhere (water). If emergency response goes wrong with these materials, the consequences are catastrophic. That is why there is no quantity exemption.

### Table 2: Placard at 1,001 Pounds or More

Table 2 materials require placarding only when the aggregate gross weight of all Table 2 materials on the vehicle reaches or exceeds 454 kg (1,001 lbs). This is commonly called the "1,001-pound rule."

Table 2 materials include:

| Hazard Class/Division | Placard Name |
|---|---|
| Division 1.4 | EXPLOSIVES 1.4 |
| Division 1.5 | EXPLOSIVES 1.5 |
| Division 1.6 | EXPLOSIVES 1.6 |
| Division 2.1 | FLAMMABLE GAS |
| Division 2.2 | NON-FLAMMABLE GAS |
| Division 4.1 | FLAMMABLE SOLID |
| Division 4.2 | SPONTANEOUSLY COMBUSTIBLE |
| Division 5.1 | OXIDIZER |
| Division 5.2 | ORGANIC PEROXIDE |
| Division 6.1 (other than PG I inhalation) | POISON |
| Class 8 | CORROSIVE |
| Class 9 | CLASS 9 (miscellaneous) |
| ORM-D | None required |

**Key detail: aggregate gross weight.** "Aggregate" means you add up the gross weight (material plus packaging) of ALL Table 2 materials on the vehicle, regardless of hazard class. You do not calculate each class separately for the 1,001-pound threshold.

> **Worked Example:** Your truck picks up the following shipments:
>
> - 400 lbs of flammabl

ate each class separately for the 1,001-pound threshold.

> **Worked Example:** Your truck picks up the following shipments:
>
> - 400 lbs of flammable liquid (Division 3, Table 2)
> - 300 lbs of corrosive material (Class 8, Table 2)
> - 350 lbs of oxidizer (Division 5.1, Table 2)
>
> **Total aggregate gross weight: 1,050 lbs.** This exceeds 1,001 lbs, so placarding IS required. You need placards for each hazard class present: FLAMMABLE, CORROSIVE, and OXIDIZER -- or you may use the DANGEROUS placard (see Section 3 below).

> **Another Example:** Your truck has only 800 lbs of flammable liquid and nothing else.
>
> 800 lbs is below the 1,001-lb threshold. No placards required for Table 2 materials.

> **Mixed Table Example:** Your truck has 1 package (50 lbs) of Division 2.3 Poison Gas and 500 lbs of flammable liquid.
>
> The Division 2.3 is a Table 1 material -- you MUST placard POISON GAS regardless of quantity. The 500 lbs of flammable liquid is below the 1,001-lb Table 2 threshold on its own -- but because you are already required to placard for the Table 1 material, you should check whether the total Table 2 aggregate triggers additional placarding. In this case, 500 lbs is below 1,001 lbs, so no FLAMMABLE placard is needed. The truck displays POISON GAS placards only.

---

## 3. The DANGEROUS Placard

The DANGEROUS placard is a white diamond with the word "DANGEROUS" in red. It exists as a convenience for carriers transporting mixed loads of Table 2 materials.

### Whe

d is a white diamond with the word "DANGEROUS" in red. It exists as a convenience for carriers transporting mixed loads of Table 2 materials.

### When You May Use It

You may use a single DANGEROUS placard in place of multiple specific Table 2 placards when:

- The vehicle carries **two or more** Table 2 materials that would require **different** placards, AND
- The **total aggregate gross weight** of all Table 2 materials is 1,001 lbs or more

Instead of displaying FLAMMABLE, CORROSIVE, and OXIDIZER placards (from the example above), you could display a single DANGEROUS placard on all four sides.

### When You Must NOT Use It

You **cannot** use the DANGEROUS placard when:

1. **1,000 kg (2,205 lbs) or more** of a single Table 2 material is loaded at one facility. In that case, you must display the **specific placard** for that material. You may still use DANGEROUS for the other Table 2 materials if they individually fall below 2,205 lbs.

2. **Table 1 materials.** The DANGEROUS placard never replaces Table 1 placards. A Table 1 material always gets its own specific placard.

> **Scenario:** A driver picks up 2,500 lbs of sulfuric acid (Class 8, Corrosive) at Warehouse A, then picks up 200 lbs of paint (Division 3, Flammable) and 300 lbs of bleach (Division 5.1, Oxidizer) at Warehouse B.
>
> - The sulfuric acid exceeds 2,205 lbs loaded at one location, so you **must** display a CORROSIVE placard.
> - For the remaining materials (paint + bleach = 500 lbs), the aggregate is b

bs loaded at one location, so you **must** display a CORROSIVE placard.
> - For the remaining materials (paint + bleach = 500 lbs), the aggregate is below 1,001 lbs on its own, so additional placards are not required for those materials.
> - Final placarding: CORROSIVE on all four sides.

> **Another Scenario:** A driver picks up 600 lbs of paint thinner (Flammable), 500 lbs of muriatic acid (Corrosive), and 400 lbs of calcium hypochlorite (Oxidizer) -- all from the same warehouse.
>
> - Total aggregate: 1,500 lbs -- exceeds 1,001 lbs, so placarding is required.
> - No single material exceeds 2,205 lbs at one loading point.
> - The driver may use the DANGEROUS placard on all four sides instead of displaying FLAMMABLE, CORROSIVE, and OXIDIZER.

---

## 4. Subsidiary Hazard Placards

Some hazardous materials pose more than one type of hazard. The primary hazard determines the main placard, but if the material has a **subsidiary hazard**, an additional placard may be required.

**How to know:** The shipping papers and the Hazardous Materials Table (49 CFR 172.101) list subsidiary hazards. If a subsidiary placard is required, it will be indicated in Column 6 of the Hazardous Materials Table.

**Common examples:**

- **Methyl isocyanate** is primarily a Division 6.1 Poison but also has a Division 3 Flammable subsidiary hazard. The vehicle displays both POISON and FLAMMABLE placards.
- **Chlorine** is Division 2.3 Poison Gas with a Division 5.1 Oxidizer and Division 8 Corrosive sub

hicle displays both POISON and FLAMMABLE placards.
- **Chlorine** is Division 2.3 Poison Gas with a Division 5.1 Oxidizer and Division 8 Corrosive subsidiary hazard. The vehicle displays POISON GAS, OXIDIZER, and CORROSIVE placards.

**Rule of thumb:** When in doubt, check the shipping papers. If a subsidiary hazard label is required on the package, the corresponding placard is required on the vehicle.

---

## 5. Placard Placement and Specifications

Federal regulations are specific about how placards must be displayed (49 CFR 172.516):

- **All four sides:** Front, rear, left side, right side. No exceptions.
- **Square-on-point orientation:** The diamond must stand on one corner, not flat on a side.
- **Clear and visible:** Placards must be readable from the direction they face. Nothing can obscure them -- no mud, no cargo straps over them, no doors swung open in front of them during loading.
- **Securely attached:** Placards must be held in holders or otherwise attached so they do not blow off at highway speeds. Wire, clips, or approved placard holders are standard.
- **Not on doors:** If a placard is mounted on a door or panel that swings open during loading/unloading, the placard is not visible while the door is open. Use a location that remains visible at all times, or have a procedure to ensure placards are displayed when the vehicle is in transit.
- **Condition:** Placards must not be faded, torn, or damaged to the point of being illegible. Replace worn placards.
- **

the vehicle is in transit.
- **Condition:** Placards must not be faded, torn, or damaged to the point of being illegible. Replace worn placards.
- **Color and size:** Must conform to the design specifications in 49 CFR 172.519 and 172.521. Minimum size is 250 mm (10.8 inches) on each side. Colors must match the regulatory standards (red for flammable, green for non-flammable gas, yellow for oxidizer, white for poison, etc.).

> **Practical Tip:** Many fleets use flip-placard holders mounted permanently on all four sides of their trailers. The driver flips to the correct placard for the current load. This is fast, keeps the placard secure, and avoids the problem of placards blowing off the truck at 65 mph.

---

## 6. When Placards Must Be Removed

This is a point many drivers overlook, and inspectors look for it.

**The rule (49 CFR 172.502):** No person may display a placard on a vehicle unless the vehicle contains the hazardous material identified by that placard, or residue of that material. Once the hazmat cargo is unloaded and the vehicle is clean, placards must be removed or covered.

**Why it matters:**

- **False alarm response:** If a truck displaying FLAMMABLE placards is involved in a fender-bender but is actually carrying auto parts, the fire department may roll a full HAZMAT team, shut down the highway, and evacuate nearby buildings -- all for nothing. This wastes emergency resources and costs money.
- **Legal violation:** Displaying incorrect placards is a fede

e nearby buildings -- all for nothing. This wastes emergency resources and costs money.
- **Legal violation:** Displaying incorrect placards is a federal violation under 49 CFR 172.502(a). Fines apply.
- **Tunnel and bridge restrictions:** A truck displaying hazmat placards may be refused entry to tunnels or bridges that restrict hazmat vehicles. If the truck is actually empty, the driver has unnecessarily limited their routing options.

> **Best Practice:** Make placard removal part of your post-unloading checklist. Walk around the vehicle after unloading and flip, remove, or cover all placards. Some carriers require a photo of the bare (unplacarded) vehicle as documentation.

---

## 7. UN/NA Number Display

Every hazardous material has a four-digit identification number assigned by the United Nations (UN) or North America (NA). This number appears in the ERG and helps responders identify the exact material, not just the hazard class.

**Display options (49 CFR 172.332-336):**

1. **On the placard itself:** The four-digit number is printed in the center of the placard, replacing the hazard class number. This is the most common method for bulk shipments.
2. **On an orange panel:** A rectangular orange panel (at least 160 mm high by 400 mm wide) displaying the four-digit number is placed adjacent to the placard. This method is often used when a vehicle carries multiple materials.

**When required:**

- Bulk packaging (capacity greater than 450 L / 119 gallons for liquids, or

ften used when a vehicle carries multiple materials.

**When required:**

- Bulk packaging (capacity greater than 450 L / 119 gallons for liquids, or greater than 400 kg / 882 lbs for solids)
- Cargo tanks, tank cars, and portable tanks
- Certain non-bulk shipments of hazmat in quantities requiring placards

> **Example:** A cargo tank carrying diesel fuel displays a red FLAMMABLE placard with "1993" (the UN number for diesel fuel/fuel oil) printed in the center on all four sides. When a responder sees "1993," they look it up in the ERG and find specific guidance for diesel fuel spills, including recommended isolation distances and firefighting procedures.

> **Scenario:** A flatbed truck carries 20 drums of two different chemicals -- UN 1203 (gasoline) and UN 1830 (sulfuric acid). Rather than printing UN numbers on the placards, the carrier uses FLAMMABLE and CORROSIVE placards plus separate orange panels displaying "1203" and "1830" on the appropriate sides.

---

## 8. Common Placard Mistakes

These are the errors that show up repeatedly in FMCSA roadside inspections and internal safety audits:

| Mistake | Why It Happens | Consequence |
|---|---|---|
| Forgetting to remove old placards | Driver unloads hazmat, picks up non-hazmat load, does not walk around vehicle | False emergency response; federal violation |
| Wrong Table 1 vs. Table 2 determination | Driver does not check shipping papers carefully; assumes all materials follow the 1,001-lb rule | Traveling without req

1 vs. Table 2 determination | Driver does not check shipping papers carefully; assumes all materials follow the 1,001-lb rule | Traveling without required placard (Table 1 material); out-of-service violation |
| Using DANGEROUS when a specific placard is required | Driver does not realize that 2,205 lbs of one material from one facility requires a specific placard | Federal violation; placard does not communicate correct hazard to responders |
| Damaged or illegible placards | Weather, road grime, age | Treated as missing placard during inspection |
| Placard on swinging door | Placard mounted on trailer door that is open during loading | Not visible during operations; inspection violation |
| Missing one side | Placard fell off or was never placed on one of the four required sides | Out-of-service violation at inspection |
| Wrong placard for the material | Mix-up between similar-looking placards (e.g., OXIDIZER vs. ORGANIC PEROXIDE) | Wrong emergency response; potential injury |

> **Cost of Getting It Wrong:** An out-of-service order for a placarding violation means the truck sits on the side of the road until the violation is corrected. The driver loses time, the load is delayed, and the carrier gets a negative mark on its CSA BASIC scores. Repeat violations can lead to increased inspections and potential operating authority consequences.

**B**: Remove or cover the placards before operating the vehicle (correct)
**C**: Leave them in place because removing placards is the shipper's responsibility
**D**: Cover only the front and rear placards, leaving the side placards visible
**Explanation**: 49 CFR 172.502(a) prohibits displaying a placard on a vehicle that does not contain the corresponding hazardous material (or its residue). Displaying incorrect placards can trigger unnecessary emergency response, restrict the vehicle from tunnels and bridges, and is a federal v

residue). Displaying incorrect placards can trigger unnecessary emergency response, restrict the vehicle from tunnels and bridges, and is a federal violation. All four placards must be removed or covered.
**CFR Reference**: 49 CFR 172.502(a)

### Q6
**Type**: multiple_choice
**Question**: What is the minimum dimension for each side of a standard hazmat placard?
**A**: 100 mm (3.9 inches)
**B**: 250 mm (10.8 inches) (correct)
**C**: 300 mm (11.8 inches)
**D**: 200 mm (7.9 inches)
**Explanation**: Under 49 CFR 172.519(c), each side of a placard must measure at least 250 mm (10.8 inches). This is distinct from hazmat labels, which are 100 mm (3.9 inches) on a side. The larger size ensures placards are readable from a distance by emergency responders.

# TNDS-HZ-007a: Carrier Requirements -- Highway

## Learning Objectives

After completing this module, you will be able to:

- Describe the dual regulatory framework (PHMSA + FMCSA) governing hazmat highway transport
- Apply loading and unloading safety procedures correctly
- Read and apply the hazmat segregation table for mixed loads
- Explain attendance and surveillance requirements for parked hazmat vehicles
- Identify parking distance restrictions and safe haven requirements
- Follow hazmat routing rules and tunnel/bridge restrictions
- Respond correctly to tire failures, fires, and emergencies on a hazmat vehicle
- Describe driver responsibilities during a hazmat incident

---

## 1. Highway Carrier Responsibilities

When you drive a truck carrying hazardous materials on public highways, you operate under two overlapping sets of federal regulations:

- **PHMSA (Pipeline and Hazardous Materials Safety Administration)** regulations under 49 CFR Parts 171-180 govern the hazardous material itself -- packaging, labeling, placarding, and shipping papers.
- **FMCSA (Federal Motor Carrier Safety Administration)** regulations under 49 CFR Parts 177 and 397 govern how you transport those materials -- loadi

- **FMCSA (Federal Motor Carrier Safety Administration)** regulations under 49 CFR Parts 177 and 397 govern how you transport those materials -- loading procedures, driving rules, parking, routing, and emergency response.

**The driver is the last line of defense.** The shipper classified the material. The packaging manufacturer built the container. The warehouse loaded it. But you are the person who takes it out on the highway, and you are the person who must deal with it if something goes wrong at 65 mph.

### Driver Responsibilities Before Departure

1. **Inspect shipping papers.** Verify that every hazmat shipment on your vehicle has complete, legible shipping papers with the proper shipping name, hazard class, UN/NA number, packing group, and emergency contact number. The papers must be within immediate reach while driving (on the seat or in the door pouch) and on the driver's seat or in the door holder when you leave the cab.

2. **Verify placards match cargo.** Walk around the vehicle and confirm that the displayed placards match the hazard classes shown on the shipping papers. If the placards are wrong, do not leave until they are corrected.

3. **Check packaging integrity.** Look for leaking containers, damaged drums, broken seals, crushed boxes, or packages that are not oriented correctly (arrows must point up). If a package is damaged and leaking, do not transport it. Notify your dispatcher and the shipper.

4. **Know your emergency procedures.** You must have acce

ackage is damaged and leaking, do not transport it. Notify your dispatcher and the shipper.

4. **Know your emergency procedures.** You must have access to the Emergency Response Guidebook (ERG) or equivalent emergency response information for every hazmat load. Know the guide number for your cargo before you start driving.

> **Real-World Scenario:** A driver picks up a load of mixed chemicals at a distribution warehouse. The warehouse dock worker hands over the shipping papers listing three different hazardous materials. The driver notices the truck has FLAMMABLE placards from a previous load, but one of the current materials is a Class 8 Corrosive. The driver stops, requests CORROSIVE placards, and does not leave the facility until all four sides are correctly placarded. This is exactly what the regulations require.

---

## 2. Loading and Unloading Rules (49 CFR 177.834-842)

Loading and unloading are the highest-risk moments in hazmat transport. The material is being moved, opened, transferred, or exposed. The regulations under Part 177 establish strict procedures.

### General Requirements for All Hazmat

- **Set the parking brake.** Before any loading or unloading begins, the parking brake must be set. The vehicle must not move during the operation.
- **Turn off the engine.** The engine must be off during loading and unloading. Exception: when the engine is needed to operate a pump for transferring product (e.g., fuel delivery), the engine may run, but only for the pum

ading. Exception: when the engine is needed to operate a pump for transferring product (e.g., fuel delivery), the engine may run, but only for the pump operation.
- **No smoking within 25 feet.** No person may smoke within 25 feet of any vehicle being loaded or unloaded with hazmat. This includes e-cigarettes and any open flame.
- **Package orientation.** Packages marked with orientation arrows ("THIS SIDE UP") must be loaded with arrows pointing upward. Containers designed for upright storage must be stored upright.
- **No damaged packages.** Do not load any package that is leaking, has a broken seal, shows damage to the outer container, or appears to have been improperly packaged. Report it to the shipper and your dispatcher.
- **Secure the cargo.** All packages must be braced, blocked, or otherwise secured to prevent shifting or falling during transport. A 55-gallon drum of solvent that topples over and breaks open at a highway speed event is a HAZMAT incident.

### Flammable Liquids -- Grounding and Bonding

When transferring flammable liquids between a cargo tank and a storage tank (fuel delivery, for example):

- **Grounding:** The cargo tank must be electrically grounded to the earth to prevent static charge buildup.
- **Bonding:** A bonding wire must connect the cargo tank to the receiving tank so both are at the same electrical potential.
- **Why it matters:** Static electricity can ignite flammable vapors. A single spark during fuel transfer can cause a fire or expl

ectrical potential.
- **Why it matters:** Static electricity can ignite flammable vapors. A single spark during fuel transfer can cause a fire or explosion. Grounding and bonding eliminate the voltage difference that causes sparks.

> **Scenario:** A fuel delivery driver pulls up to a gas station. Before opening any valves, the driver connects the grounding cable from the truck to the grounding lug at the station, then connects the bonding cable between the truck's discharge port and the station's fill port. Only after both connections are made does the driver begin the transfer. This is not optional -- it is the law, and it prevents fires.

### Specific Loading Rules by Material

- **Explosives (Division 1.1-1.6):** Must be loaded in a vehicle with a closed body or covered with a tarpaulin. No metal tools near detonators. No detonators loaded in the same vehicle with other explosives (with limited exceptions).
- **Flammable solids and oxidizers:** Keep away from heat sources. Do not load with acids.
- **Corrosives:** Load below other freight to prevent leaks from dripping onto other cargo. Secure upright.
- **Poisons:** Never load in the same vehicle space with foodstuffs, animal feed, or any material intended for consumption by humans or animals.

---

## 3. Segregation and Separation (49 CFR 177.848)

Not all hazardous materials can ride together. Some combinations react violently. The segregation table in 49 CFR 177.848 tells you which materials can share a vehicle and wh

als can ride together. Some combinations react violently. The segregation table in 49 CFR 177.848 tells you which materials can share a vehicle and which cannot.

### Reading the Segregation Table

The table is a grid with hazard classes listed across the top and down the side. Where a row and column intersect, you will find one of these symbols:

| Symbol | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **X** | **Do not load together in the same vehicle.** These materials are incompatible. They must be in separate vehicles. |
| **O** | **May be loaded together only if separated by a non-combustible divider.** The divider must be solid, non-combustible (metal or equivalent), and must physically separate the two materials within the vehicle. |
| *(blank)* | **No segregation restriction.** These materials may be loaded together. |

### Critical Combinations to Know

These are the combinations that cause the most serious incidents when the segregation rules are violated:

1. **Oxidizers + Flammable Liquids = X (do not load together).** Oxidizers feed fire. Flammable liquids are fuel. If a container of each breaks open in the same vehicle space, you have the two ingredients for an intense fire or explosion.

2. **Poisons + Foodstuffs = Absolute prohibition.** Poisonous materials (Division 6.1) must never be loaded in the same vehicle with food, feed, or any edible material. This is not just a segregation table issue -- it is a separate, specific prohibition in 49 CFR 177.841(e). Contamination of food wit

edible material. This is not just a segregation table issue -- it is a separate, specific prohibition in 49 CFR 177.841(e). Contamination of food with toxic chemicals is a public health catastrophe.

3. **Cyanides + Acids = Produces hydrogen cyanide gas.** This is a lethal combination. Cyanide salts reacting with even mild acids produce hydrogen cyanide, a fast-acting lethal gas. These materials must be separated.

4. **Oxidizers + Organic Peroxides.** Organic peroxides are inherently unstable. Adding an oxidizer accelerates decomposition. Do not load together.

> **Worked Example:** A dispatcher assigns a driver to pick up three shipments:
>
> - Shipment A: 500 lbs of sodium hypochlorite solution (Division 5.1, Oxidizer)
> - Shipment B: 300 lbs of acetone (Division 3, Flammable Liquid)
> - Shipment C: 200 lbs of sodium hydroxide solution (Class 8, Corrosive)
>
> The driver checks the segregation table:
> - Oxidizer (5.1) vs. Flammable Liquid (3) = **X** (do not load together)
>
> The driver cannot carry Shipment A and Shipment B in the same vehicle. The driver contacts dispatch to arrange separate transport or sequence the deliveries so both are never on the truck at the same time.
>
> - Oxidizer (5.1) vs. Corrosive (8) = **blank** (no restriction)
> - Flammable Liquid (3) vs. Corrosive (8) = **blank** (no restriction)
>
> The driver could carry A + C together, or B + C together, but not all three.

## 4. Attendance and Surveillance (49 CFR 397.5)

driver could carry A + C together, or B + C together, but not all three.

## 4. Attendance and Surveillance (49 CFR 397.5)

A vehicle containing hazardous materials must be attended or parked in a safe haven at all times when it is on a public road or shoulder.

### What "Attended" Means

A hazmat vehicle is "attended" when the driver (or a qualified representative):

- Is **within 100 feet** of the vehicle
- Has an **unobstructed view** of the vehicle
- Is **awake and alert** (not sleeping in the sleeper berth)
- Is **aware** that the vehicle contains hazmat and knows what to do in an emergency

Sitting in a restaurant 200 feet from the truck does not count. Sleeping in the cab while parked at a truck stop does not count. Standing at a fuel island 50 feet away with a clear line of sight to the vehicle does count.

### What a "Safe Haven" Is

A safe haven is a location approved for the parking of unattended vehicles carrying hazmat. This includes:

- The property of the cargo's **consignee** (the delivery destination)
- The **carrier's own terminal**
- A location designated by government authorities or approved for hazmat vehicle parking

A random truck stop, a highway shoulder, or a Walmart parking lot is **not** a safe haven unless specifically designated as one.

> **Scenario:** A driver hauling chlorine (Division 2.3, Poison Gas) needs to use the restroom at a truck stop. The restroom is inside the building, 250 feet from the truck. The driver cannot leave the vehic

Poison Gas) needs to use the restroom at a truck stop. The restroom is inside the building, 250 feet from the truck. The driver cannot leave the vehicle unattended at a truck stop -- it is not a safe haven. Options:
>
> 1. Use a facility where the restroom is within 100 feet and the driver can maintain an unobstructed view.
> 2. Have a co-driver or qualified person stay with the vehicle.
> 3. Park at a designated safe haven before leaving the vehicle.
>
> This is one of the most practically challenging rules for solo hazmat drivers, and it requires planning stops in advance.

---

## 5. Parking Rules (49 CFR 397.7)

When a hazmat vehicle must be parked, the location matters. These rules protect the public from the consequences of a hazmat release at a stationary vehicle.

### General Parking Rules (All Hazmat)

- **Never park within 5 feet of the traveled portion of the road.** The vehicle must be completely off the roadway, not partially on the shoulder with the wheels in the traffic lane.
- **Never park within 300 feet** of a bridge, tunnel, dwelling, building, or place where people gather (church, school, stadium, theater) **unless there is no practicable alternative.** If you must park within 300 feet due to an operational necessity (e.g., making a delivery to that building), that is permitted, but you do not park near these structures for convenience, rest, or meals.

### Explosives Parking Rules (Division 1.1, 1.2, 1.3)

Vehicles carrying Division 1.1, 1.2, or 1.3 explo

structures for convenience, rest, or meals.

### Explosives Parking Rules (Division 1.1, 1.2, 1.3)

Vehicles carrying Division 1.1, 1.2, or 1.3 explosives have the strictest parking rules:

- Must comply with the **300-foot rule** from all structures, plus:
- Must be parked in a location approved by local authorities when possible
- Must be attended at all times or parked in a safe haven

> **Scenario:** A driver hauling Division 5.1 Oxidizer needs to stop for the night. The driver sees a truck stop with available parking. The nearest building (the truck stop convenience store) is 150 feet from the available parking spaces. Can the driver park there?
>
> Under 49 CFR 397.7(a), the driver should not park within 300 feet of a building unless there is no practicable alternative. If another safe parking location exists farther from buildings, the driver must use it. If this is the only available option after a reasonable search, the driver may park there but should document the necessity.

---

## 6. Routing (49 CFR 397.67, Subpart C)

Hazmat vehicles cannot always take the shortest or fastest route. Federal and state regulations may require specific routing.

### Designated Routes

Many states and municipalities designate specific routes for hazmat vehicles, particularly for:

- **Radioactive materials (Highway Route Controlled Quantities):** Must use Interstate highways or state-designated alternate routes whenever possible. Drivers must use the route that results in the lowes

antities):** Must use Interstate highways or state-designated alternate routes whenever possible. Drivers must use the route that results in the lowest overall radiation exposure to the public.
- **Other hazmat:** States and cities may designate routes through industrial areas and away from residential neighborhoods, hospitals, and schools.

### When No Designated Route Exists

If there is no designated route for the material being transported, the driver must use the route that **minimizes time through or near populated areas.** This does not always mean the shortest route or the fastest route. It means the route with the least exposure of the public to risk.

> **Practical Application:** A driver delivering flammable liquids has two route options from the terminal to the customer:
>
> - Route A: 15 miles through a downtown area with schools, hospitals, and heavy pedestrian traffic. Estimated time: 40 minutes.
> - Route B: 22 miles on a bypass highway through an industrial zone. Estimated time: 35 minutes.
>
> Route B is the correct choice. It is longer in distance but passes through fewer populated areas and actually takes less time. Even if Route B took 45 minutes, it would still be the better choice because it minimizes exposure to populated areas.

### Know Your Routes

Drivers should familiarize themselves with hazmat routing in their regular operating area. Many carriers post approved hazmat routes in their terminals and include them in route planning software. If you

ing in their regular operating area. Many carriers post approved hazmat routes in their terminals and include them in route planning software. If you are entering an unfamiliar area, contact dispatch or check with local authorities before proceeding.

---

## 7. Fires, Smoking, and Fueling (49 CFR 397.11-15)

### Open Fires

- **Do not drive a hazmat vehicle near an open fire** unless you can safely pass without stopping. If a fire is blocking or adjacent to the road, stop at a safe distance and do not proceed until the fire is out or you receive clearance from emergency personnel.
- **Do not park within 300 feet of an open fire.** If a fire starts near your parked vehicle, move the vehicle immediately if it is safe to do so.

### Smoking

- **No smoking within 25 feet of a hazmat vehicle.** This applies whether the vehicle is loaded with hazmat or is empty but has not been cleaned of hazmat residue.
- This rule applies to the driver, co-driver, dock workers, bystanders -- everyone. The driver is responsible for enforcing this around the vehicle.
- "No smoking" means no cigarettes, no cigars, no pipes, no e-cigarettes, no matches, no lighters, no open flames of any kind.

> **Common Violation:** A driver parks a tanker carrying gasoline residue (the tank is "empty" but not cleaned) at a truck stop, walks 30 feet away, and lights a cigarette. Another driver is smoking 15 feet from the tanker. Both are in violation. The tanker contains flammable vapors even though the liquid ha

a cigarette. Another driver is smoking 15 feet from the tanker. Both are in violation. The tanker contains flammable vapors even though the liquid has been unloaded. Vapors are what ignite, not the liquid itself.

### Fueling

- **Engine off during fueling** in most cases. The engine may remain running only when it is necessary to operate equipment during the fueling process (rare).
- **Someone must be at the nozzle** at all times during fueling. Do not insert the nozzle, lock it open, and walk away. A fuel spill on a hazmat vehicle creates an additional hazard.

---

## 8. Tire and Vehicle Inspection (49 CFR 397.17)

Tire failures on hazmat vehicles are not just inconveniences -- they are potential ignition sources and safety hazards.

### Inspection Requirements

- **At the beginning of each trip:** Inspect all tires before departure.
- **Each time the vehicle is parked:** Inspect tires when you stop. Feel for heat. Look for damage, bulges, cuts, or low pressure.
- **During the trip:** Be alert for handling changes that may indicate a tire problem (vibration, pulling to one side).

### What to Do About a Problem Tire

- **Flat tire or hot tire:** Do not continue driving. A flat tire running on the rim generates sparks. A hot tire may be about to blow out or catch fire. Either condition near a cargo of flammable or explosive material is extremely dangerous.
- **Immediate action:** Move the vehicle to a safe location away from populated areas if possible. Remove the damaged

aterial is extremely dangerous.
- **Immediate action:** Move the vehicle to a safe location away from populated areas if possible. Remove the damaged tire or wait for roadside service. Do not drive on a damaged tire to "get to the next exit."

> **Scenario:** A driver hauling Division 1.5 blasting agents feels a vibration and pulls over to check. The right rear outside tire is flat and hot. The driver:
>
> 1. Parks the vehicle as far from the road and any structures as possible.
> 2. Sets the parking brake and chocks the wheels.
> 3. Does NOT attempt to change the tire (the hot tire could ignite if disturbed near the cargo).
> 4. Calls for roadside service and notifies dispatch.
> 5. Remains with the vehicle (attendance requirement) and keeps bystanders away.
>
> This is the correct response. The instinct to "just change it and keep going" can lead to a fire or worse when hazmat is on board.

---

## 9. Emergency Response and Incident Reporting

When something goes wrong with a hazmat load on the highway, the driver's actions in the first minutes determine whether the situation is contained or becomes a disaster.

### Driver's Immediate Actions (in order)

1. **Protect yourself.** Move upwind and uphill from any spill or release. Do not walk through spilled material. Do not inhale vapors.
2. **Warn others.** Keep bystanders, other motorists, and pedestrians away from the area. Use emergency triangles or flares (but NOT flares near flammable materials -- use triangles only).
3

er motorists, and pedestrians away from the area. Use emergency triangles or flares (but NOT flares near flammable materials -- use triangles only).
3. **Isolate the area.** Establish a perimeter. The ERG provides recommended isolation distances for each material.
4. **Call for help.** Call 911. Then call your carrier's emergency number and the emergency contact number listed on the shipping papers.
5. **Provide information.** Give the shipping papers to emergency responders when they arrive. The papers tell responders exactly what material is involved, how much there is, and what hazards it presents.

### Using the ERG

The Emergency Response Guidebook (ERG 2024) is your field reference. Every hazmat driver should carry a copy and know how to use it:

1. Look up the four-digit UN/NA number in the yellow-bordered pages (numerical index) or the material name in the blue-bordered pages (alphabetical index).
2. The index gives you a three-digit Guide Number.
3. Turn to that Guide Number in the orange-bordered pages for specific emergency response guidance: fire, spill, and first aid procedures.
4. If the material is highlighted in the index, check the green-bordered pages for initial isolation and protective action distances.

### Incident Reporting

- **Immediate notification:** Certain hazmat releases require immediate telephone notification to the National Response Center (1-800-424-8802). This includes: death or major injury, evacuation of the general public, closure of a ma

notification to the National Response Center (1-800-424-8802). This includes: death or major injury, evacuation of the general public, closure of a major roadway or facility, or release of a marine pollutant exceeding its reportable quantity.
- **Written report:** A detailed written Hazardous Materials Incident Report (DOT Form 5800.1) must be filed within 30 days of any incident involving unintentional release of hazmat during transport, or any event that meets the reporting criteria above.

> **Scenario:** A driver is involved in a rear-end collision. The impact cracks a drum of hydrochloric acid (Class 8, Corrosive, UN 1789) in the trailer. The driver smells acid and sees liquid dripping from the trailer.
>
> 1. The driver exits the cab, moves upwind, and does not approach the leaking drum.
> 2. The driver warns nearby motorists to stay back and move upwind.
> 3. The driver calls 911, reports a hazmat spill, and provides the material name, UN number, and hazard class.
> 4. The driver calls the carrier's emergency number.
> 5. When fire department HAZMAT team arrives, the driver hands over the shipping papers.
> 6. After the scene is cleared, the driver notifies the National Response Center.
> 7. Within 30 days, the carrier files DOT Form 5800.1.

---

## 10. Tunnels and Bridges

Many tunnels and some bridges restrict or completely prohibit hazmat vehicles. These restrictions exist because a hazmat incident in an enclosed tunnel or on a bridge over water presents extreme da

etely prohibit hazmat vehicles. These restrictions exist because a hazmat incident in an enclosed tunnel or on a bridge over water presents extreme danger with limited escape routes and difficult emergency access.

### What Drivers Must Know

- **Plan your route in advance.** Know which tunnels and bridges along your route allow hazmat and which do not.
- **Restrictions vary by material class.** Some tunnels ban all hazmat. Others ban specific classes (e.g., explosives and flammable gases) but allow corrosives and non-flammable materials.
- **Time-of-day restrictions.** Some facilities allow hazmat passage only during off-peak hours (e.g., between 10:00 PM and 6:00 AM) to reduce public exposure.
- **Escort requirements.** Some tunnels require hazmat vehicles to be escorted through by facility personnel. The driver must follow escort instructions exactly.
- **Placards trigger enforcement.** Tunnel and bridge authorities identify hazmat vehicles by their placards. This is another reason why removing placards from empty vehicles matters -- an unplacarded empty truck can use tunnels and bridges freely; a placarded empty truck may be turned away.

### Common Examples

- **Lincoln Tunnel, Holland Tunnel (New York/New Jersey):** Significant hazmat restrictions. Many classes prohibited entirely. Hazmat vehicles are often routed to the George Washington Bridge instead.
- **Fort McHenry Tunnel, Baltimore Harbor Tunnel (Maryland):** Restrict various hazmat classes. The Key Bridge (prior

eorge Washington Bridge instead.
- **Fort McHenry Tunnel, Baltimore Harbor Tunnel (Maryland):** Restrict various hazmat classes. The Key Bridge (prior to 2024) or alternate routes must be used.
- **Eisenhower Tunnel (Colorado, I-70):** Restricts many hazmat classes due to elevation and limited ventilation.

> **Practical Tip:** When planning a route through an unfamiliar area, check tunnel and bridge restrictions before you start driving. Your carrier's route planning department, state DOT websites, and commercial GPS systems with hazmat routing features can identify restricted tunnels and bridges. Do not assume that because a tunnel is on a major interstate, it permits hazmat. Many do not.

**C**: The materials must not be loaded in the same vehicle (correct)
**D**: The materials require special permits to be loaded together
**Explanation**: An "X" in the segregation table means the two materials are incompatible and must not be loaded, transported, or stored together in the same vehicle. They require completely separate transport. An "O" means they may share a vehicle only with a non-combustible divider between them.

### Q2
**Type**: multiple_choice
**Question**: A driver hauling hazardous materials parks the vehicle at a truck stop and walks 150 feet away to enter the restaurant, leaving the vehicle unattended. The truck stop is not a designated safe haven. Is this a violation?
**A**: No, because truck stops are automatically considered safe havens
**B**: Yes, because the driver is beyond 100 feet from the vehicle and the location is not a safe haven (correct)
**C**: No, because the driver can still see the vehicle from inside the restaurant
**D**: Yes, but only if the vehicle is carrying explosives
**Explanation**: Under 49 CFR 397.5, a hazmat vehicle on a public road must be attended at all times or parked in a safe haven. "Attended" requires the driver to be within 100 feet with an unobstructed view. At 150 feet, the driver exceeds the 100-foot limit. A truck stop is not a safe haven unless specifically designated as one.
**CFR Reference**: 49 CFR 397.5(a)

### Q3

**Question**: How close to a bridge, tunnel, or building may a hazmat vehicle be parked?
**A**: No closer than 100 feet
**B**: No closer than 300 feet, unless there is no practicable alternative (correct)
**C**: No closer than 500 feet under any circumstances
**D**: There are no parking distance restrictions for hazmat vehicles
**E

alternative (correct)
**C**: No closer than 500 feet under any circumstances
**D**: There are no parking distance restrictions for hazmat vehicles
**Explanation**: 49 CFR 397.7 prohibits parking a hazmat vehicle within 300 feet of a bridge, tunnel, dwelling, or place where people gather, except when there is no practicable alternative. The "no practicable alternative" exception covers operational necessities like making a delivery, but does not apply to convenience stops.
**CFR Reference**: 49 CFR 397.7(a)

### Q4
**Type**: multiple_choice
**Question**: During loading of hazardous materials, how far from the vehicle is smoking prohibited?
**A**: 10 feet
**B**: 25 feet (correct)
**C**: 50 feet
**D**: 100 feet
**Explanation**: 49 CFR 177.834(c) and 49 CFR 397.13 both establish a 25-foot no-smoking zone around any vehicle being loaded or unloaded with hazardous materials. This also applies to parked hazmat vehicles (loaded or empty uncleaned). The rule covers all persons, not just the driver.
**CFR Reference**: 49 CFR 177.834(c), 49 CFR 397.13

### Q5
**Type**: multiple_choice
**Question**: A driver discovers a hot tire on a vehicle carrying flammable liquids during a trip inspection. What should the driver do?
**A**: Continue driving slowly to the nearest service station
**B**: Stop immediately in a safe location, do not continue driving on the damaged tire, and call for service (correct)
**C**: Deflate the tire and continue driving on the rim to the next exit
**D**:

continue driving on the damaged tire, and call for service (correct)
**C**: Deflate the tire and continue driving on the rim to the next exit
**D**: Ignore it if the tire is not visibly flat
**Explanation**: Under 49 CFR 397.17, a driver who discovers a tire that is flat, leaking, or overheated on a hazmat vehicle must stop at the nearest safe location. A hot tire on a vehicle carrying flammable liquids is a fire hazard -- sparks from a rim or a tire fire could ignite the cargo. The driver must not continue driving on the damaged tire.
**CFR Reference**: 49 CFR 397.17(b)

### Q6
**Type**: multiple_choice
**Question**: Which of the following cargo combinations would violate the hazmat segregation rules?
**A**: Corrosive material loaded with non-flammable gas
**B**: Oxidizer loaded with flammable liquid in the same vehicle (correct)
**C**: Flammable solid loaded with corrosive material
**D**: Non-flammable gas loaded with Class 9 miscellaneous material
**Explanation**: The segregation table in 49 CFR 177.848 shows an "X" at the intersection of Division 5.1 (Oxidizer) and Division 3 (Flammable Liquid), meaning they must not be loaded together. Oxidizers support combustion, and flammable liquids are fuel -- combining them in the same vehicle creates a severe fire and explosion risk if containers are breached.
**CFR Reference**: 49 CFR 177.848(d)

### Q7
**Type**: multiple_choice
**Question**: When no designated hazmat route exists, what routing principle must a driver follow?
**A**: Always take the shortest route to minimize time the vehicle is on the road
**B**: Always use the interstate highway system regardless of distance
**C**: Use the route that minimizes time through or near populated areas (correct)
**D**: Follow the same route used for non-hazmat loads
**Explanation**: Under 49 CFR 397.67, when no state or local designated route exists, carriers and drivers must use the route that reduces the risk to the public. This means choosing routes that minimize time spent traveling through populated areas, even if those routes are longer in distance.

# Carrier Requirements: Air

## Learning Objectives
- Explain why air transport has the strictest hazmat rules of any mode
- Identify materials that are forbidden on passenger and cargo aircraft
- Interpret Column 9A and 9B of the Hazardous Materials Table for air quantity limitations
- Describe packaging requirements specific to air transport
- Complete or verify a Shipper's Declaration for Dangerous Goods
- Apply current lithium battery shipping rules
- Recognize how air transport restrictions affect highway fleet operators

## Section 1: Why Air Transport Has the Strictest Rules

Of all transportation modes, air transport imposes the most restrictive rules on hazardous materials. There are three reasons for this, and each one is non-negotiable.

**Altitude changes everything.** A commercial aircraft cruises at 30,000 to 40,000 feet, where cabin pressure is maintained at the equivalent of roughly 6,000 to 8,000 feet altitude. Cargo holds may be pressurized to similar levels, but the pressure is still significantly lower than at sea level. A container that is perfectly sealed at ground level can leak, bulge, or rupture at altitude because the reduced external pressure allows internal pressure to push outward.

fectly sealed at ground level can leak, bulge, or rupture at altitude because the reduced external pressure allows internal pressure to push outward. Liquids expand. Gases become more volatile. Pressure relief devices may activate when they would not have activated on the ground.

**Temperature swings are extreme.** Outside temperatures at cruise altitude can reach minus 60 degrees Fahrenheit. The cargo hold temperature depends on the aircraft type — some are heated, some are not. Materials that are stable at room temperature may become unstable at these extremes. Aerosol cans can rupture. Chemical reactions can accelerate or slow unpredictably.

**You cannot pull over at 35,000 feet.** On a highway, if a drum starts leaking, the driver can pull to the shoulder, call hazmat response, and isolate the area. On a rail car, the train can stop. On a ship, the crew can access the deck. In an aircraft, the options are limited to whatever the crew can do in a pressurized metal tube traveling at 500 miles per hour. A fire in the cargo hold of an aircraft is one of the most dangerous scenarios in commercial aviation. There is no "exit to the right."

**History proves the point.** On May 11, 1996, ValuJet Flight 592 crashed into the Florida Everglades, killing all 110 people on board. The cause was improperly packaged chemical oxygen generators — hazmat that should never have been on that aircraft — that ignited in the cargo hold. The generators were not classified, not labeled, and not

ators — hazmat that should never have been on that aircraft — that ignited in the cargo hold. The generators were not classified, not labeled, and not documented on shipping papers. The airline did not know they were on board. That single incident led to sweeping changes in air cargo hazmat regulations. More recently, lithium battery fires in cargo holds have caused multiple aircraft losses, including UPS Flight 6 in Dubai (2010) and Asiana Flight 991 (2011). These are not theoretical risks.

## Section 2: What Is Forbidden on Aircraft

Many hazardous materials that travel safely by highway, rail, or vessel every day are completely forbidden on aircraft. The Hazardous Materials Table in 49 CFR 172.101 tells you what is and is not allowed through two critical columns:

**Column 9A — Passenger Aircraft.** This column shows the maximum net quantity per package allowed on a passenger-carrying aircraft. If the entry says "Forbidden," the material cannot fly on any aircraft carrying passengers — period. No exceptions, no special permits for small quantities.

**Column 9B — Cargo Aircraft Only.** This column shows the maximum net quantity per package allowed on cargo-only aircraft. If this column also says "Forbidden," the material cannot fly at all, on any type of aircraft.

### Materials That Are Always Forbidden by Air

Certain categories of materials are never permitted on aircraft regardless of quantity:

- **Division 1.1 and 1.2 explosives** (mass explosion and projection haza

gories of materials are never permitted on aircraft regardless of quantity:

- **Division 1.1 and 1.2 explosives** (mass explosion and projection hazards) — You cannot ship dynamite, detonators, or military ordnance by air through normal commercial channels.
- **Division 6.2 Category A infectious substances** in quantities exceeding the limits specified in the ICAO Technical Instructions — Ebola virus samples, for example, follow an extremely restricted set of air transport rules.
- **Class 7 radioactive materials** exceeding transport index limits for aircraft.
- **Magnetized materials** with a field strength that could affect aircraft instruments.
- Any material that is "Forbidden" in both Column 9A and Column 9B of the Hazmat Table.

### The "Cargo Aircraft Only" Label

When a material is forbidden on passenger aircraft (Column 9A says "Forbidden") but allowed on cargo aircraft (Column 9B shows a quantity), the package must bear a **"Cargo Aircraft Only"** label. This is a distinctive orange-and-white label that tells everyone in the handling chain: this package cannot go on any aircraft that carries passengers.

**Real-World Scenario**: Your fleet picks up 20 drums of a Class 3 flammable liquid from a chemical plant and delivers them to an air freight forwarder at the airport. You look at the Hazmat Table and see that Column 9A says "Forbidden" but Column 9B says "60 L." This means each drum (up to 60 liters net) can fly on a cargo-only aircraft but not on a passenger fli

9A says "Forbidden" but Column 9B says "60 L." This means each drum (up to 60 liters net) can fly on a cargo-only aircraft but not on a passenger flight. The shipper must apply the Cargo Aircraft Only label to every drum, and the air carrier must ensure those drums never end up on a passenger flight. If your driver notices the labels are missing when picking up the load, that is a problem that needs to be resolved before the cargo moves.

## Section 3: Quantity Limitations

Even when a material is permitted on aircraft, the allowed quantities per package are dramatically lower than what you would handle on a highway shipment.

### Passenger Aircraft Limits (Column 9A)

Typical limits for passenger aircraft are measured in small quantities — often 1 liter, 5 liters, or 25 kilograms per package. Compare that to highway transport, where a single cargo tank can carry 9,000 gallons of the same material. The air limits exist because any incident on a passenger aircraft puts hundreds of lives at immediate risk.

**Example**: Acetone (UN1090, Class 3, Packing Group II) has a Column 9A limit of 5 liters per package on a passenger aircraft. On a highway, you can haul a 5,500-gallon tanker of acetone with proper placarding and documentation. The difference is more than three orders of magnitude.

### Cargo Aircraft Limits (Column 9B)

Cargo aircraft limits are more generous than passenger limits but still far below highway quantities. Typical limits range from 60 liters to 220 liters f

go aircraft limits are more generous than passenger limits but still far below highway quantities. Typical limits range from 60 liters to 220 liters for liquids, and 25 kilograms to 200 kilograms for solids.

### How Limits Apply in Practice

The quantity limits are **per package**, not per shipment. You can ship multiple packages on the same aircraft, each at or below the per-package limit, subject to the aircraft operator's own policies and any aggregate quantity restrictions. However, you cannot put 10 liters of a liquid into one package when the limit is 5 liters and call it compliant — the limit applies to each individual package.

**Practical Tip for Fleet Operators**: If your company ships hazmat that eventually moves by air, you need to package it to air standards from the start. Repackaging at the airport is expensive, time-consuming, and introduces additional handling risk. Build the air quantity limits into your packaging process at the origin.

## Section 4: Accessibility Requirements

Certain hazardous materials loaded on aircraft must remain **accessible to the crew** during flight. This means the cargo must be loaded in a position where a crew member can physically reach it, inspect it, and if necessary, apply fire suppression or separate it from other cargo.

On passenger aircraft, hazmat that is permitted in the cabin or in accessible cargo compartments must be stowed so that a crew member can monitor it. Materials classified as toxic or corrosive that are pe

cabin or in accessible cargo compartments must be stowed so that a crew member can monitor it. Materials classified as toxic or corrosive that are permitted in limited quantities, for example, must not be buried behind tons of other freight where no one can reach them if a leak develops.

On cargo-only aircraft, the accessibility requirement is relaxed for most materials because the entire cargo area is effectively accessible (there is no passenger cabin to protect). However, the operator must still ensure that the hazmat is loaded in a way that allows for identification and response if an incident occurs during flight.

**Why This Matters**: If you are loading a container or unit load device (ULD) that will be placed on an aircraft, the placement of hazmat packages within that container matters. Hazmat should be loaded last (so it is accessible first) or positioned near the container door, not buried in the back behind 4,000 pounds of general freight.

## Section 5: Packaging Requirements Specific to Air

Air transport packaging requirements go beyond what is required for surface transport. PHMSA and the International Civil Aviation Organization (ICAO) both impose additional requirements.

### Inner Packaging Requirements

Liquids shipped by air must have inner packagings — individual bottles, cans, or containers — inside an outer packaging. The inner packagings must be oriented with closures upward and secured against movement. This prevents a single point of failure: if t

uter packaging. The inner packagings must be oriented with closures upward and secured against movement. This prevents a single point of failure: if the outer box is damaged, the inner container still contains the liquid.

### Absorbent Material

Packages containing liquid hazmat for air transport must include enough absorbent material to absorb the entire contents of the inner packaging. If a 1-liter bottle of a Class 3 flammable liquid breaks inside the outer package, the absorbent material must be able to soak up all 1 liter so that nothing leaks out of the outer packaging. This is not optional — it is a regulatory requirement.

### Pressure Differential Testing

Inner packagings for liquids must withstand a pressure differential equivalent to the altitude change during flight. The standard test is either a pressure differential of not less than 95 kPa (approximately 14 psi) or the internal pressure at 55 degrees Celsius (131 degrees Fahrenheit), whichever is greater. This ensures the container will not burst when the aircraft climbs to cruising altitude and the cargo hold pressure drops.

### Closure Requirements

All closures (caps, lids, valves) must be secured against loosening due to vibration. This typically means threaded closures must be torqued to specification and may need secondary closure devices (tape, wire, or shrink bands). Aviation involves constant vibration from engines and turbulence — a loosely threaded cap that would survive a smooth highway trip may w

hrink bands). Aviation involves constant vibration from engines and turbulence — a loosely threaded cap that would survive a smooth highway trip may work itself open in flight.

**Real-World Scenario**: A shipper packages 12 bottles of a Class 8 corrosive liquid in a fiberboard box for air shipment. Each bottle is 500 mL. The shipper uses bubble wrap between the bottles but does not include any absorbent material. At the air freight facility, the cargo is rejected. The shipper must repackage with absorbent pads capable of absorbing at least 6 liters (12 bottles x 500 mL) before the shipment can fly. This delay costs the shipper a day and an additional packaging expense that could have been avoided by doing it right the first time.

## Section 6: Shipper's Declaration for Dangerous Goods

Air shipments of hazardous materials require a specific document called the **Shipper's Declaration for Dangerous Goods**. This is required in addition to any standard shipping papers, bills of lading, or other documentation you might prepare for surface transport. The form is mandated by the ICAO Technical Instructions and the International Air Transport Association (IATA) Dangerous Goods Regulations.

### What Makes This Different from a Highway Shipping Paper

A standard highway shipping paper (bill of lading) includes the proper shipping name, hazard class, UN number, packing group, and quantity. The Shipper's Declaration for Dangerous Goods requires all of that plus:

- The name and addr

e, hazard class, UN number, packing group, and quantity. The Shipper's Declaration for Dangerous Goods requires all of that plus:

- The name and address of the shipper and the consignee
- The airport of departure and the airport of destination
- A declaration that the shipment is within the limitations prescribed for the type of aircraft (passenger or cargo only)
- The number and type of packages
- The packing instruction number from the ICAO Technical Instructions
- The authorization (if applicable) for any special permits or exemptions
- A signed certification by the shipper that the contents are properly identified, classified, packaged, marked, labeled, and in proper condition for transport by air

### Two Copies Required

The shipper must provide at least **two signed copies** of the Declaration. One accompanies the shipment; the other is retained by the air carrier at the origin. The document must be in English (additional languages are permitted but English is mandatory for international shipments).

### Common Errors That Get Shipments Rejected

Air cargo facilities check Shipper's Declarations carefully. The most common reasons for rejection include:

- Missing or incorrect packing instruction number
- Quantity exceeds the limit for the declared aircraft type
- Shipper's certification not signed or dated
- Proper shipping name does not match the UN number
- "Cargo Aircraft Only" not declared when required

**Practical Tip**: If your fleet delivers hazmat to an air f

ing name does not match the UN number
- "Cargo Aircraft Only" not declared when required

**Practical Tip**: If your fleet delivers hazmat to an air freight forwarder, confirm that the Shipper's Declaration is in the envelope with the shipment before you leave the origin. A missing Declaration means the air carrier will refuse the freight, and your driver may get a call to return and pick it up.

## Section 7: Lithium Batteries

Lithium batteries deserve their own section because they are the most common hazmat air transport issue facing fleet operators today. They are everywhere — in laptops, cell phones, power tools, electric vehicles, medical devices, and thousands of consumer products — and they are a serious fire risk when damaged, defective, or improperly packaged.

### Why Lithium Batteries Are Dangerous in Air Transport

Lithium batteries can enter "thermal runaway," a self-heating chain reaction that produces intense fire, toxic gases, and projectile fragments. A single battery in thermal runaway can ignite adjacent batteries, creating a cascading fire that overwhelms aircraft fire suppression systems. The halon-based systems in aircraft cargo holds were designed for Class A fires (paper, wood) and are not effective against lithium battery fires.

### Classification Categories

Lithium batteries are divided into two types:
- **Lithium metal batteries** (non-rechargeable) — UN3090 (standalone) or UN3091 (packed with/contained in equipment)
- **Lithium ion batteries**

es:
- **Lithium metal batteries** (non-rechargeable) — UN3090 (standalone) or UN3091 (packed with/contained in equipment)
- **Lithium ion batteries** (rechargeable) — UN3480 (standalone) or UN3481 (packed with/contained in equipment)

Each type has different quantity thresholds that determine how restrictive the shipping requirements are.

### Section II vs Section I

The ICAO Technical Instructions divide lithium batteries into two sections based on size:

**Section II** covers smaller batteries (e.g., lithium ion cells up to 20 Wh, batteries up to 100 Wh; lithium metal cells up to 1 g lithium content, batteries up to 2 g). These have less restrictive requirements — they may ship as "excepted" with simplified documentation, limited marking, and no full Shipper's Declaration. Most consumer electronics batteries fall into Section II.

**Section I** covers larger batteries that exceed Section II thresholds. These require full Class 9 hazmat shipping procedures: proper shipping name, UN number, Class 9 label, Shipper's Declaration, and compliance with all air transport packaging requirements.

### Standalone vs Packed With vs Contained In

The regulations distinguish between three configurations:

- **Standalone batteries** (not installed in or packed with any device) — These face the strictest rules. Standalone lithium ion batteries shipped by air must be at a **state of charge not exceeding 30%** of their rated capacity. This requirement exists because a lower state of charge

es shipped by air must be at a **state of charge not exceeding 30%** of their rated capacity. This requirement exists because a lower state of charge reduces the energy available to feed a thermal runaway event.

- **Packed with equipment** (batteries are in the same package as the device but not installed in it) — Slightly less restrictive. The batteries must be individually protected against short circuits and must be packed so they cannot move within the outer packaging.

- **Contained in equipment** (batteries are installed in the device) — Least restrictive. The device must be packaged to prevent accidental activation during transport, and the battery must be protected against short circuits.

### Recent Regulatory Changes

Regulations around lithium batteries continue to tighten. Key recent developments include:

- Standalone lithium ion batteries (UN3480) are **forbidden on passenger aircraft** as cargo. They may only fly on cargo aircraft. This has been in effect since 2016 and remains in force.
- The 30% state of charge requirement for standalone lithium ion batteries on cargo aircraft.
- Enhanced packaging requirements including fire-resistant containers and new performance testing standards.
- Proposals for active fire suppression systems in aircraft cargo compartments specifically designed for lithium battery fires.

**Real-World Scenario**: Your fleet delivers a pallet of power tool kits to an air freight facility. Each kit contains a cordless drill with a lithiu

**Real-World Scenario**: Your fleet delivers a pallet of power tool kits to an air freight facility. Each kit contains a cordless drill with a lithium ion battery installed (contained in equipment, UN3481) plus two spare batteries packed alongside (packed with equipment, UN3481). The spare batteries are 40 Wh each — well within Section II limits. The shipment qualifies for Section II exceptions, but each package must be marked with the lithium battery handling mark, and the air waybill must include the statement "Lithium ion batteries in compliance with Section II of PI 966" (for the spares packed with equipment, the applicable packing instruction is PI 967). If the spare batteries were 150 Wh each, they would fall under Section I and require full Class 9 shipping procedures including the Shipper's Declaration. Knowing the watt-hour rating of the batteries before you ship determines which rules apply.

## Section 8: Relevance to Fleet Operators

You might be thinking: "My fleet only runs trucks on highways. Why do I need to know air transport rules?"

The answer is that the transportation chain does not stop at the mode boundary. If your fleet picks up hazmat from a shipper and delivers it to an air freight facility, a seaport, or a rail terminal, the shipment must already comply with the requirements for the next mode. The air carrier will reject a shipment that does not meet air transport standards — and the rejection creates delays, extra costs, and potential violations f

carrier will reject a shipment that does not meet air transport standards — and the rejection creates delays, extra costs, and potential violations for everyone in the chain.

Here are the situations where air transport rules directly affect highway fleet operators:

**Delivering to air freight forwarders.** If the cargo is bound for air transport, the shipper must package and document it to air standards. Your driver should verify that the Shipper's Declaration is present, the Cargo Aircraft Only labels are applied (if required), and the packages are marked correctly before accepting the load.

**Picking up air freight for final-mile delivery.** When your driver picks up hazmat at an airport cargo facility for delivery to the consignee, the shipment was packaged and documented for air transport. The highway leg still requires your own shipping papers, placards (if applicable), and compliance with Part 177 highway rules.

**Shipper errors flow downstream.** If a shipper improperly packages a lithium battery shipment for air transport and your fleet delivers it to the airport, the air carrier will reject it. Your driver is now stuck with refused freight, needs to coordinate a return, and your customer is unhappy. Understanding the basics of air transport rules helps your team catch problems before they leave the dock.

**Intermodal awareness.** Many modern supply chains use multiple modes. A shipment may move by truck to the airport, fly to a hub city, move by truck again to

dal awareness.** Many modern supply chains use multiple modes. A shipment may move by truck to the airport, fly to a hub city, move by truck again to a distribution center, and then move by truck once more to the final customer. Compliance must be maintained across every handoff.

---

## Key Takeaways
- Air transport has the strictest hazmat rules due to altitude pressure changes, temperature extremes, and inability to stop mid-flight
- Column 9A (passenger aircraft) and 9B (cargo aircraft only) in the Hazmat Table determine what can fly and in what quantity
- Packaging for air requires inner containers, absorbent material, and pressure differential testing
- The Shipper's Declaration for Dangerous Goods is mandatory for air hazmat shipments
- Standalone lithium ion batteries are forbidden on passenger aircraft and must be at 30% state of charge or less for cargo aircraft
- Highway fleet operators who deliver to or pick up from air freight facilities must understand air transport standards

**A**: The material cannot be transported by air under any circumstances
**B**: The material can only be transported on cargo-only aircraft, up to 60 liters net per package
**C**: The material can be transported on passenger aircraft in quantities up to 60 liters
**D**: The material

craft, up to 60 liters net per package

**D**: The material requires a special permit from PHMSA for any air transport

A quantity in 9B means the material is allowed on cargo-only aircraft up to that quantity per package. The package must bear a "Cargo Aircraft Only" label.

### Q5

last highway carrier to handle the shipment
