# Carrier Requirements: Rail

## Learning Objectives
- Describe the scale and types of hazmat transported by rail
- Explain train placement rules for hazmat rail cars under 49 CFR 174.81-85
- Identify speed restrictions that apply to trains carrying hazardous materials
- Describe safe switching operations for placarded cars
- Outline tank car inspection requirements
- Explain how rail hazmat incidents differ from highway incidents
- Recognize how rail hazmat rules affect highway fleet operators at transload and intermodal facilities

## Section 1: Rail Transport Overview

Rail is the workhorse of bulk hazmat transportation in the United States. Railroads carry approximately 1.7 million carloads of hazardous materials per year — roughly 4,600 carloads every single day. The materials moving by rail include many of the most dangerous substances in commerce: chlorine for water treatment, anhydrous ammonia for agriculture, liquefied petroleum gas (LPG) for heating, crude oil, ethanol, sulfuric acid, and sodium hydroxide.

The scale of rail hazmat transport is what sets it apart from every other mode. A single railroad tank car can hold between **20,000 and 34,500 gallons** depending on its specification. That is ro

s it apart from every other mode. A single railroad tank car can hold between **20,000 and 34,500 gallons** depending on its specification. That is roughly the equivalent of four to six highway cargo tank trucks in a single railcar. A unit train — a train composed entirely of a single commodity — can consist of 100 or more tank cars. A 100-car unit train of crude oil holds approximately 3 million gallons. When something goes wrong with a rail hazmat shipment, the quantities involved can dwarf anything that occurs on the highway.

### The Incidents That Changed the Rules

Rail hazmat incidents tend to be catastrophic because of the volume involved. These are some of the events that shaped current regulations:

**Lac-Megantic, Quebec (2013)**: A 72-car unit train of Bakken crude oil rolled unattended downhill into the town of Lac-Megantic. Multiple tank cars derailed and ruptured, causing a massive fireball that destroyed 30 buildings in the downtown core and killed 47 people. This disaster led to sweeping changes in tank car design standards, speed restrictions for high-hazard flammable trains, and braking system requirements.

**Graniteville, South Carolina (2005)**: A freight train collided with a parked train in a rail yard, puncturing a tank car carrying chlorine gas (Division 2.3, Toxic by Inhalation). The release of 11,500 gallons of chlorine killed 9 people, injured 554, and forced the evacuation of 5,400 residents within a one-mile radius. Emergency responders could no

gallons of chlorine killed 9 people, injured 554, and forced the evacuation of 5,400 residents within a one-mile radius. Emergency responders could not approach the car for days.

**East Palestine, Ohio (2023)**: A Norfolk Southern freight train carrying vinyl chloride, butyl acrylate, and other hazardous materials derailed. Authorities conducted a controlled burn of vinyl chloride from five tank cars to prevent an uncontrolled explosion, sending a massive plume of black smoke over the community and contaminating soil and waterways.

Each of these incidents reinforced the reality that rail hazmat safety is not about one tank car — it is about the aggregate risk of dozens or hundreds of tank cars moving in a single train.

## Section 2: Train Placement Rules (174.81-85)

The physical position of hazmat cars within a train is regulated because the location of a railcar affects its risk profile in a derailment. The rules in 49 CFR 174.81 through 174.85 establish where hazmat cars can and cannot be placed.

### Buffer Car Requirements

Certain types of hazmat railcars must have **buffer cars** — non-placarded railcars that do not contain hazardous materials — placed between them and other high-risk cars or the locomotive. The buffer car absorbs impact energy in a collision and creates physical separation between incompatible materials.

The general rules for placement include:

- **Placarded cars cannot be placed next to the locomotive** in most cases. There must be at least one

ls.

The general rules for placement include:

- **Placarded cars cannot be placed next to the locomotive** in most cases. There must be at least one non-placarded car (buffer) between any placarded car and an occupied locomotive. The exception is for cars displaying COMBUSTIBLE placards only, which may be placed adjacent to the locomotive.

- **Division 1.1 and 1.2 explosives** (mass explosion hazard and projection hazard) must be placed with the maximum practical separation from the locomotive and from any occupied caboose or crew car. They must also be separated from Division 2.3 (poison gas) and Division 6.1 (poison) cars by at least one buffer car.

- **Division 2.3 Toxic Inhalation Hazard (TIH) materials** — such as chlorine, anhydrous ammonia, and hydrogen fluoride — have some of the most restrictive placement rules. These cars must not be placed next to the locomotive and must be separated from Division 1.1 explosives. Railroads often place TIH cars toward the middle of the train where they are most protected in a derailment.

- **Radioactive materials** (Class 7) require separation distances from occupied spaces and from undeveloped photographic film.

### Why Placement Matters

In a derailment, the forces involved cause cars to jackknife, pile up, and override each other. Cars closest to the point of derailment suffer the most damage. By placing hazmat cars away from the locomotive (which is often near the head of the derailment) and by buffering them from incompati

the most damage. By placing hazmat cars away from the locomotive (which is often near the head of the derailment) and by buffering them from incompatible materials, the rules reduce the chance that a derailment will breach a hazmat car or that two incompatible materials will mix.

**Real-World Scenario**: A railroad is assembling a train that includes 15 loaded tank cars of chlorine (Division 2.3, TIH), 5 cars of Division 1.3 explosives (propellant), and 80 cars of general freight. The train crew cannot simply hook the cars together in any order. The chlorine cars must be separated from the explosives cars by buffer cars. Neither group can be adjacent to the locomotive. The railroad's operating rules and the placement requirements of 174.81-85 dictate a specific marshaling order. Getting this wrong is a federal violation and a safety hazard.

## Section 3: Speed Restrictions

Speed is a critical factor in derailment severity. The kinetic energy involved in a derailment increases with the square of the speed — a train traveling at 50 mph has four times the derailment energy of one traveling at 25 mph. For this reason, trains carrying certain hazardous materials face mandatory speed limits that are lower than the limits for general freight trains.

### Key Speed Rules

- **High-Hazard Flammable Trains (HHFT)**: A train with 20 or more loaded tank cars of Class 3 flammable liquids in a continuous block, or 35 or more such cars anywhere in the train, is classified as a High-Hazar

more loaded tank cars of Class 3 flammable liquids in a continuous block, or 35 or more such cars anywhere in the train, is classified as a High-Hazard Flammable Train. HHFTs are subject to a maximum speed of **50 mph** nationwide, reduced to **40 mph** within high-threat urban areas (HTUAs) unless the train is equipped with electronically controlled pneumatic (ECP) brakes.

- **Toxic Inhalation Hazard (TIH) materials**: Trains carrying TIH materials in key trains (certain configurations) are subject to speed restrictions, typically **50 mph** or less, depending on railroad operating rules and the specific route.

- **Track condition factors**: Speed restrictions also depend on the class of track. A train carrying hazmat on Class 3 track (maximum 40 mph for freight) cannot exceed the track speed limit regardless of any higher general speed limit. Railroads must ensure their hazmat trains operate on track that is in good condition relative to the speed being used.

- **Railroad operating rules**: Individual railroads may impose their own speed restrictions for hazmat trains that are more restrictive than federal requirements. For example, a railroad may limit all TIH trains to 40 mph on a specific subdivision based on curvature, grade, or community exposure.

### Practical Impact

Speed restrictions mean hazmat trains take longer to reach their destinations. This has economic implications — slower trains tie up track capacity and delay other traffic — but the tradeoff is reduc

to reach their destinations. This has economic implications — slower trains tie up track capacity and delay other traffic — but the tradeoff is reduced derailment risk. After Lac-Megantic and East Palestine, the regulatory and public pressure for stricter speed limits on hazmat trains has only increased.

## Section 4: Switching Operations (174.82-83)

Switching is the process of assembling and disassembling trains in rail yards — coupling and uncoupling cars, moving them between tracks, and building trains for departure. Switching is one of the most hazardous activities in railroading because it involves moving cars at close quarters with workers on the ground guiding movements.

### Rules for Handling Placarded Cars During Switching

49 CFR 174.82 and 174.83 establish specific requirements:

- **No rough handling.** Placarded hazmat cars must not be subjected to "humping" or "kicking." Humping is the practice of pushing a car over a hump (a small hill) in the yard and letting gravity roll it down to the correct track, where it couples to other cars with a hard impact. Kicking is a similar practice where a car is given a hard shove and allowed to roll freely into a track. Both practices create violent impacts that can damage tank car fittings, valves, and shells. Placarded cars must be **flat-switched** — moved at controlled speed with the locomotive connected the entire time.

- **Speed limits during coupling.** When coupling to a placarded car, the movement must be made a

peed with the locomotive connected the entire time.

- **Speed limits during coupling.** When coupling to a placarded car, the movement must be made at **no more than 4 mph** and preferably slower. The goal is to minimize the impact force on the tank car. A hard coupling impact can crack welds, damage pressure relief valves, or unseat fittings.

- **Brakes must be set.** Before a placarded car is left standing on a track, its hand brakes must be applied. The car must not be left unattended on a grade where it could roll.

- **Attendance and monitoring.** Placarded cars in a switching yard must not be left on an open main track. They must be placed on a designated track with appropriate derails or other physical protections to prevent uncontrolled movement.

**Real-World Scenario**: A yard crew needs to add a tank car of anhydrous ammonia (Division 2.3, TIH) to an outbound train. The yard has a hump classification system, but the TIH car cannot go over the hump. Instead, the crew must pull the car with a yard locomotive, flat-switch it to the correct track, and couple it gently at no more than 4 mph. The crew member on the ground signals the engineer to slow down during the coupling move. This process takes more time than humping, but the alternative — a ruptured ammonia car in a populated rail yard — is not acceptable.

## Section 5: Inspection Requirements

Railroad tank cars carrying hazmat must be inspected at multiple points during their lifecycle and during each trip.

#

on 5: Inspection Requirements

Railroad tank cars carrying hazmat must be inspected at multiple points during their lifecycle and during each trip.

### Pre-Trip Inspection

Before a loaded tank car departs, it must be inspected for:

- **Leaks** — Any visible liquid dripping from valves, fittings, or the shell is a rejection criterion. The car must be repaired or the material transferred before the car moves.
- **Valve condition** — All valves (liquid, vapor, bottom outlet where applicable) must be properly closed and secured. Protective housings and manway covers must be in place.
- **Shell damage** — Dents, cracks, gouges, and signs of corrosion on the tank car shell must be evaluated. Certain types of damage (especially near welds or in the head area) are grounds for removing the car from service.
- **Pressure relief devices** — The safety valve or pressure relief device must be present, properly set, and not leaking. A missing or malfunctioning relief device means the car cannot transport pressurized materials.
- **Placards and markings** — The car must display the correct placards for the material being carried, and all required markings (reporting marks, stenciled tank specification, retest dates) must be legible.

### En Route and Arrival Inspection

When a train stops at an intermediate point (crew change, fuel stop, or interchange with another railroad), hazmat cars should be checked for leaks or signs of distress. At the destination, the car is inspected again befo

interchange with another railroad), hazmat cars should be checked for leaks or signs of distress. At the destination, the car is inspected again before unloading.

### Periodic Tank Car Qualification

Beyond trip-by-trip inspections, tank cars must undergo periodic testing and requalification under 49 CFR Part 180. This includes:

- **Visual inspection** of the tank shell, heads, fittings, and protective structures at prescribed intervals (typically every 5 or 10 years depending on the car's specification).
- **Thickness testing** of the shell using ultrasonic or other methods to ensure corrosion has not reduced the wall thickness below minimum specifications.
- **Pressure testing** (hydrostatic or pneumatic) to confirm the tank can withstand its rated pressure.
- **Valve and fitting testing** to ensure pressure relief devices are set correctly and all closures are tight.

A tank car that fails qualification testing must be repaired or retired from hazmat service.

## Section 6: Incident Response for Rail

When a rail hazmat incident occurs, it presents challenges that are fundamentally different from a highway incident.

### Scale

A single highway cargo tank holds roughly 5,000 to 9,000 gallons. A single railroad tank car holds 20,000 to 34,500 gallons. A unit train can involve 100+ cars. The sheer volume of material that can be released in a rail derailment is staggering. The East Palestine derailment involved approximately 100,000 gallons of vinyl chloride alone.

### Ac

be released in a rail derailment is staggering. The East Palestine derailment involved approximately 100,000 gallons of vinyl chloride alone.

### Access Difficulties

Derailments often occur in locations with limited road access. Unlike a highway spill where responders can drive right up to the scene, a rail derailment may be in a ravine, on a bridge, or in a remote area accessible only by rail or on foot. Heavy cranes, vacuum trucks, and response equipment may need to be moved in by rail or helicopter.

### Cascading Risks

When one car derails and ruptures, the resulting fire or explosion can damage adjacent cars. A pool fire under a pressurized tank car can heat the liquid inside, increasing the internal pressure until the car fails catastrophically in a **BLEVE (Boiling Liquid Expanding Vapor Explosion)**. A single BLEVE from an LPG car can throw fragments over a half-mile radius and produce a fireball 300 feet in diameter.

### The ERG and Isolation Zones

The Emergency Response Guidebook (ERG 2024) is critical for initial rail incident response. The orange pages provide material-specific response guidance. The green pages provide **initial isolation and protective action distances** for TIH materials. These distances tell first responders how far to evacuate or shelter-in-place around a release.

For a chlorine release from a rail car, the ERG recommends an initial isolation distance of 330 feet in all directions and a protective action distance of up to 2.9 miles dow

rom a rail car, the ERG recommends an initial isolation distance of 330 feet in all directions and a protective action distance of up to 2.9 miles downwind during a large nighttime spill. These numbers are not theoretical — the Graniteville chlorine release required evacuation of a one-mile radius and was lethal within 1,500 feet of the car.

### Railroad Hazmat Response Resources

The major Class I railroads (BNSF, Union Pacific, CSX, Norfolk Southern) maintain their own hazmat response teams, emergency management centers, and specialized equipment. They also participate in mutual aid agreements such as CHEMTREC and regional hazmat response cooperatives. However, first response typically falls to local fire departments, who may arrive on scene before railroad resources. This makes ERG training for first responders at rail crossings and rail-adjacent communities critically important.

**Real-World Scenario**: A 15-car section of a train carrying mixed hazmat derails at 2 AM near a small town. Three tank cars of ethanol (Class 3) are on fire. One car of chlorine (Division 2.3) is on its side but appears intact. Local fire fighters arrive first. Using the ERG, they identify the chlorine car by its placard and reporting marks, look up the initial isolation distance (1,000 feet for a large spill), and establish a perimeter. They do not attempt to fight the ethanol fire near the chlorine car — they evacuate the downwind area and wait for the railroad's hazmat team, who arrive four

not attempt to fight the ethanol fire near the chlorine car — they evacuate the downwind area and wait for the railroad's hazmat team, who arrive four hours later with specialized monitoring equipment and plugging kits. The initial ERG-based response by local firefighters likely saved lives by establishing the correct isolation distance before the railroad specialists arrived.

## Section 7: Relevance to Fleet Operators

If your fleet operates on highways, you may wonder why rail hazmat rules matter to you. The answer is that highway and rail operations intersect at multiple points, and understanding rail rules prevents costly mistakes.

### Transload Facilities

A transload facility is a location where material is transferred between rail cars and highway vehicles. Your tanker truck may pull up alongside a railroad tank car to load or unload product. At a transload:

- The material must be properly reclassified and documented for the new mode of transport if any differences apply.
- The highway shipping papers must be prepared correctly — the rail waybill is not a substitute for a highway bill of lading.
- The loading operation must comply with both Part 174 (rail) and Part 177 (highway) requirements. The tank car valves must be properly connected, the truck's ground strap must be attached (for flammable liquids), and both the car and the truck must be attended during the transfer.
- Quantity differences matter. A rail car holding 20,000 gallons of a flammable liquid may fee

car and the truck must be attended during the transfer.
- Quantity differences matter. A rail car holding 20,000 gallons of a flammable liquid may feed multiple truck loads. Each truck load needs its own shipping papers and placards.

### Intermodal Terminals

Container-on-flatcar (COFC) and trailer-on-flatcar (TOFC) operations involve moving highway trailers or containers by rail for part of the journey. If your fleet delivers a hazmat container to a rail terminal for intermodal transport, the container must meet both highway and rail standards. Placards must be applied to all four sides of the container (visible whether on a truck or a flatcar). The shipping papers must include the intermodal routing information.

### Rail Yard Pickups and Deliveries

Some fleet operations involve picking up or delivering freight directly at rail yards. If your driver enters a rail yard to pick up a container or deliver a load, the driver must follow the yard's safety rules, which include rail-specific hazmat handling procedures. Drivers should never walk between rail cars, should be aware of train movements, and should verify that any hazmat container they pick up has intact placards and documentation before leaving the yard.

**Real-World Scenario**: Your fleet sends a tanker truck to a transload facility to pick up 5,000 gallons of sodium hydroxide (Class 8, Corrosive) from a rail tank car. The driver arrives and connects the hose to begin loading. After loading, the driver needs to prep

ydroxide (Class 8, Corrosive) from a rail tank car. The driver arrives and connects the hose to begin loading. After loading, the driver needs to prepare a highway bill of lading listing the proper shipping name, hazard class, UN number, packing group, and quantity — the rail waybill that accompanied the tank car is not a valid highway shipping paper. The driver must also verify that CORROSIVE placards are displayed on all four sides of the tanker truck before departing. Failure to prepare highway-specific documentation at the transload point is a common violation that inspectors specifically look for.

---

## Key Takeaways
- Rail transports enormous quantities of hazmat — a single tank car holds 20,000-34,500 gallons
- Train placement rules require buffer cars between hazmat cars and locomotives, and between incompatible materials
- Speed restrictions (40-50 mph) apply to high-hazard flammable trains and trains carrying TIH materials
- Placarded cars must not be humped or kicked during switching — flat-switching at 4 mph or less is required
- Rail incidents can involve massive quantities and may require ERG-based isolation zones measured in miles
- Highway fleet operators interact with rail at transload facilities and intermodal terminals, where proper documentation and compliance handoffs are critical

# Carrier Requirements: Vessel

## Learning Objectives
- Describe the scope of hazmat transported by vessel and the role of the IMDG Code
- Explain stowage requirements including "on deck" vs "under deck" categories
- Apply vessel segregation rules for incompatible materials
- Identify documentation requirements for domestic and international marine hazmat shipments
- Recognize marine pollutant marking and reporting requirements
- Describe the U.S. Coast Guard's role in port and waterway hazmat safety
- Explain how vessel hazmat rules affect highway fleet operators who serve port facilities

## Section 1: Vessel Transport Overview

Marine vessels move the largest single quantities of hazardous materials of any transportation mode in the world. A single chemical tanker can carry 10,000 to 50,000 metric tons of product. A large container ship may carry 20,000 containers, hundreds of which may contain hazardous materials. Inland barges on the Mississippi River system routinely carry 10,000 to 30,000 barrels of petroleum products per barge, with barge tows consisting of 15 or more barges lashed together.

The materials that move by vessel include crude oil, refined petroleum products, liquefied natural gas (LNG)

ng of 15 or more barges lashed together.

The materials that move by vessel include crude oil, refined petroleum products, liquefied natural gas (LNG), industrial chemicals, agricultural chemicals, explosives, radioactive materials, and every other hazard class. International maritime trade is the backbone of the global chemical supply chain.

### The Regulatory Framework: Two Sets of Rules

Vessel transport of hazmat operates under two overlapping regulatory systems:

**U.S. Domestic Rules (49 CFR Part 176)**: PHMSA regulations that apply to domestic waterway movements within the United States. These rules govern stowage, segregation, and handling of hazmat on vessels operating in U.S. waters. For domestic movements, the standard U.S. hazmat shipping papers (bills of lading that comply with 49 CFR Part 172) are generally sufficient.

**International Maritime Dangerous Goods (IMDG) Code**: Published by the International Maritime Organization (IMO), the IMDG Code is the international standard for transporting hazardous materials by sea. It applies to all international marine shipments. The IMDG Code and the U.S. HMR are broadly aligned, but they are not identical. Where the shipment crosses international waters or involves a foreign port, the IMDG Code requirements apply in addition to U.S. domestic rules. The IMDG Code uses the same UN number and proper shipping name system as the HMR, so the classification framework is familiar, but specific packaging, stowage, and documenta

same UN number and proper shipping name system as the HMR, so the classification framework is familiar, but specific packaging, stowage, and documentation details may differ.

For fleet operators, the practical takeaway is this: if the cargo is moving domestically by barge or coastwise vessel, U.S. rules under Part 176 govern. If the cargo is heading overseas on a container ship, the shipper must also comply with the IMDG Code. Your job as the highway carrier is to deliver cargo that is properly classified, packaged, and documented so it meets the requirements for the vessel leg.

### Historical Context

Major maritime hazmat incidents have shaped current regulations:

**SS Grandcamp, Texas City (1947)**: A cargo ship loaded with ammonium nitrate fertilizer caught fire and exploded in the port of Texas City, Texas, killing nearly 600 people and destroying a significant portion of the city. This remains one of the deadliest industrial disasters in U.S. history and led to fundamental changes in how hazmat is stowed and handled in port.

**Exxon Valdez, Alaska (1989)**: A tanker ran aground in Prince William Sound, releasing 11 million gallons of crude oil. While primarily an environmental disaster rather than a hazmat transportation violation, this incident led to the Oil Pollution Act of 1990 and strengthened vessel safety and response planning requirements.

**MSC Flaminia, Atlantic Ocean (2012)**: A container ship suffered a fire and explosion caused by improperly declared h

sponse planning requirements.

**MSC Flaminia, Atlantic Ocean (2012)**: A container ship suffered a fire and explosion caused by improperly declared hazardous materials (divinylbenzene) in the cargo hold. Three crew members were killed. The vessel drifted in the Atlantic for weeks before a port would accept it. This case highlighted the danger of undeclared or misdeclared hazmat in container shipping.

## Section 2: Stowage Requirements (176.76-84)

Stowage is the maritime term for where and how cargo is physically placed on a vessel. For hazardous materials, stowage is not optional or left to the judgment of the dock crew — it is prescribed by regulation based on the hazard characteristics of the material.

### On Deck vs Under Deck

The two fundamental stowage locations on a vessel are:

**On Deck**: The cargo is placed on the weather deck (the top of the ship, exposed to the elements). On-deck stowage provides natural ventilation — vapors dissipate into the open air rather than accumulating in an enclosed hold. Crew can access on-deck cargo more easily for inspection and emergency response. However, on-deck cargo is exposed to weather, wave action, and temperature extremes.

**Under Deck**: The cargo is placed in an enclosed cargo hold below the weather deck. Under-deck stowage protects cargo from weather and is used for materials that must be kept dry or at controlled temperatures. However, under-deck cargo is in an enclosed space where leaking vapors can accumulate to da

rials that must be kept dry or at controlled temperatures. However, under-deck cargo is in an enclosed space where leaking vapors can accumulate to dangerous concentrations, and access for emergency response is more limited.

### Stowage Categories

The Hazardous Materials Table in 49 CFR 172.101 includes stowage information in **Column 10A**, which specifies the stowage category for each material. The IMDG Code uses a parallel system. The stowage categories include:

- **Category A**: May be stowed on deck or under deck. The material does not pose a significant ventilation concern.
- **Category B**: Must be stowed on deck only, or in a mechanically ventilated space under deck. This applies to materials whose vapors need to dissipate — such as certain flammable liquids and toxic gases.
- **Category C**: Must be stowed on deck only. No under-deck stowage is permitted, even with mechanical ventilation. This applies to materials like organic peroxides and certain oxidizers that need both ventilation and accessibility.
- **Category D**: Must be stowed on deck only and "protected from sources of heat." These materials are both vapor-hazardous and heat-sensitive.
- **Category E**: Must be stowed on deck or under deck, but "away from sources of heat." The material can go below deck but must not be near engine rooms, heated fuel tanks, or other heat sources.

### Why Stowage Matters for Highway Carriers

When you deliver a container of hazmat to a port, the stowage category determine

or other heat sources.

### Why Stowage Matters for Highway Carriers

When you deliver a container of hazmat to a port, the stowage category determines where the shipping line places that container on the vessel. If the cargo is Category C (on deck only), the container must be placed on the top tiers of the deck stack. The documentation you deliver with the cargo must accurately describe the material so the vessel operator can assign the correct stowage. Incorrect or incomplete documentation can cause the container to be stowed in the wrong location — a serious safety violation that puts the vessel, crew, and cargo at risk.

**Real-World Scenario**: Your fleet delivers a 20-foot container of organic peroxides (Division 5.2) to a container terminal. Organic peroxides are stowage Category D — on deck only, protected from sources of heat. The shipping documentation your driver provides at the gate must clearly identify the material with the correct proper shipping name, UN number, and hazard class so the terminal operator assigns the container to an on-deck slot away from the engine room stack and any containers generating heat. If the paperwork says "industrial chemicals" without specifying the hazard, the container might be stowed under deck — creating a serious fire and explosion risk.

## Section 3: Segregation on Vessels (176.83)

Segregation on a vessel follows the same general principle as highway segregation — incompatible materials must be separated so that a leak or fi

83)

Segregation on a vessel follows the same general principle as highway segregation — incompatible materials must be separated so that a leak or fire in one cannot trigger a reaction in another. But the vessel segregation system is more detailed than the highway system because of the physical structure of ships.

### The Four Levels of Vessel Segregation

The IMDG Code and 49 CFR 176.83 define four levels of segregation, from least to most restrictive:

**1. "Away from"**: The materials must be placed in different cargo spaces or holds, or at least 3 meters (10 feet) apart if in the same cargo space. This is the lowest level of separation — the materials should not be immediately adjacent but do not require structural barriers between them.

**2. "Separated from"**: The materials must be in different cargo compartments or holds. If on deck, they must be separated by a distance of at least 6 meters (20 feet) horizontally. A single bulkhead (wall between compartments) satisfies this requirement for under-deck stowage. This means the materials must have a real physical barrier or meaningful distance between them.

**3. "Separated by a complete compartment from"**: The materials must have at least one complete cargo compartment between them. If material A is in Hold 1 and material B is in Hold 3, with Hold 2 (containing neither A nor B) between them, this requirement is met. On deck, this translates to a separation of at least 12 meters (40 feet) horizontally. One shared bulkh

nor B) between them, this requirement is met. On deck, this translates to a separation of at least 12 meters (40 feet) horizontally. One shared bulkhead is not enough — there must be an intervening space.

**4. "Separated longitudinally by an intervening complete compartment from"**: This is the most restrictive level. The materials must be separated along the length of the ship by an entire intermediate cargo compartment. This level applies to the most dangerous combinations — materials that, if they came into contact, could produce catastrophic reactions such as explosion, toxic gas generation, or uncontrolled fire.

### How Segregation Is Determined

The IMDG Code includes a segregation table that cross-references hazard classes. For example:

- Class 1 (Explosives) must be "separated longitudinally by an intervening complete compartment from" Division 2.1 (Flammable Gas).
- Class 3 (Flammable Liquid) must be "away from" Class 5.1 (Oxidizer).
- Division 4.3 (Dangerous When Wet) must be "separated from" Class 8 (Corrosive).

The specific materials within each class may have additional segregation requirements listed in the IMDG Code supplement or in the individual material entries.

### Practical Impact

For a large container ship carrying thousands of containers, the segregation requirements are a three-dimensional puzzle. The vessel's cargo planner must account for stowage categories (on deck vs under deck), segregation distances (which classes cannot be in the same hold

he vessel's cargo planner must account for stowage categories (on deck vs under deck), segregation distances (which classes cannot be in the same hold), and physical ship constraints (hold sizes, ventilation capability, access routes). This is why accurate hazmat declarations are absolutely critical — the planner cannot solve the puzzle if they do not know what is in the containers.

**Real-World Scenario**: A container ship is loading at a U.S. port. Hold 2 already has containers of Class 5.1 oxidizers. A truck arrives at the terminal gate with a container of Division 4.1 flammable solids. The segregation table requires these be "separated from" — they must be in different holds or at least 6 meters apart on deck. The terminal operator must place the Division 4.1 container in a different hold or an on-deck position sufficiently distant from the Hold 2 stack. If the container's documentation does not accurately reflect its contents, the operator might place it in Hold 2 next to the oxidizers — a combination that could produce an intense, self-sustaining fire.

## Section 4: Documentation Requirements

Marine hazmat shipments require specific documentation that goes beyond the standard highway bill of lading.

### Dangerous Goods Declaration (Marine)

The Dangerous Goods Declaration is the marine equivalent of the Shipper's Declaration used in air transport. For international shipments governed by the IMDG Code, this document must include:

- The shipper's name and address
- T

ration used in air transport. For international shipments governed by the IMDG Code, this document must include:

- The shipper's name and address
- The consignee's name and address
- The proper shipping name, UN number, hazard class, packing group, and quantity
- The number and type of packages
- The flash point (for flammable liquids with a flash point of 60 degrees C / 140 degrees F or below)
- Marine pollutant designation (if applicable)
- EmS (Emergency Schedule) number — the IMDG equivalent of the ERG guide number
- A signed certification that the cargo is properly classified, packaged, marked, labeled, and in proper condition for carriage by sea

For domestic waterway shipments within the United States, a standard shipping paper complying with 49 CFR 172.200-204 is generally acceptable. However, if the cargo will be loaded onto a vessel that also carries international cargo, the vessel operator may require IMDG-format documentation for all hazmat on board to simplify their compliance tracking.

### Container/Vehicle Packing Certificate

When hazmat is loaded into a freight container or vehicle for transport by vessel, the packer must sign a **Container/Vehicle Packing Certificate**. This certificate states that:

- The container or vehicle was clean, dry, and fit for the cargo
- Incompatible materials were not packed together unless permitted by regulation
- All packages were inspected for damage and leaks before loading
- Packages were properly secured inside the cont

ther unless permitted by regulation
- All packages were inspected for damage and leaks before loading
- Packages were properly secured inside the container to prevent shifting
- The container is properly marked and placarded on the outside

This certificate may be combined with the Dangerous Goods Declaration or provided as a separate document. The vessel operator requires this certificate before accepting the container for loading.

### Bulk Liquid Cargo Documentation

For bulk liquid hazmat carried in tanker vessels or barges, additional documentation includes the cargo manifest (listing all tanks and their contents), compatibility certificates (confirming the material is compatible with the tank coatings and previous cargoes), and certificates of fitness for the vessel itself.

## Section 5: Marine Pollutants

Materials identified as **marine pollutants** in Appendix B to the Hazardous Materials Table (49 CFR 172.101) are subject to additional requirements when transported by vessel. A marine pollutant is a material that poses a significant risk to the marine environment if released into waterways.

### Identification

The Hazardous Materials Table identifies marine pollutants with a "G" designation in Column 1. You can also find the complete list in Appendix B to Section 172.101. Examples of common marine pollutants include certain pesticides, heavy metal compounds, petroleum distillates, and chlorinated solvents.

### Marking Requirements

Packages containing marine poll

certain pesticides, heavy metal compounds, petroleum distillates, and chlorinated solvents.

### Marking Requirements

Packages containing marine pollutants must be marked with the **marine pollutant mark** — a distinctive symbol showing a fish and tree inside a diamond (or triangle for single packagings). This mark must be in addition to any other marks and labels required for the material's hazard class. The mark alerts vessel crews and port workers that a spill of this material into the water requires immediate notification and response.

For bulk shipments, the marine pollutant mark must be displayed on the tank or container in addition to the standard placards.

### Reporting Requirements

If a marine pollutant is released into navigable waters of the United States, the responsible party must notify the **National Response Center (NRC)** at 1-800-424-8802. This reporting requirement exists under both DOT regulations and the Clean Water Act. The Coast Guard coordinates the response.

For vessel operators, any discharge of a marine pollutant — even a small amount from a leaking container on deck — triggers a reporting obligation. The threshold is lower than for most hazmat spill reporting because of the direct environmental impact on marine ecosystems.

**Real-World Scenario**: Your fleet delivers a flatbed load of drums containing a chlorinated solvent (Class 6.1, Toxic, also designated as a marine pollutant) to a container loading facility at a port. The shipper has appl

aining a chlorinated solvent (Class 6.1, Toxic, also designated as a marine pollutant) to a container loading facility at a port. The shipper has applied the Class 6.1 TOXIC labels on each drum, but the marine pollutant mark is missing. The loading facility spots this during their pre-loading inspection and refuses to accept the drums until the marks are applied. Your driver is stuck at the gate while the shipper sends someone out with the correct marks. This delay could have been prevented if the shipper had applied both the TOXIC labels and the marine pollutant marks at the origin — or if your driver had checked the drums against the shipping papers before departing.

## Section 6: Port and Waterway Safety

The **United States Coast Guard (USCG)** is the primary federal agency responsible for enforcing hazmat safety on vessels and at port facilities. While PHMSA writes the hazmat rules in 49 CFR Part 176, the Coast Guard enforces them on the water and at the dock.

### Captain of the Port (COTP)

Every major port area in the United States has a designated **Captain of the Port (COTP)** — a Coast Guard officer who has broad authority over vessel safety, port security, and hazmat operations within the port zone. The COTP can:

- Require vessels to stop, anchor, or divert if hazmat is improperly stowed or documented
- Restrict vessel movements during hazmat loading or unloading operations
- Order evacuations of port areas if a hazmat release occurs
- Impose penalties for viola

ssel movements during hazmat loading or unloading operations
- Order evacuations of port areas if a hazmat release occurs
- Impose penalties for violations of hazmat and port safety regulations
- Close a port to traffic during a hazmat emergency

### Vessel Response Plans

Vessels carrying oil or certain hazardous materials must maintain a **Vessel Response Plan (VRP)** that describes how the vessel operator will respond to a spill or release. The VRP must identify:

- Response resources (cleanup contractors, spill response equipment, trained personnel) that are pre-arranged and on call
- Notification procedures (Coast Guard, National Response Center, state agencies)
- Worst-case discharge scenarios and the planned response to each
- Communication protocols between the vessel, the response contractor, and government agencies

These plans must be approved by the Coast Guard and carried on board the vessel.

### Notice of Arrival

Vessels carrying hazmat must provide a **Notice of Arrival (NOA)** to the Coast Guard before entering a U.S. port. The NOA must include information about the vessel, crew, cargo (including all hazmat), and last port of call. For vessels arriving from foreign ports, the NOA must be submitted at least 96 hours before arrival. For domestic voyages, the timeline is shorter (typically 24 hours). The NOA allows the Coast Guard to assess risks, assign boarding priorities, and pre-position response resources if needed.

### Port Facility Security

Port facili

s the Coast Guard to assess risks, assign boarding priorities, and pre-position response resources if needed.

### Port Facility Security

Port facilities that handle hazmat are subject to the Maritime Transportation Security Act (MTSA) and must have approved facility security plans. These plans address access control, surveillance, cargo handling procedures, and response to security threats. Fleet drivers delivering hazmat to port facilities will encounter security checkpoints, identification requirements, and restricted access areas that are part of this regulatory framework.

**Real-World Scenario**: Your driver arrives at a container terminal to deliver a load of Class 3 flammable liquids destined for vessel transport. At the terminal gate, the driver must present a valid Transportation Worker Identification Credential (TWIC) card, the shipping papers for the hazmat cargo, and the container/vehicle packing certificate. The terminal security officer verifies the TWIC, inspects the documentation, and checks the container placards before granting entry. Without a TWIC, the driver cannot enter the secure area of the port — there is no workaround and no escort option at many facilities. If your fleet delivers to port facilities, every driver who enters the marine terminal must have a current TWIC card.

## Section 7: Relevance to Fleet Operators

Even if your fleet never loads a barge or sails on a ship, vessel hazmat rules matter to you if any of these situations apply:

###

Fleet Operators

Even if your fleet never loads a barge or sails on a ship, vessel hazmat rules matter to you if any of these situations apply:

### Delivering to Port Terminals

If your trucks deliver hazmat containers or cargo to a marine container terminal or bulk facility, you are the last land-based link before the cargo goes to sea. The documentation your driver delivers with the cargo must be accurate and complete. Errors in the proper shipping name, hazard class, or marine pollutant designation can cause:

- The container to be rejected at the terminal gate
- Incorrect stowage on the vessel (wrong hold, wrong deck position)
- Segregation violations (incompatible materials placed too close together)
- Regulatory violations that trace back to the shipper but affect the entire supply chain

### Picking Up from Port Terminals

When your fleet picks up a container from a marine terminal, the container may have been packed overseas and shipped by vessel. The markings, labels, and documentation should comply with both IMDG Code and U.S. HMR requirements. Your driver should verify:

- Placards are present on all four sides plus the top (if required)
- The shipping papers are present and legible
- The container shows no signs of leakage, damage, or shifting cargo (listen for sloshing, look for stains or residue around doors)
- Marine pollutant marks are present if required

### Understanding Stowage Categories for Documentation Accuracy

The stowage category for a material is

arine pollutant marks are present if required

### Understanding Stowage Categories for Documentation Accuracy

The stowage category for a material is determined by its hazard class and specific properties. As a highway carrier, you do not assign the stowage category — the vessel operator does that. But the vessel operator relies on the shipping documents you deliver. If the documents are wrong, the stowage assignment will be wrong. Getting the proper shipping name, hazard class, and packing group right on the highway shipping paper ensures the cargo is stowed correctly once it reaches the vessel.

### TWIC Card Requirements

Drivers who enter secure areas of marine port facilities must hold a valid **Transportation Worker Identification Credential (TWIC)** issued by TSA. The TWIC card requires a background check and takes several weeks to process. If your fleet regularly delivers to or picks up from ports, every driver assigned to port runs must have a current TWIC. An expired TWIC or a driver without one means the load cannot be delivered — the terminal will turn the truck away at the gate.

---

## Key Takeaways
- Vessels carry the largest quantities of hazmat of any mode, and the IMDG Code supplements U.S. regulations for international shipments
- Stowage rules (on deck vs under deck) are determined by the material's stowage category and directly affect safety
- Vessel segregation has four levels, from "away from" (3 meters) to "separated longitudinally by an intervening

category and directly affect safety
- Vessel segregation has four levels, from "away from" (3 meters) to "separated longitudinally by an intervening complete compartment from" (most restrictive)
- Marine pollutants require a specific mark (fish and tree symbol) and trigger reporting if released into waterways
- The Coast Guard enforces vessel hazmat rules through the Captain of the Port, vessel response plans, and notice of arrival requirements
- Highway fleet operators need accurate documentation and TWIC cards when delivering to or picking up from port facilities

## Assessment Questions

### Q1
**Type**: multiple_choice
**Question**: What regulatory code supplements U.S. domestic rules (49 CFR Part 176) for international shipments of hazardous materials by vessel?
**A**: IATA Dangerous Goods Regulations
**B**: International Maritime Dangerous Goods (IMDG) Code
**C**: ICAO Technical Instructions
**D**: NFPA 472 Hazmat Operations Standard
**Correct Answer**: B
**Explanation**: The International Maritime Dangerous Goods (IMDG) Code, published by the International Maritime Organization (IMO), is the international standard for transporting hazardous materials by sea. It supplements 49 CFR Part 176 for any shipment moving through international waters or involving a foreign port. IATA rules apply to air transport, and ICAO Technical Instructions also apply to air transport.
**CFR Reference**: 49 CFR 176.11

### Q2
**Type**: multiple_choice
**Question**: A material with stow

l Instructions also apply to air transport.
**CFR Reference**: 49 CFR 176.11

### Q2
**Type**: multiple_choice
**Question**: A material with stowage Category C must be stowed in which location on a vessel?
**A**: Under deck only, in a mechanically ventilated space
**B**: On deck or under deck, with no restrictions
**C**: On deck only — no under-deck stowage is permitted
**D**: Under deck only, protected from weather
**Correct Answer**: C
**Explanation**: Stowage Category C requires on-deck stowage only. No under-deck stowage is permitted, even with mechanical ventilation. This applies to materials like organic peroxides and certain oxidizers that require both natural ventilation and accessibility in an emergency.
**CFR Reference**: 49 CFR 176.76

### Q3
**Type**: multiple_choice
**Question**: Under the vessel segregation rules in 49 CFR 176.83, what does "separated by a complete compartment from" require?
**A**: Materials must be at least 3 meters apart in the same cargo hold
**B**: Materials must be in different compartments with a single shared bulkhead between them
**C**: Materials must have at least one complete intermediate cargo compartment between them, with no shared bulkheads
**D**: Materials must be on different vessels entirely
**Correct Answer**: C
**Explanation**: "Separated by a complete compartment from" means there must be at least one entire cargo compartment between the two materials. If material A is in Hold 1, there must be an intervening Hold 2

means there must be at least one entire cargo compartment between the two materials. If material A is in Hold 1, there must be an intervening Hold 2 (containing neither material) before material B in Hold 3. On deck, this translates to at least 12 meters horizontal separation. This is the third of four increasingly restrictive segregation levels.
**CFR Reference**: 49 CFR 176.83

### Q4
**Type**: multiple_choice
**Question**: What mark must be applied to packages containing materials designated as marine pollutants?
**A**: A blue diamond with a white anchor symbol
**B**: A diamond (or triangle) showing a fish and tree
**C**: A red circle with the letters "MP" in black
**D**: A green square with the word "MARINE" in white
**Correct Answer**: B
**Explanation**: Marine pollutant packages must display the marine pollutant mark — a distinctive symbol showing a fish and tree inside a diamond (or triangle for single packagings). This mark is required in addition to any other hazard class labels on the package. It alerts handlers that a release of this material into waterways requires immediate notification and response.
**CFR Reference**: 49 CFR 172.322

### Q5
**Type**: scenario
**Question**: Your driver arrives at a marine container terminal to deliver a container of hazmat for vessel transport. The terminal security officer asks for a TWIC card, shipping papers, and a container packing certificate. Your driver has the shipping papers but does not have a TWIC card. What

er asks for a TWIC card, shipping papers, and a container packing certificate. Your driver has the shipping papers but does not have a TWIC card. What happens?
**A**: The driver can enter the terminal if escorted by terminal security staff
**B**: The driver can present a CDL with hazmat endorsement as an alternative to the TWIC card
**C**: The driver will be denied entry to the secure area of the terminal — the delivery cannot be completed without a valid TWIC card
**D**: The driver can enter the terminal but only during daylight hours
**Correct Answer**: C
**Explanation**: Marine terminal secure areas require a Transportation Worker Identification Credential (TWIC) issued by TSA. A CDL with hazmat endorsement is not a substitute. Most marine terminals do not offer escort alternatives for drivers without TWIC cards. The delivery cannot be completed, resulting in a failed trip and delayed shipment. Fleet operators who serve port facilities must ensure all port-assigned drivers have current TWIC cards.
**CFR Reference**: 49 CFR 1572.5

# Security Requirements

## Learning Objectives
- Explain why security requirements were added to the Hazardous Materials Regulations
- Determine which operations require a written security plan under 49 CFR 172.800
- Describe the three required components of a hazmat security plan
- Distinguish between security awareness training and in-depth security training
- Apply practical security measures during hazmat transport
- Know when and how to report suspicious activity
- Maintain compliant security plan records

## Section 1: Why Security Matters for Hazmat

Before September 11, 2001, the Hazardous Materials Regulations were almost entirely focused on safety — preventing accidental releases, fires, and exposures during normal transportation operations. The attacks on that date demonstrated that hazardous materials in transportation could be deliberately weaponized, and the regulatory framework had a gap: it addressed accidents but not intentional acts.

The threat is real and specific. Consider what moves by highway every day:

- **Chlorine gas** (Division 2.3, Toxic Inhalation Hazard) — Used for water treatment, transported in bulk quantities. A deliberate release in a populated area could injure or kill thousands

Inhalation Hazard) — Used for water treatment, transported in bulk quantities. A deliberate release in a populated area could injure or kill thousands. The 2005 Graniteville, SC rail incident (an accident) killed 9 people and injured 554 from a single tank car. An intentional release of the same material in a subway tunnel, highway tunnel, or arena district would be far worse.

- **Anhydrous ammonia** (Division 2.2/2.3) — Used in agriculture and refrigeration. Highly toxic when inhaled, and transported in enormous quantities.

- **Gasoline and other flammable liquids** (Class 3) — Every fuel tanker on the highway is a rolling incendiary device if intentionally misused. A 9,000-gallon gasoline tanker detonated in a crowded area would cause mass casualties.

- **Explosives** (Division 1.1-1.6) — Commercial explosives for mining and construction move by highway daily. Theft or diversion of these materials is a direct national security concern.

- **Radioactive materials** (Class 7) — Medical and industrial radioactive sources are transported by highway. A stolen radioactive source used in a "dirty bomb" (radiological dispersal device) would cause widespread contamination and panic, even if the immediate casualty count were low.

The regulatory response was 49 CFR 172.800 through 172.804, which require security plans for certain hazmat operations and security training for all hazmat employees. These rules are not bureaucratic exercises — they are the government's attempt to close

mat operations and security training for all hazmat employees. These rules are not bureaucratic exercises — they are the government's attempt to close the gap between safety regulations and security threats.

### The Ongoing Threat

The threat has not diminished since 2001. In the years since, law enforcement has interdicted plots involving hazardous materials, and theft of chemicals from transport vehicles has occurred. The regulatory posture is that prevention is far more effective than response — it is better to prevent a chemical tanker from being hijacked than to respond to a mass casualty event after the fact.

## Section 2: Who Needs a Security Plan (172.800)

Not every hazmat operation requires a written security plan, but the threshold for needing one is lower than many operators realize. Under 49 CFR 172.800, a security plan is required for any person who **offers for transportation or transports** any of the following:

### Materials Requiring a Security Plan

1. **Any quantity of a highway route-controlled quantity of Class 7 (radioactive) material.** Highway route-controlled quantities are the highest activity radioactive shipments — they require specific routing and advance coordination.

2. **More than 25 kg (55 lbs) of a Division 1.1, 1.2, or 1.3 material.** These are the highest-hazard explosives: Division 1.1 presents a mass explosion hazard, Division 1.2 a projection hazard, and Division 1.3 a fire hazard. Even 55 pounds of these materials triggers the secu

resents a mass explosion hazard, Division 1.2 a projection hazard, and Division 1.3 a fire hazard. Even 55 pounds of these materials triggers the security plan requirement.

3. **Any quantity of a material that requires placarding of Division 2.1 (flammable gas) or Division 2.3 (toxic gas).** If your truck carries enough flammable gas or toxic gas to require a placard — and placarding thresholds are as low as any quantity for some materials — you need a security plan. This catches propane deliveries, chlorine shipments, anhydrous ammonia hauls, and many other common operations.

4. **A highway route-controlled quantity of Class 7 (radioactive) material.** (Restated for emphasis — any quantity of these high-activity shipments.)

5. **A select agent or toxin regulated by the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC) or the U.S. Department of Agriculture (USDA).** These are biological agents with potential for misuse, including certain pathogens and toxins regulated under 42 CFR Part 73.

6. **Any quantity of a material identified in the security plan section as a security-sensitive material.** The list of security-sensitive materials includes large bulk quantities of certain Classes 3 (flammable liquid) and 8 (corrosive) materials transported in quantities requiring placarding in bulk packaging, portable tanks, or cargo tanks.

### Who This Covers in Practice

If your fleet does any of the following, you almost certainly need a security plan:

- Delivers propane (Divisio

ks.

### Who This Covers in Practice

If your fleet does any of the following, you almost certainly need a security plan:

- Delivers propane (Division 2.1) in bulk — even a single bobtail propane truck triggers the requirement
- Hauls chlorine (Division 2.3) in any placarded quantity
- Transports gasoline, diesel, or other flammable liquids in cargo tanks (placarded quantities)
- Carries anhydrous ammonia for agricultural customers
- Moves commercial explosives for mining or construction customers
- Transports radioactive materials for medical, industrial, or waste disposal purposes

**Real-World Scenario**: A small fleet operates three bobtail propane trucks delivering to rural residential customers. The fleet manager assumes that because the operation is small and local, they do not need a security plan. This is wrong. Propane is Division 2.1 (flammable gas), the trucks carry placarded quantities, and 49 CFR 172.800(b)(4) requires a security plan for any quantity of placarded Division 2.1 material. The fleet must develop, implement, and maintain a security plan — and train all hazmat employees on it.

## Section 3: Security Plan Components (172.802)

A hazmat security plan is not a generic document downloaded from the internet and filed in a drawer. It must address three specific elements that are tailored to your actual operation. DOT inspectors will check not only that you have a plan, but that the plan reflects how your company actually handles hazmat.

### Component 1:

ation. DOT inspectors will check not only that you have a plan, but that the plan reflects how your company actually handles hazmat.

### Component 1: Personnel Security

The personnel security element of the plan must address how the company ensures that persons with access to hazardous materials are trustworthy and authorized.

**What this means in practice:**

- **Background checks**: The plan must describe the procedures for verifying the background of employees who have access to hazmat. For CDL holders with a hazmat endorsement, TSA already conducts a security threat assessment (STA) as part of the endorsement process. But your plan should address how you verify that the STA is current and what additional checks you perform for non-CDL employees who handle hazmat (warehouse workers, shipping clerks, dock loaders).

- **Employment verification**: Confirm previous employment history and check references. A person who claims to have worked at a specific chemical company for five years should have that claim verified.

- **Identification protocols**: How do you verify that the person who shows up to pick up a load of hazmat is actually the person authorized to do so? This includes driver identification verification at pickup and delivery points.

- **Addressing unauthorized access by personnel**: What happens when an employee is terminated, resigns, or has their hazmat endorsement revoked? The plan must describe how you revoke access to hazmat, vehicles, and facilities. Thi

is terminated, resigns, or has their hazmat endorsement revoked? The plan must describe how you revoke access to hazmat, vehicles, and facilities. This includes recovering keys, badges, access cards, and any electronic access credentials.

**Real-World Scenario**: A driver with a hazmat endorsement is terminated for cause. Your security plan should require that the company immediately recover the truck keys, fuel cards, and facility access badges. The plan should also require notification to the dispatcher and terminal security that the former driver is no longer authorized to enter the premises or access any hazmat vehicle or cargo. A terminated employee who retains a key to a propane truck or a gate code to the yard is a security gap.

### Component 2: Unauthorized Access Prevention

This element covers physical security measures to prevent unauthorized persons from gaining access to hazmat, vehicles, and facilities.

**What this means in practice:**

- **Vehicle security**: Trucks carrying hazmat must be locked when unattended. Cargo compartments, valves, and tank openings must be secured. King pin locks, glad-hand locks, and air brake valve locks can prevent unauthorized persons from moving the vehicle. Engine kill switches or GPS-based anti-theft systems add additional layers.

- **Facility security**: The terminal, warehouse, or yard where hazmat is stored or handled must have controlled access. This means fencing, locked gates, lighting, and surveillance cameras at a m

se, or yard where hazmat is stored or handled must have controlled access. This means fencing, locked gates, lighting, and surveillance cameras at a minimum. The plan should describe who has access to the facility after hours and how that access is controlled.

- **Cargo security during loading and unloading**: Hazmat cargo should not be left unattended on a dock or in a yard where unauthorized persons can access it. If your operation involves leaving loaded hazmat trailers in a drop yard overnight, the plan must address how those trailers are secured — locked, within a fenced area, and monitored.

- **Lock protocols**: The plan should specify what gets locked, when, and by whom. The minimum standard: every time a driver leaves the cab, the truck is locked. Every time a loaded trailer is dropped, the trailer doors are locked and sealed. Valve caps on cargo tanks are locked when not actively loading or unloading.

**Real-World Scenario**: A fleet parks six loaded hazmat trailers in an unfenced lot adjacent to the warehouse over the weekend. The lot is on a public street, there are no security cameras, and the trailer doors have no locks — just standard latches. On Monday morning, a driver discovers that one trailer's rear doors are ajar and several drums are missing. This is a reportable security incident and a violation of the security plan requirements. The missing drums may contain hazardous materials that are now in the hands of an unknown party. A proper security plan wou

ecurity plan requirements. The missing drums may contain hazardous materials that are now in the hands of an unknown party. A proper security plan would have required the trailers to be parked inside the fenced yard, with door locks applied and camera surveillance recording.

### Component 3: En Route Security

This element addresses security risks that arise while hazmat is in transit — on the road between origin and destination.

**What this means in practice:**

- **Route adherence**: Drivers should follow designated routes and not deviate without authorization and a legitimate reason. Some hazmat routes are federally mandated (such as highway-route-controlled quantities of radioactive materials), but your plan should establish standard routes for your most common hazmat shipments and require drivers to notify dispatch of any deviations.

- **Communication protocols**: Drivers must be able to contact dispatch at all times during a hazmat trip. The plan should specify how frequently the driver must check in (e.g., at each stop, every 2 hours, upon arrival at destination) and what information is communicated (current location, ETA, any unusual observations).

- **Rest stop and fueling procedures**: The plan should address where drivers are allowed to stop and what precautions they must take. Drivers should fuel at well-lit, populated truck stops rather than remote, unattended fuel points. When stopped for rest, the truck must be locked, and the driver should park in a visibl

ulated truck stops rather than remote, unattended fuel points. When stopped for rest, the truck must be locked, and the driver should park in a visible, well-lit area.

- **Delivery verification**: Before unloading hazmat at a delivery point, the driver should verify that the receiving party is legitimate. Does the delivery address match the shipping papers? Is the contact person who signed for the load actually an employee of the consignee? For sensitive materials, the plan may require advance confirmation of the delivery appointment and identification verification of the receiver.

- **Deviation and incident reporting**: If a driver observes anything unusual — a vehicle following the truck, someone attempting to flag down the truck on a remote road, a suspicious package left on the truck during a stop — the plan must specify who to call and what information to report.

**Real-World Scenario**: A driver hauling a tanker of anhydrous ammonia stops at a rest area on an interstate at 2 AM. While walking back from the restroom, the driver notices a person examining the tanker's valves and fittings. The driver returns to the cab, locks the doors, and calls dispatch, who calls 911. The police arrive and find that the person was simply curious, not malicious. This is still the correct response. The security plan should train drivers to never confront suspicious persons themselves, always call for help, and document the incident. An overreaction costs nothing; an underreaction could

o never confront suspicious persons themselves, always call for help, and document the incident. An overreaction costs nothing; an underreaction could cost lives.

## Section 4: Security Awareness Training (172.704(a)(4))

Security awareness training is required for **every hazmat employee** — regardless of whether their employer is required to have a security plan. This is a critical distinction. Even if your company handles hazmat quantities below the security plan threshold, every hazmat employee must still receive security awareness training.

### What Security Awareness Training Covers

Security awareness training must provide employees with an understanding of:

1. **Security risks associated with hazmat transportation.** Employees must understand that the materials they handle could be targeted for theft, sabotage, or misuse. This is not about creating paranoia — it is about creating awareness that hazmat has a dual-use potential.

2. **Methods designed to enhance transportation security.** Practical measures that employees can take in their daily work to improve security: locking trucks, securing cargo, following routes, verifying deliveries, and controlling access.

3. **How to recognize and respond to possible security threats.** What does suspicious activity look like in the context of hazmat transportation? It might be a person asking unusual questions about a load's contents, timing, or route. It might be an unauthorized person in a restricted area. It might be a

be a person asking unusual questions about a load's contents, timing, or route. It might be an unauthorized person in a restricted area. It might be a vehicle following a hazmat truck for an unusually long distance. Employees must know what to look for and what to do about it.

### When Security Awareness Training Is Required

- At initial hire (within the 90-day training window for new hazmat employees)
- At least every 3 years as part of recurrent training
- Whenever the employer's security posture changes or new threats emerge

### Documentation

Security awareness training must be documented in the employee's training record, just like general awareness, function-specific, and safety training. The record must include the date of training, a description of the training content, and a certification that the employee was trained and tested.

## Section 5: In-Depth Security Training (172.704(a)(5))

In-depth security training goes beyond awareness and is required only for employees of companies that must have a security plan under 172.800. If your company needs a security plan, your hazmat employees need in-depth security training — in addition to security awareness training.

### What In-Depth Security Training Covers

In-depth security training must cover:

1. **The company's specific security plan.** Employees must know what the plan says, where it is located, and how it applies to their particular job. A driver's in-depth training focuses on en route security, vehicle loc

the plan says, where it is located, and how it applies to their particular job. A driver's in-depth training focuses on en route security, vehicle locking protocols, communication check-ins, and delivery verification. A warehouse worker's training focuses on facility access control, cargo security during handling, and recognizing unauthorized persons in the area.

2. **The employee's specific role in implementing the plan.** Generic training is not enough. Each employee must understand their individual responsibilities under the plan. The driver's role is different from the dispatcher's role, which is different from the terminal manager's role. Training must be specific to each function.

3. **Company-specific procedures.** How does your company handle a security breach? Who is the designated security coordinator? What is the phone number to call? What information must be provided when reporting an incident? These are company-specific details that must be part of in-depth training.

### The Difference Between Awareness and In-Depth Training

| Aspect | Security Awareness | In-Depth Security |
|--------|-------------------|-------------------|
| Who needs it | ALL hazmat employees | Only employees of companies with a required security plan |
| Content | General awareness of threats, methods, and reporting | Company-specific plan, individual roles, detailed procedures |
| Depth | Overview level | Detailed, role-specific |
| Triggers | Any hazmat employee function | Company meet

individual roles, detailed procedures |
| Depth | Overview level | Detailed, role-specific |
| Triggers | Any hazmat employee function | Company meets the threshold in 172.800 |

**Real-World Scenario**: A fleet operates 20 fuel tankers delivering gasoline and diesel (Class 3, flammable liquid, placarded quantities in cargo tanks). The fleet is required to have a security plan under 172.800. The fleet's drivers receive security awareness training (general threats, general measures, general reporting) plus in-depth security training (the company's specific security plan, the driver's specific responsibilities under that plan, the company's specific communication protocols and incident reporting procedures). A new dispatcher who also prepares hazmat shipping papers receives the same security awareness training but different in-depth training — focused on verifying delivery appointments, monitoring driver check-ins, and coordinating with law enforcement if a driver reports a security concern.

## Section 6: Practical Security Measures for Drivers

The security plan creates the framework. The driver implements it on the road every day. Here are the practical security measures that every hazmat driver should follow:

### Vehicle Security

- **Lock your truck every time you leave the cab.** This is the single most important security measure. It does not matter if you are going inside a gas station for five minutes or walking 50 feet to check a tire. Lock the cab. A hazmat vehicle

measure. It does not matter if you are going inside a gas station for five minutes or walking 50 feet to check a tire. Lock the cab. A hazmat vehicle left running and unlocked is an invitation for theft or hijacking.

- **Do not leave the keys in the ignition.** This seems obvious, but it happens — especially during loading and unloading when the driver steps away "for just a second." Remove the keys and take them with you.

- **Use all available anti-theft devices.** King pin locks on trailers, glad-hand locks on air brake connections, steering wheel locks, engine kill switches, GPS tracking with geofencing — use what your company provides.

- **Park in well-lit, visible locations.** When stopping for fuel, food, or rest, choose locations with lighting, cameras, and other vehicles. Avoid isolated rest areas, dead-end industrial roads, and unlit parking areas.

### Cargo Security

- **Seal all cargo openings.** Trailer doors, tank hatches, valve covers, and any other access points to the cargo must be closed and secured when not actively loading or unloading. Use numbered seals where required and record the seal numbers on your shipping papers.

- **Do not discuss your cargo with strangers.** If someone at a truck stop asks what you are hauling, you are not obligated to tell them. A polite "just freight" or no answer at all is appropriate. The proper shipping name, hazard class, and quantity of your cargo is information that belongs on your shipping papers and in your dispatc

ppropriate. The proper shipping name, hazard class, and quantity of your cargo is information that belongs on your shipping papers and in your dispatch system — not in a casual conversation.

- **Verify pickup and delivery contacts.** Before accepting a load, confirm the pickup appointment with your dispatch. Before delivering, confirm the delivery appointment and verify the identity of the person signing for the cargo. If something feels wrong — the address does not match, the contact person is not who was expected, the facility looks abandoned — call dispatch before unloading.

### Route Discipline

- **Follow designated routes.** If your company has established routes for hazmat shipments, follow them. Deviations should only occur for legitimate reasons (road closures, accidents, weather) and should be communicated to dispatch immediately.

- **Monitor your surroundings.** Be aware of vehicles that appear to be following you, especially on less-traveled roads. If you suspect you are being followed, do not stop in an isolated area — continue to a well-populated location (truck stop, police station, fire station) and call 911.

- **Comply with hazmat routing restrictions.** Certain materials are prohibited from specific routes (tunnels, bridges, densely populated areas). Follow posted hazmat route signs and any route restrictions in your company's security plan.

## Section 7: Reporting Suspicious Activity

The security framework only works if people on the front lines — dri
